custody of the City Accounting Office and the GSO, including the maintenance
of Real Property Ledger Cards for land
for a valid reporting and presentation of PPE accounts balances that are verifiable,
accurate and in existence, thus, attain fairness of presentation of all PPE accounts
in the Financial Statements of the City at every reporting period.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC-Accountant to make appropriate adjustments on the Other Payables
account by reclassifying into Accounts Payable the claims against services
rendered or goods delivered, duly supported with appropriate documents that
establish the obligations incurred by the City Government. Otherwise, consider
dropping from the books those recorded obligations at year-end, where validity
is suspect in view of the absence of valid document to support such obligations;
and
b. OIC-Accountant to ensure that obligations at year-end arising from the receipt
of goods and services, particularly infrastructure projects, are properly
classified under the Accounts Payable account duly supported with valid
documents, hence, attain fair presentation of accounts in the financial
statements at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City GSO to:
a. Prepare and maintain the SSMI, on a weekly basis, duly supported with RIS and
furnish a copy to the OIC- City Accountant; and
b. Conduct the physical count of inventories on a semi-annual basis and
incorporate the results thereof in the RPCI, and submit the same to the OIC-City
Accountant to cause immediate reconciliation of records.

We reiterated that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City Accountant to:
a. Comply strictly with reconciliation of the Actual Amounts reported in the
Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts and the Statement of
Financial Performance so as to clarify or settle the discrepancies between the
two reports, and submit the same to the Audit Team within 15 days from receipt
hereof; and
b. For the succeeding year, prepare the Notes to Financial Statements using the
prescribed Pro-forma NFS. Ensure that each item on the face of the statement
of financial position, statement of financial performance, statement of changes
in net assets/equity and cash flow statement shall be cross-referenced to any
related information in the notes, thus address any deficiency and present
reliable and accurate information to the public.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC-City Accountant to prepare and submit the CAF for the 43 listed projects,
totaling P147,344,523.99, as enumerated in Annex I, to the Audit Team for
verification;
b. OIC-City Accountant, upon ensuring that sufficient funds are available, prepare
and issue the CAF as support documents for every procurement of
infrastructure projects to ensure propriety of such undertaking;
c. BAC Chairman to strictly ensure that all contract agreements are accompanied
by a valid and acceptable form of Performance Security as prescribed under the
RIRR of RA No. 9184;
d. BAC Chairman to address the seeming lack of manpower in the TWG and the
BAC Secretariat to assist with the submission of contracts along with their
supporting documents to ensure timely and complete submission, so that all its
functions would not suffer from non-compliance with their duties and
responsibilities; and
e. OIC-City Accountant to check the completeness of supporting documents
before processing payments in order to establish the legality, validity, and
propriety of the disbursement.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City Accountant to:
a. Submit the noted missing documentary requirements per COA Circular No.
2012-001;
b. Submit to the Audit Team the supporting documents relative to the
discrepancies noted in the Deed of Sale of one parcel of land involving different
square meters as against the payments made to clear doubts as to the regularity
of the lot purchased. Otherwise, Notice of Suspension/Disallowance may ensue
in view of the overpayment made to a 2,022 square meter lot; and
c. Comply strictly with all the required documents in the procurement of lot, ensure
that all required documents are complete and properly attached to the
Disbursement Vouchers before processing payments to avoid delays and
ensure compliance with the relevant regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Require the members of the City Planning and Development Council to assess
strictly the viability of the identified Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) at
the planning stage to ensure that only those projects that are procurement-and-
implementation-ready would be given budget allocation for the optimal and
prompt utilization of 20% DF within the set timelines of the projects’
implementation; and
b. Instruct the City Engineer’s Office, with the assistance of the City Budget Office,
to evaluate and assess whether its current manpower is sufficient to handle the
growing number of infrastructure projects and find ways to address the
perceived shortage of personnel complement dealing with the engineering
aspects of each and every project of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City GSO to comply strictly with the pertinent documents in the disposal of PPE,
pursuant to Section C.1 of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1, and submit the same
to the Appraisal and Disposal Committee for the necessary actions deemed
appropriate, thus hastening the process of disposal;
b. Appraisal and Disposal Committee, upon the receipt of reports from the City
GSO, to prepare the updated IIRUP and determine the appraised value of
unserviceable PPE items to proceed with the disposal of the same;
c. OIC-City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the
carrying amount of PPE retired from active use and held for disposal;
d. Disposal Committee to ensure that all unserviceable properties for disposal are
accounted for and pertinent documents are completed to proceed with the disposal
of properties; and
e. OIC-City Accountant, upon disposal of the unserviceable PPE, to record the
dropping of the properties from the books of accounts and, in case of sale, to
recognize the proceeds accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. Ensure the timely submission of the GPB, GAD AR, and other required GAD-
related reports to the DILG for the effective and efficient implementation of the
identified GAD activities, and the judicious utilization of the GAD budget in
accordance with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01;
b. Plan thoroughly the activities of the City in attaining women’s empowerment and
addressing gender equality, focusing more on priority projects while taking into
consideration the limited resources of the City with respect to funds, time, and
manpower;
c. Prioritize coordinating with the technical working group to gather the necessary
sex-disaggregated data and conduct HGDG attribution for all GAD-related
activities to ensure their gender responsiveness; and
d. Review the GPB thoroughly to ensure its reconciliation with the Annual Budget
and submit any changes therein to the DILG-Regional Office to strictly monitor
the actual expenditures and ensure that the City has enough funds for the
implementation of all its GAD activities.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City GSO to prioritize the preparation, maintenance, and reconciliation of SPC,
RegSPI, RSPI and other related documents, as mandated by COA Circular No.
2022-004, to ensure transparency, accountability, and proper asset
management;
b. OIC-City Accountant to prepare the required SPLC and record promptly the
acquisition, description, custody, impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair
history, and other information about the property, for easy reference; and
c. City GSO to review the semi-expendable properties procured prior to CY 2024
to determine whether the existing AREs should be replaced with ICS or
automatically be canceled for low-valued semi-expendable properties with
expired estimated useful life, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2024-006.

We recommend that the City Mayor: a. Direct the City Accountant to record the transfer of 101 completed projects with an aggregate contract cost of P704,030,134.50 and carrying value of P378,972,917.44; and b. Ensure necessary coordination with DPWH on the implementation of government projects, particularly those that are intended to be subsequently transferred to the LGU, to ascertain timely and complete recording of assets in the financial statements.

Therefore, we recommend that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Formulate a policy in the measurement of financial assets, particularly in the provision of allowance for credit loss or impairment loss; the recognition of doubtful allowance for credit loss or impairment losses. b. Apply the formulated policy in analyzing the balance of receivable accounts and provide the necessary impairment accordingly; and c. Consider to undertake request for write-off of qualified dormant receivable accounts in accordance with relevant COA Circulars.

We recommend that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to make the necessary journal entry to correct the noted misclassification of funds to appropriate accounts and ensure that income accounts in the succeeding receipt of the General Fund.

We recommend that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to: a. Adjust the accumulated depreciation and the accumulated surplus by P9,387,614.88; and b. Commence the recognition of depreciation at the date of its completion, and not at the date of final payment.
We recommend that the City Mayor direct the City Budget Officer to record obligations based on perfected contracts rather than on mere purchase requests.

Therefore, we recommend that the City Mayor direct the City Personnel Office to: a. Include in the review and update of the City's organizational structure and staffing pattern the necessity to engage and hire the latter accordingly; and the City's organizational structure and staffing pattern in coordination with the City Personnel Office. b. Include in the contract for job-order personnel clear and specific statement of their duties and responsibilities aligned with the work program of the Office they will be assigned to.

We recommend that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Review the validity of outstanding Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable which are outstanding for over one year to assess their validity. b. Notify the contractors and private entities concerned to file their claims for the immediate release of the retention money to avoid the accumulation of the account balances. Otherwise, require them to execute a waiver of their right to claim such deposits for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

Foregoing considered, we recommend that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to: a. Conduct confirmation among identified source agencies to confirm the balances due to them and facilitate the return of the remaining unutilized/unused fund. b. Ensure that all subsequent unused balance of funds received are returned immediately after the completion of the related program or project pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor strictly require the Gender and Project Development Focal Point System (GFPS) Technical Working Group (TWG) to provide sufficient MOVs for the HGDG checklist of the attributed programs or projects presented in the GPB in CY 2024 and subsequent years.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CGSO, City Accountant and the members of the Inventory Committee to follow the procedural guidelines on the one-time cleansing of PPE prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 such as: (1) Preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking; (2) Inventory taking; (3) Reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records; (4) Disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs; (5) Procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability; and (6) Accounting entries and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements thereafter.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. City General Services Officer to prepare the PIF by extracting the data from the RPCPPE and submit to Audit Team and GIF of the GSIS which will be used as a basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the City’s insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; b. City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all the insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; and c. City General Services Officer to insure with the GIF of the GSIS all of the City’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) to provide sufficient MOVs for the HGDG checklist of the attributed programs or projects presented in the GPB in CY 2024 and subsequent years.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the remaining unsubmitted paid DVs and their supporting documents to the Auditor for verification and audit as required in Section 347 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1.a of COA Circular No. 2009-006 to avoid the issuance of notices of suspension and/or disallowance in audit on the subjects transactions. Otherwise, cause the suspension of payment of the salaries of the officials concerned relative to the unsubmitted DVs, pursuant to Section 122(2) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Disposal Committee to submit the
IIRUP of the 23 derecognized unserviceable properties to the Audit Team and
comply with the procedure stated under COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 or the
Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties which provides the
necessary requirements that government agencies must adhere to prior to
commencement of disposal activities.
Furthermore, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Inventory
Committee, the City Accountant and the General Services Office (GSO) to conduct
the one-time cleansing of accounts following the guidelines and procedures under
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to address the
following:
a. The accounting office’ preparation of PPE ledger cards and prompt
reconciliation of the results of the physical count with the accountabilities
recorded in the books of accounts and the property cards; and
b. Drawing of an adjusting entry to record the return of derecognized non-
existing and unserviceable PPEs with net book values of P161,609.49 and
P2,195,716.53 respectively, and the boom truck costing P4,933,000.00 which
was transferred to the books of accounts of Barangay Darasa.
We further recommended the submission to the Audit Team of the proposed
timeline for immediate completion of the one-time cleansing of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to make the
necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the noted misclassification of
accounts and ensure that any succeeding transactions of the same kind are
appropriately recorded in their corresponding accounts. Further, we
recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to review the
validity of the unpaid obligations which are outstanding for more than two years
and if found to have no valid claims against the City, request for the reversion of
the same to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to immediately
enforce the submission of the required documents to support the validity of the
recorded Accounts Payable with noted lacking documentation cited in the
Annexes. If found not valid as claim, effect the reversal of such in the books and
submit the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to the Audit Team with
details of the reversed transactions.
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the City Mayor direct
the City Budget Officer to refrain from approving obligation requests unless
otherwise, a contract has already been perfected and for the General Services
Officer to strictly ensure that contracts are submitted for obligation requests,
while delivery receipts, sales invoices and Inspection and Acceptance Reports
(IARs) along with the procurement documents are submitted in support of
recording claims by the City Accountant.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CSWDO to:
a. Submit the complete set of distribution lists duly signed and acknowledged by
recipients to account for the welfare goods transactions detailed herein;
b. Prepare and maintain a monitoring of the goods being received by its office
and update such record for every distribution;
c. Furnish the City Accounting Office with the details of the issued inventories, if
any, on a weekly basis;
d. Conduct periodic inventory of the goods in accordance with Section 124 of the
MNGAS for LGUs; and
e. Forward such to the Audit Team for verification and to the City Accounting
Office to prepare for the necessary accounting entries to recognize the cost of
the undistributed welfare goods, if any.
Henceforth, we further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City
Accounting Office to record the succeeding procurements of inventories to its
appropriate account following IPSAS 12 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 and
coordinate with CSWDO to recognize in the books the full cost of the inventory
of goods as at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to prepare and
furnish the City Accounting Office with the schedule of unreleased checks as of the
end of year and the City Accountant to prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to
restore all unreleased checks to the cash account and recognize the
corresponding liability/payable account to fairly present the accounts in the
financial statement.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to compute for
the withholding taxes on the payables amounting to P56,306,315.83 and make the
necessary adjusting entries in the books. Henceforth, recognize in the books the
taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of Accounts Payable (AP) for
goods received or services rendered, inspected and accepted, and for
construction/infrastructure projects partially or fully completed and inspected
pursuant BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. The CDRRMO to refrain from charging against the LDRRMF disbursements of
such kind and plan and utilize the fund only for programs and projects which
are supportive of disaster risk reduction and management activities to avoid
audit disallowance and
b. The CDRRMO and GSO to submit the copies of the MOA, if any, with the 42
end-user Barangays, supporting the agreement and conditions on the intended
use and incurrence of subsequent expenses pertaining to the emergency
response vehicles. Henceforth, ensure that any succeeding procurement of
vehicle align with the guidelines provided by DBM Budget Circular No. 2022-1
and with the classification, description and specifications of vehicles
therein prescribed.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Administrator to formulate a
well-defined policy/guidelines, include therein documentary requirements, criteria
for the selection of qualified applicants, desired amount and the like, for the grants
and financial assistance to ensure determination of qualified
individuals/entities, equitable allocation of assistance and economical use of City
funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and City
Treasury to:
a. submit immediately the remaining six disbursement vouchers including the
supporting documents to the Audit Team to facilitate post-audit, to avoid the
issuance of Notice of Suspension pursuant to the 2009 RRSA prescribed under
COA Circular 2009-006. We also recommended strict compliance with the
provisions of Sections 39 and 100 of PD No. 1445 and Section 7.2.1(a) of COA
Circular No. 2009-006 on prompt submission of accounts, otherwise, enforce
the provision of Section 122 of PD No. 1445 against concerned officials; and
b. ensure the completeness of the necessary supporting documents of the
transactions before processing the payment.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City HRMO to submit legal
basis or support on the validity of the designation of the three coterminous
personnel as OICs.
Further, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the City HRMO to strictly
adhere to the provisions of the 2017 ORAOHRA on the appointment, assignment
and movement of City personnel.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Human Resource and
Management Officer (HRMO), in coordination with concerned City Budget Office to
consider the update of daily wage rate of Job Order workers of the City taking into
consideration the daily minimum wage rate in the private sector, and if funds are
available, daily rate of comparable position in the government sector.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRMO to closely monitor
the implementation of the LDRRM projects and for the LDRRMC to evaluate the
feasibility of the LDRRMP, particularly projects with continuing appropriations to
identify those which due to valid reasons can no longer be implemented, hence,
for reversion and reprogramming to other more urgent disaster projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Development Council to assess the feasibility of the identified PPAs in the
planning stage by conducting proper assessment and evaluation of the priority
projects to prevent or at least minimize series of changes of the plan to ensure
that only those projects, which are procurement-and-implementation-ready
would be given budget allocation;
b. City Engineering Office, in coordination with the barangays and other
concerned officials, to perform in-depth planning and detailed engineering,
including thorough site inspections to anticipate and consider interruptions
that may probably arise to ensure optimal and timely implementation of the
projects to be funded by the 20 percent Development Fund; and
c. City Budget Office, in coordination with the City Planning and Development
Office, to consider the unexpended balances of the completed projects for
reversion to unappropriated surplus for possible allocation to other
developmental projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City General Services Office to
strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 on the prompt
submission of copies of perfected Contracts and Purchase Orders, with complete
details, brand names and specifications of items within the reglementary period of
five working days after execution to facilitate timely review/evaluation by the Audit
Team and communicate to Management deficiencies noted therein. Likewise,
submit the notice of delivery in accordance with COA Circular No. 95-006 to
facilitate timely inspection of goods delivered and to verify whether such conform
to the specifications in the PO.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to initiate the
request for write-off of the long outstanding balances complete with the
documentary requirements and following the guidelines and procedures
prescribed under the aforementioned COA Circular, to facilitate the submission to
the Audit Team of the request for authority to write-off dormant receivables
amounting to P296,308.21.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System, GAD
TWG and the Gender and Development Section Head to conduct thorough
planning and analysis, taking into consideration the feasibility and propriety of the
proposed PPA to ensure that those identified PPAs to be included in the GAD Plan
and Budget are those that can be implemented and completed within the target
period so that the envisioned GAD objectives and results will be fully achieved.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the members of the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive
physical count and inspection of all PPE items to fully verify their existence, and
accurately incorporate the results into the RPCPPE, ensuring that only those PPE
duly accounted for during the inventory count are included in the report;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accounting Office and the MGSO to address any deficiencies that
may arise out of the conduct of complete inventory-taking of all PPE items owned by the
Municipality, and to update and maintain the required Property Cards/
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment in their custody, for easy reference
during the conduct of inventory-taking; and
c. Adopt and fully implement the one-time cleansing procedures of PPE accounts
incorporated into the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to properly
address the missing/unaccounted/undocumented PPE items that have been in the
books for several years, so as to arrive at a valid reporting and presentation of PPE
accounts balances that are verifiable, accurate and in existence, thus, attain
fairness of presentation of all PPE accounts in the Financial Statements of the
Municipality at every reporting period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
ascertain with finality of the existence or absence of documents supporting these
Other Receivable accounts, outstanding for more than 20 years, and if found futile
adopt the procedures for the proper disposition of dormant accounts pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2023-008 to achieve a fairly presented Receivable accounts in the financial
statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct confirmation among
identified source agencies of the unexpended and unremitted balances and
facilitate its return. Otherwise, if there is still a need, the Municipality may make a
written representation with the source agencies to request the use of money for
other projects or purpose; and
b. Municipal Accountant ensure that all subsequent unused balances of funds
received shall be returned immediately after the completion of the project pursuant
to COA Circular No. 94-013 so as to rid the books of long outstanding obligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board to cause the
immediate reversion of savings from completed projects into an unappropriated
surplus so as to support other programs, projects and activities that are truly beneficial
to its public schools, thus attain the purpose of Special Education Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the MDRRM Council:
a. Cause the appropriate and thorough evaluation of every project funded under the
LDRRMF, so as to ensure its readiness for immediate implementation and
completion, thus contribute greatly to the prevention/mitigation of any disaster-
related events on the lives of its constituents, pursuant to Section 12(c) of RA No.
10121; and
b. Require the MDRRMO to include in the LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be charged against
the unexpended appropriation of previous years and continuing appropriations as
a guide for the MDRRMC in the prioritization of LDRRMF, and for monitoring
purposes so as to increase its capacity to build a disaster-resilient community.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to submit the justification for the need of the
Variation Order of the aforementioned project and perform detailed site
investigations prior to commencement of on-site work to minimize the occurrence
of variation orders; and
b. Comply with the guidelines provided in Annex E of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184
in the preparation and submission of variation orders to show that the same are
justified, necessary and in order.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Officer to
strictly adopt the required monthly net take-home pay of employees of not lower than
P5,000.00, pursuant with the directive embodied in the 2024 GAA, so as to preserve the
employees’ morale and work performance. If the total authorized deductions shall
reduce net take-home pay to less than P5,000.00, deductions should be made in
accordance with the order of preference under Section 56 of 2024 GAA.

Foregoing considered, we recommended that the Local School Board submit the
Quarterly and Annual SEF Utilization Reports for CY 2024, and in succeeding years, to
the DepEd Central Office, through appropriate channels, and furnish copies thereof to
the Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG, pursuant to Item 6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-
DILG JC No. 1 s. 2017, to ensure transparency and accountability in reporting the
allocation and utilization of the Special Education Fund to the intended users of
information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory team to strategically
plan the completion of one-time cleaning of PPE in terms of inventory taking of PPE
items, reconciliation of accounting records with the Inventory Report on PPE,
disposition of missing PPE items, and those items with no available record of
accountability, and on accounting entries and Disclosures in the Notes to Financial
Statements to complete the procedures in the one-time cleansing at the soonest
possible time.

We recommended also that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Inventory Team for the results of the one-time cleansing where
there is a need to prepare with adjusting journal entries to come-up with the reliable
balance of PPE accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality at every
reporting period.

Management’s Comments:
The GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant-Designate assured that they will coordinate
to the Inventory Team in order to reconcile the accounting records with the Inventory Report
on PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
adopt the guidelines incorporated under COA Circular No. 2023-008 relative to the
write-off of the dormant receivables accounts, and immediately act accordingly, so as
to achieve fairness of the accounts’ presentation in the financial statements of the
Municipality at any reporting date, where respective accounts balances are duly
supported with valid and reliable documents to warrant their continuous recording in
their books of accounts.

Management’s Comments:
The Municipal Accountant commits to comply with the recommendation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to:
a. Limit the use of Petty Cash Fund strictly to allowable petty operating expenses in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
b. Ensure that disbursements supported by CAFOA are paid through the regular
disbursement system to ensure propriety and regularity of such disbursements.

Management’s Comments:
The Petty Cash Custodian commits to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:
a. Strictly ensure that all vital information required in the signboards on every
ongoing infrastructure projects such as the contract amount, the Approved
Budget for the Contract, dates of project commencement and of completion,
updates on the status of work accomplished as of date, name of contractor,
contact number for any work concern, and other requirements are complied with
in accordance with Section 2.2.3 and Annex A, and henceforth, strictly comply
with COA Circular No. 2013-004, so as to promote good governance through
transparency and accountability; and
b. Coordinate with the Barangay Officials with jurisdiction over the vicinity of the
project to assist in the monitoring of installed project signboards to prevent its
unauthorized removal, thus ensuring accurate communication of vital information
to the general public.

Management’s Comments:
The Municipal Engineer commits to follow the proper guidelines in preparing the project
signboards. He also assured that they will coordinate with the barangay officials to ensure
that the signboards will be properly and regularly monitored.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GADSP in accordance with
the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC)
No. 2013-01 and Sections 1 and 4 of the PCW Memorandum Circular 2018-04 to
ensure that gender issues present in the locality would be fully addressed with the
various GAD-related activities set out of the Fund so as to achieve the GAD goals
and outcome; and
b. Require the GAD Focal Point System to monitor the implementation of the GAD
Agenda according to the budget allotted for the year to avoid under-utilization of
the funds intended for each specific programs and activities.

Management’s Comments:
The Management assured that the recommendations of the Audit Team regarding the
preparation off GAD Strategic Plan and the monitoring of the implementation of the GAD
Agenda are well-taken and will be followed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to exert utmost effort and adopt effective strategies/ways to
complete the inventory taking, particularly, on Land, Land Improvements,
Infrastructure Assets, and Buildings and Other Structures;

b. designated GSO and Inventory Committee to re-evaluate the noted inconsistencies
in the adoption of the One-Time Cleansing and take note of those procedures that
can be re-applied or make corrective actions, and to complete the remaining
procedures of one-time cleansing as soon as possible;

c. Municipal Accountant and designated GSO to prepare and maintain the property
ledger cards (PPELCs and RPLCs), and property cards, respectively, for the
properties of the Municipality in accordance with Sections 119 and 120 of MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I; and

d. Municipal Accountant and designated GSO to reconcile the inventory count as
reported in the RPCPPE with property and accounting records and ensure that any
additions, reclassifications, and adjustments to PPEs are properly documented and
communicated between the two offices to maintain accurate and consistent
records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. ensure that succeeding claims are supported with the required documentation to
establish their validity and legitimacy before recording it as Accounts Payable;

b. set-up in the books of accounts the appropriate withholding taxes due on remaining
obligations booked under the Accounts Payable at year-end, and make the
necessary adjusting entries in the books. Henceforth, recognize in the books the
taxes withheld simultaneously upon set-up of Accounts Payable for goods received
or services rendered, and inspected and accepted pursuant to BIR MC No. 10-2018;
and

c. make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the noted misclassification
of obligations under the Accounts Payable into Guaranty/Security Deposit Payable
account and ensure that any succeeding transactions of the same kind are recorded
in their corresponding accounts appropriately.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prospectively recognize appropriate expense account in the books upon transfer of
the funds to different government entities to fairly present its year-end balance
pursuant to Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:

a. impose the corresponding liquidated damages for the total number of days delay
incurred by the contractor and deduct the computed amount from their claims;

b. strictly review and evaluate the Program of Works to ensure that project designs
and estimates are prepared accordingly, and that all phases of the projects are
covered to minimize variation orders and time extensions, which often result in
an increase of project cost and delayed completion; and

c. strictly monitor and supervise the status and progress of projects being
implemented by the Municipality to ensure its completion on the set time frame
thus protect the interest of the government and its general public.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. MDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council (MDRRMC), to prioritize the inclusion of
projects/programs/activities that are implementation-ready, without any restrictions
or prohibitions prior to its inclusion in the LDRRMF, and review the necessity and
reasonable funding of each PPA under the Continuing Appropriations and STF
budget so that funds allocated thereof would be utilized to the fullest within the
timelines set by the MDRRMC, thus contribute to achieving the objectives of
setting-up LDRRMF; and

b. Municipal Accountant to disclose fully in the NFS the complete amount and details
of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF of the Municipality as of year-end to
enhance the quality and transparency of financial reporting for the LDRRMF and
provide users with information relevant to decision-making.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Development Council (MDC) to assess the viability of the identified PPAs
in the planning stage to ensure that only those projects which are procurement and
implementation ready will be given budget allocation;

b. MEO, in coordination with the barangays and other concerned official, to perform
in-depth planning and detailed engineering including thorough site inspections to
anticipate and consider interruptions that may probably arise during
implementation of the projects to ensure the optimal and timely implementation of
the projects to be funded by the 20 percent DF; and

c. MBO in coordination with the MEO to reconcile their reports to ensure accuracy
and consistency of records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Designated GSO to ensure that all issued semi-expendable properties are properly
supported with ICS, to prioritize the preparation, maintenance and reconciliation of
ICS, RSPI, SPC, RegSPI and other related documents, and to update the RSPI,
reflecting all issued semi-expendable properties, regardless of the year of
acquisition;

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that all applicable data fields in the SPLC are
promptly and accurately updated to reflect all transactions involving semi-
expendable properties, and

c. Designated GSO to review the semi-expendable properties procured prior to CY
2024 and replace the existing PARs/AREs with ICS or automatically canceled for
low-valued semi-expendable properties with expired estimated useful life, in
compliance with COA Circular No. 2024-006.

We recommended that the Local School Board ensure the timely submission of the
SEF Budget to the Local Budget Officer, the Local Accountant, and the Local
Treasurer, the Quarterly and Annual SEF Utilization Reports to the DepEd Central
Office, through appropriate channels, and furnish copies thereof to the Sangguniang
Bayan and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG, and ensure the complete posting of
the said reports in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in compliance with the
Full Disclosure Policy of the DILG, pursuant to Item 6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC
No. 1 s. 2017, to ensure transparency and accountability in reporting the allocation
and utilization of the Special Education Fund to the intended users of information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Designated members of the Inventory Committee, particularly those from the Municipal Engineering and Municipal Assessor Offices, to collaborate with each other and exert utmost effort and adopt effective strategies/ways to complete the inventory taking, particularly, on Land, Other Land Improvements, Infrastructure Assets, Buildings and Other Structures and Road Networks. Ensure that all the members are temporarily relieved of their regular duties to focus on the inventory process, and strictly commit themselves to meet the timeline set in the Physical Inventory Plan for each outlined procedure in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006;

b. General Services Office and Inventory Committee to submit the duly approved and updated Physical Inventory Plan, including the inventory working papers indicated under Section 6.1.5 of the COA Circular No. 2020-006 in support of the PIP; and

c. Accounting and General Services Office to expedite the preparation and maintenance of updated Property Cards, Property Ledger Cards, and any other necessary documents to support the Inventory Committee and other stakeholders involved in the inventory process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant, using the Requisition of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by Requisition Issue Slips to make the necessary adjusting entry to reflect accurate balances of each inventory account, and commence with the preparation of Subsidiary Ledger Cards to account properly all receipts and issuances in the books; and

b. Municipal Health Officer to prepare its inventory report using the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories format prescribed in the NGAS Manual for LGUs, and submit copies thereof to the Accounting Office, copy furnished the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to consistently adhere to the rules and regulations on proper recording and reporting of financial transactions in the books pursuant to IPSAS and PD 1445 so as to report accurately each account’s balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of all dormant accounts or those accounts that have been non-moving for ten years or more, and adopt the procedures for the proper disposition of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008 to achieve a fairly presented accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Human Resource and Management officer to:

a. Trace and identify old account balances by reviewing source documents/available information to establish the validity of claims to respective inter-agencies and make representation with these agencies, if warranted;

b. Adopt an appropriate monitoring tool for the timely remittance of amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period and to prevent and detect over/under remittance of amounts withheld and deducted from personnel contributions. Review thoroughly the involved transactions and perform regular reconciliation of records; and

c. Review and analyze their respective records. Make sure that journal entries are clear of ambiguity and with sufficient details necessary to facilitate easy tracking, identification and resolution in case of disparity.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to fast-track the implementation of the two projects funded by the 2020 and 2023 20% Development Fund Continuing Appropriation. Prospectively, assess thoroughly the viability of the identified development projects in the planning stage to ensure that only those projects that are procurement and implementation ready will be given budget allocation under the 20% Development Fund pursuant to DBM-DOF- DILG JMC No. 2020-01; and

b. Require the Local Finance Committee to cause the immediate reversion of savings from completed projects to unappropriated surplus in order to finance other developmental programs, projects and activities that are truly beneficial to its constituents, thus attain the purpose of 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council headed by the Municipal Mayor, evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper planning and identification/selection of projects most in need in the locality so as to accelerate its timely implementation and reap benefits therefrom at the earliest time possible, considering the vulnerability of the Municipality to floods and landslides.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource Management Officer to:

a. Redefine existing administrative procedures and prescribe a clear operational policy and guidelines on the use of Biometrics system across all departments/offices to ensure its effective enforcement and strict observance of the rules on office attendance and punctuality; and

b. Strategize other recourse to complement or bridge the gap between the use of Biometrics and reporting requirements of employees, who, by reason of their work assignment could not access the same.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the OIC-Municipal Engineer to submit a written explanation on the noted overestimation of Subgrade Preparation and Aggregate Base Course in the computation of the Approved Budget for the Contract for the project “Supply of Labor, Materials and Equipment for the Concreting of Portion of Barangay Road at Barangay Cahilan II, Lemery, Batangas”; and

b. Require from the Contractor the refund of the excess of Contract Cost over COA Evaluated Cost, otherwise, the concerned municipal officials responsible for the transaction would be held liable for failing to exercise caution in the discharge of their duties and responsibilities leading to overestimation of subgrade preparation and aggregate base course, thereby incurring excessive expenditures of P240,042.69.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical inventory of PPEs,
label all properties with property tags/stickers and update the RPCPPE with full
and complete details to be submitted to the Audit Team not later than January 31
of the following year pursuant to Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant and GSO to prepare, maintain, update and
complete the property ledger cards (PPELCs and RPLCs), and property cards,
respectively, for the properties of the Municipality in accordance with Sections 119
and 120 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and

c. prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE balances as prescribed by
COA Circular No. 2020-006 taking strict consideration of the guidelines and
procedures on the preliminary activities, inventory taking, reconciliation, and
disposition and derecognition of non-existing/missing items for a valid reporting
and presentation of PPE accounts balances that are accurate and verifiable as to
existence, completeness and condition, thus attain fairness of presentation of all
PPE accounts in the Financial Statements of the Municipality at every reporting
period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the Reconciliation between the Actual Amounts as presented in the SCBAA
and the SFPer as additional disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements to clarify
the discrepancies between the two reports, and submit the same to the Audit Team
for the appropriate evaluation and analysis to ensure a reconciled report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Set-up in the books of accounts the appropriate withholding taxes due on
obligations booked under the Accounts Payable at year-end of P376,798.24 and
make the necessary adjusting entries in the books. Henceforth, recognize in the
books the taxes withheld simultaneously upon set-up of Accounts Payable for
goods received or services rendered and inspected and accepted pursuant to BIR
MC No. 10-2018;

b. Make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the noted
misclassification of obligations under the Accounts Payable into Other Payables
account and ensure that any succeeding transactions of the same kind are
recorded in their corresponding accounts appropriately; and

c. Review the validity of the unpaid obligations amounting to P220,929.80 which
have been outstanding for several years, and if found to have no support
documents to establish validity of such obligations against the Municipality,
henceforth, request for its reversion to unappropriated surplus from the
Sangguniang Bayan so that appropriate funds could be made available and for
use to finance other beneficial projects and programs for its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Accountable Officers to strictly liquidate the cash advances granted to them within
the prescribed timeline pursuant to Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for
safety of government funds; and

b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure observance of the
maximum cash accountability in the granting of cash advances and facilitate, if
necessary, the increase in the amount of bond so as to ensure adequate safeguard
over cash accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Disposal Committee and the
Designated Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) to:

a. Prioritize the disposal of unserviceable properties, in accordance with the COA DBM JC No. 2024-1 so as to preserve whatever value that these may present, thus
avoid its further deterioration leading to lesser resale value, and eliminate the
continuing storage cost for the government; and

b. Prepare and submit a complete and accurate Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Property in the prescribed format provided in Annex 51 of the
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II for transparency of records and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Development Council (MDC) to resolve the issue with the concerned lot
owners on the Concreting of FMR Projects at Brgy. Bungahan, and demand from
the contractors the immediate correction/rectification of the portions of the
Rehabilitation of FMR at San Juan Subdivision, Brgy. Malaruhatan and the Multi-
purpose Covered Court at Brgy. Matabungkay with damages/defects. Otherwise,
the concerned Municipal Officials responsible for the implementation, monitoring
and inspection of the completed project could be held liable for failing to adopt
measures of ensuring compliance with the project’s plans and specifications to
avoid wastage of government funds;

b. MDC to assess the viability of the identified unimplemented development projects,
or else realign/reprogram the allotted appropriations to other priority projects
which are procurement-and-implementation-ready to ensure the optimal and
timely implementation of the projects to be funded by the 20 percent DF; and

c. Local Finance Committee (LFC) to cause the immediate reversion of savings from
completed projects to unappropriated surplus in order to finance other
developmental programs, projects and activities that are truly beneficial to its
constituents, thus attain the purpose of 20 percent DF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. MDRRMO, in coordination with the MDRRMC, to prioritize the inclusion of PPAs
that are implementation-ready, without any restrictions or prohibitions prior to its
inclusion in the LDRRMF, and review the necessity and reasonable funding of
each PPA under the Continuing Appropriations and STF budget so that funds
allocated thereof would be utilized to the fullest within the timelines set by the
MDRRMC, thus contribute to achieving the objectives of setting-up LDRRMF; and

b. MDRRMO to ensure the correctness of the incorporated data in the LDRRMFIP
and to include therein the PPAs to be charged against the unexpended
appropriation of previous years and continuing appropriations as a guide for the
MDRRMC in the prioritization of LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the GFPS, to:

a. Establish a functional GAD Database in accordance with Section 4 of the PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 that will strengthen the planning and budgeting
processes for GAD and ensure that planned PPAs address identified gender issues
and that objectives, performance indicators and targets are well-formulated to
facilitate monitoring and evaluation for the attainment of GAD objectives; and

b. Require the GFPS Executive Committee to submit the Annual GPB to the PPDO,
from PPDO to DILG Provincial Office and secure the Certificate of Endorsement
from the DILG Provincial Office, where such certification would truly establish the
relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness of the PPAs
indicated in the GPB pursuant to Section 5.0 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all the properties rightfully
belong to the Municipality, and submit on time the duly accomplished and complete
inventory reports, including Inventory Count Form and Report on the Physical Count
of PPE;
b. Accounting Office and Inventory Committee to strengthen reconciliation procedures
and pursue reconciled PPE records;
c. Accounting and General Service Offices to expedite the preparation and maintenance
of updated Property Cards, Property Ledger Cards, and any other necessary
documents to support the Inventory Committee and other stakeholders involved in the
inventory process;
d. Accounting Office to seek approval from appropriate authorities for the write-off or
derecognition of old/beginning balances, lumpsum and unverified amounts and to take
up the necessary accounting entries pertaining thereto;
e. General Services Office to prepare the updated PAR and submit the report to the
Team for inspection and validation;
f. Inventory Committee to conduct inspection and prepare Inventory and Inspection
Report of Unserviceable Property for all unserviceable properties and initiate disposal
proceeding; and
g. Inventory Committee to maintain Registry of Derecognized PPE to track all
derecognized assets, with complete asset details and documentation on disposal
actions taken by Management and the authority or approval granted by appropriate
authorities, where applicable.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare and record the necessary adjusting entries to correct the noted
understatement of appropriate PPE accounts by P22,135,221.24, understatement
and overstatement of Other Payables and Trust Liability accounts by P311,200.00
and understatement of Net Assets/Equity account by P22,135,221.24, including the
recognition or adjustment of related accumulated depreciation for the affected PPE
accounts, and submit to the Audit Team the action taken thereon.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee prepare the updated RPCPPE,
considering that such adjustments would affect the PPE accounts with the
recognition of the aforementioned projects into PPE by the Accounting Office so as
to reflect an accurate and a reliable property record and reports.

a. Immediately prepare and submit to the Audit Team the required monthly BRS for all
accounts being maintained with the LBP, DBP and PVB, pursuant to PD No. 1445
and Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011, as this is essential for timely detection
and identification of errors, and resolve transaction leaks, whereby it serves as a way
to prevent and detect fraud;
b. Submit explanation/justification as to the continued non-preparation of BRS for the
four bank accounts under the General Fund since CY 2021;
c. Perform reconciliation of records with the Treasury Office to identify the nature and
particulars of the noted discrepancy in records totaling P8,783.00; and
d. For stale checks of P13,123.18, prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to cancel
the payment recorded for the stale checks and restore the amount to cash and
appropriate liability accounts and furnish the Municipal Treasurer with a copy of the
JEV as her basis for correcting the amount of stale checks in the cashbook.
We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer to send written notices
to payees with stale checks and verify the continued existence of valid claims. If
warranted, require from them the submission of documents, including the stale
check itself, to facilitate the issuance of a check replacement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the HRMO and the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Trace and identify old accounts and negative balances by reviewing source
documents/available information to establish the validity of claims to respective
inter-agencies and make representations with these agencies, if warranted; and
b. Adopt an appropriate monitoring tool for the timely remittance of amounts due to
the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period and to prevent
and detect over/under remittance of amounts withheld and deducted from personnel
contributions. Review thoroughly the involved transactions and perform regular
reconciliation of records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal
Engineer to submit a written explanation on the erroneous application of indirect
cost of 25% instead of 20% in the computation of the Approved Budget for the
Contract for the project “Supply of Labor, Materials and Equipment for the
Concreting/Improvement of Brgy. Road at Purok Maligaya 2, Brgy. Banalo, Purok 7,
Brgy. Masaguitsit and Brgy. Jaybanga, Lobo, Batangas”; and
b. Require from the Contractor a refund of the excess of Contract Cost over COA
Evaluated Cost in the amount of P452,399.25. Otherwise, the concerned
municipal officials responsible for the transactions would be held liable for
failing to exercise caution in the discharge of their duties and responsibilities
leading to incorrect application of percentage rates for indirect cost.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal
Engineer to submit a written explanation relative to the defects and uninstalled
items for the projects: 1) Supply of Materials, Labor and Equipment for the
Rehabilitation of Fram to Market Road at Barangay Calumpit, Lobo, Batangas; 2)
Supply of Materials, Labor and Equipment for the Concreting/Improvement of
Multi-Purpose Hall at Barangay Mabilog na Bundok, Lobo Batangas; and 3)
Supply of Materials, Labor and Equipment for the Concreting/Improvement of
Multi-Purpose Hall at Barangay Sawang, Lobo Batangas; and
b. Demand from the contractors the immediate correction/rectification of the
portions of Concrete Pavement with cracks and scaling, and installation of the
items not found in the constructed Multi-Purpose Halls of Barangay Mabilog na
Bundok and Sawang. Otherwise, the concerned municipal officials responsible
for the implementation, monitoring and inspection of completed projects would
be held liable for failing to adopt measures of ensuring compliance with the
projects’ plans and specifications, which avoid wastage of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal General
Services Officer to:
a. Cause the immediate submission of the duly accomplished Delivery Receipt for the
equipment delivered, the Performance /Quality Test Results for Sinotruk Mini
Dump Truck and the Specification Plate for the Excavator in order to fully ascertain
the reliability and accuracy of the details of the procured equipment and ensure that
19
the procured equipment are in accordance with the terms and conditions set by the
LGU; and
b. Submit a written justification/explanation on the lacking information and records,
and the discrepancies in the technical specifications of the procured heavy
equipment. Otherwise, appropriate audit action would be instituted to protect the
interest of the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned local officials to:
a. Make appropriate and immediate representation with the higher officials of the
PAGCOR for the approval, either of the long pending proposed revision of the
Project’s design or for other alternative proposition that would lead to the
completion of the said Project, and submit to the Audit Team the status/updates
on the action taken relative thereto; and
b. If all else failed, implement a legally appropriate, multi-faceted approach to
ensure the completion of the project so as to attain its purpose and avoid
wastage of government funds. Otherwise, non-completion of the subject project
might lead to appropriate audit action that the Audit Team may take given the
prevailing circumstances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator and Municipal Engineer to fast-track the implementation
of those projects that are still not completed as at year-end. Prospectively, assess
thoroughly the viability of the identified development projects during the planning
stage to ensure that only those projects that are procurement and implementation
ready would be given budget allocation under the 20% Development Fund.,
pursuant to DBM-DOF- DILG JMC No. 2020-01.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council,
headed by the Municipal Mayor, to cause the immediate implementation of the four
projects funded under the Mitigation Fund – Capital Outlay of CY 2024 with total
appropriation of P1,596,240.83, or re-evaluate the needs of their constituents and
adopt, accordingly, all the procedures necessary for its implementation so as to
optimally utilized the funds for projects that are truly beneficial and needed in the
locality.
We also recommended that the LDRRM Council prepare the LDRRMFIP for CY 2025
using the prescribed format of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and update the Plan
annually, identifying the activities to be funded from the Current Appropriations and
unexpended Special Trust Fund, for clarity and monitoring of projects for
implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource and
Management Officer to strictly adopt the required monthly net take-home pay of
employees of not lower than P5,000.00, pursuant with the directive embodied in the
2024 GAA, so as to preserve the employees’ morale and work performance. If the
total authorized deductions shall reduce net take-home pay to less than P5,000.00,
deductions should be made in accordance with the order of preference under
Section 56 of 2024 GAA.
We also recommended that the Municipal Cooperative Development Office exercise
due diligence and caution in granting loan applications to its members, depending on
their financial and paying capacity. Refrain from approving the loan application of
member who could not meet the required monthly net take-home pay, following the
existing laws, rules, and regulations.

We reiterated the prior year's audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant and GSO to conduct the one-time cleansing of account following the guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and to obtain and reconcile the list of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) recorded in their books. A physical inventory of PPE has already been conducted in all barangays of the municipality, while the inventory of assets per office within the municipal government is currently ongoing. Upon completion of the office-level inventory, the Municipality shall undertake the one-time cleansing of accounts in accordance with the guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. The target date for the completion of the one-time cleansing is March 2026. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to ensure that all reported items in the RPCPPE are physically existing and duly accounted for based on the annual inventory count undertaken, and to ascertain that the said report is duly prepared in accordance with the prescribed format, supplying all the necessary particulars therein.

Foregoing considered, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant costs of to: a. Adjust the Accumulated Depreciation and the Accumulated Surplus by ₱1,736,768.28. b. Commence the recognition of depreciation at the date of its completion, and not at the date of final payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. General Service Officer (GSO) to consolidate (BAC) Purchase Requests (PRs) of goods alike and similar in nature and refer all PRs to the BAC for adoption and conduct of appropriate procurement modality; b. BAC Secretariat to prepare, based on Project Procurement Management Plans of implementing offices, an indicative Annual Procurement Plan (APP) aligned with the budget proposal; c. GSO and BAC to undertake the procurement in accordance with the approved APP, taking into consideration the consolidation of purchases and the timeline set therein. We further recommended Increase in personnel responsible in the procurement so as not to compromise the substantive and procedural requirements of the procurement law.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Review the validity of Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable which are outstanding for over one year; and securing the Certification from the end-users/Municipal Engineer concerning the final acceptance of the concerned projects/goods and thereafter notify in writing all contractors/suppliers with valid claims to file their request for the immediate release of the retention money. b. Notify the contractors and their private entities concerned to file their claims for the immediate release of the retention money to avoid accumulation of the account balances in the books. Otherwise, require them to execute a waiver of their right to claim such deposits for the reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

Hence, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and GSO to facilitate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties, including the determination of the appropriate mode of disposal and appraisal of the same, if necessary, to prevent further deterioration and reduction of the properties' economic value.

We recommended that the General Services Office and Municipal Engineering Office acknowledge the audit observation and strictly comply with Section 301 of PD No. 1096 (National Building Code of the Philippines). Moving forward, the offices shall ensure that all future demolitions of government properties will be supported with duly secured demolition permits issued by the Municipal Building Official prior to implementation. We likewise recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Building Official to undertake proactive measures in implementing the required permit for government properties where the fact of demolition came to his knowledge.

Therefore, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to immediately issue ICS signed by the designated end-users for semi-expendable items that are not yet covered by the said form to establish clear accountability and proper monitoring of government properties.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to re-evaluate the planned Program, Projects and Activities (PPAs), and reprogram the funds available in the Continuing Appropriations and Special Trust Fund. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF, taking into consideration possible obstacles associated thereto, and to prioritizing the PPAs that are ready-for-implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to: a. create an Inventory Committee for the one-time cleansing of PPE in 2026. b. require the General Services Officer, Municipal Accountant and the duly created Inventory Committee to follow the procedural guidelines on the one-time cleansing of PPE as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 such as (1) Preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking; (2) Inventory taking; (3) Reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records; (4) Disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs; (5) Procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability; and (6) Accounting entries and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and GSO to prepare the Local Road Network Ledger Card and the Local Road Network Property Card.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. MDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs; and
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the GFPS to prepare the GAD Agenda pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GSO to conduct a periodic physical count of inventory as required in Section 124 of NGAS Manual for LGUs and submit the Report of Physical Count of Inventory (RPCI) to the Accounting Office and copy furnish the Audit Team. The GSO shall conduct the yearly inventory taking of all PPEs, prepare the corresponding Inventory and submit to the COA within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Engineer to obtain documents from the DPWH DEO relative
to these projects for recording as Municipal assets;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize under the appropriate PPE
accounts the 12 completed projects with aggregate contract cost of
P215,038,442.81 and record as prior year adjustments the relative accumulated
depreciation of P8,276,703.03; and

c. Ensure necessary coordination with the DPWH DEO on the implementation of
government projects, particularly those that are intended to be subsequently
transferred to the LGU, to enable timely and complete recording of Municipal
assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to include in the estimate of fund appropriation under
Personnel Services the estimated cost of earned leave credits per year starting
this year 2025 as part of the employee benefits; and

b. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries for the leave
benefit expenses incurred in prior years including that for year 2024 and record
quarterly the expenses for earned leave credits as incurred beginning with the
first quarter of 2025.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record
purchases under the appropriate semi-expendable inventory account and recognize
expense upon issuance to the end-user based on the submitted Report of Semi-
Expendable Property Issued by the GSO and likewise maintain Semi-Expendable
Property Ledger Card for each class of semi-expendable property.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GSO to maintain
Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued and Semi-Expendable Property Card for
each class of issued semi-expendable property, prepare the Report of Semi-
Expendable Property Issued (RSPI) to report all issued semi-expendable property and
submit the same to the Municipal Accountant at least weekly.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget
Officer to allocate and obligate funds for semi-expendable items using the
appropriate inventory and expense account to ensure alignment with the records of
the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Recognize the Tax Revenue - Fines and Penalties - Property Taxes for every
collection of Real Property Tax – Basic and Special Education Tax; and

b. Record collections of advance payments during the year in the GF and SEF books
of accounts and record the related liability using Deferred Real Property Tax and
Deferred Special Education Tax accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
receive collections for advance payments in the General and SEF accounts, record
them in the separate Cashbooks for Cash – Local Treasury and deposit the same in
their corresponding fund accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take
up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to adjust the
surplus and net book value of the affected PPE accounts totaling P373,321.05 and
submit the same to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly observe
the policies on the recognition of depreciation for PPE prescribed in PAG 3 of IPSAS
17 and Section 104 of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Heads of the Municipal departments and offices, in coordination with the General
Services Officer, to ensure that complete documentation for all delivered
purchases as of year-end is submitted to the Municipal Accountant for proper
recording of Accounts Payable; and

b. Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entry to record the P41,778.00
Documentary Stamp Tax as separate expense under appropriate budgetary
provisions and to reflect full amount of the loan.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer
to obligate funds upon perfection of contracts in order to provide/earmark funds for all
commitments or encumbrances incurred as of every year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to
ensure that a minimum of five percent of the annual budget is appropriated as
LDRRMF including all regular sources of income.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prepare adjusting entries for the misclassification of the P100,000.00 and
P30,000.00 donations; and

b. Ensure that disclosures for the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account is completely and
accurately presented in accompanying Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC - Municipal Engineer,
Accountant and General Services Officer to establish each road network under the
Municipality’s jurisdiction with segregated costing per component based on complete
description and cost segregation of road components in time for the cleansing of
Property, Plant and Equipment of the Municipality.

Thereafter, we recommended that the GSO maintain road networks property cards
and Report on Local Road Network and the Municipal Accountant prepare and
maintain the prescribed property ledger cards for Local Road Networks and disclose
all the required information for the said properties in the Notes to Financial
Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Engineer to incorporate the occupational safety and health
in every project construction cost, and

b. Direct the BAC to require the concerned contractors of completed and currently
ongoing infrastructure projects to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP in
compliance with DOLE DO No. 13, s. 1998 and Section 37.2.3(f) of the Revised
IRR of RA No. 9184 or refund of the pay item for occupational health and safety.

We also recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant
require the contractors to submit CSHP duly approved by DOLE prior to signing of
contracts for future projects to be implemented by the Municipality, and deduct the
corresponding CSHP cost from the payment to the contractor, in case the contractor
could not submit the approved CSHP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the responsible office to prepare
and submit the detailed breakdown of ABC for all infrastructure projects to enhance
transparency and facilitate proper cost estimation and evaluation.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Engineer
to consistently prepare and accomplish the Certificate of Inspection upon the
completion of all infrastructure projects to ensure that all projects are executed in
accordance with the approved plans and specifications, and will serve as proper
documentation of project compliance and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the HRMO to:

a. Formulate uniform policy and procedures in engaging and managing outsourced
workers taking into consideration the relevant issuances of oversight agencies;
and internal controls to ensure that they are performing the required task,
reporting to the official station, and bases of assigning wage rates; and

b. Require offices to document their organizational structure presenting the actual
manpower requirement vis-a-vis the number of existing permanent personnel as
basis for requesting for hiring of JOs of the concerned offices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to
institutionalize the conduct of gender analysis during the planning phase of all PAPs
with the use of appropriate tools such as the Gender Responsive LGU (GERL)
Assessment, Gender Mainstreaming Evaluation Framework (GMEF), the Harmonized
Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG), and sex-disaggregated data to support
the identification of gender issues and require the implementing offices of the
relevant documentation for means of verification of the identified PAPs based on
results of gender analysis.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Create an Inventory Committee tasked to adopt and implement the Committee procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I and COA Circular No. 2020-006, thus exist the PPE non-accounts’ gross balance of ₱3,211,057,148.23 was not duly established affecting the fair presentation of PPE accounts in the Financial Statements of the Municipality at every reporting period; and

b. Instruct the Inventory Committee and the GSO to prepare a complete and updated RPCPPE with full and complete details of information relative to each PPE items to be submitted to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of the following year pursuant to the Audit Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I to support the accuracy of PPE accounts balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to:

a. Submit within 60 days from receipt hereof the plan for the implementation of the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, and the MDRRM Office; Then, a Sangguniang Bayan identified. Then, a Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No. 222 was approved regarding the reprogramming and utilization of LDRRMF Trust Fund amounting P7,674,389.52.

b. Strive for the timely and full implementation of the LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objectives of the fund so that the benefits accorded to these projects can be fully availed of by its intended beneficiaries; and
