c. Identify and breakdown by year composition of STF and revert the portion of STF pertaining to CY 2018 to General Fund, including of any savings out of the completed projects, so that appropriate funds can be used to finance other projects and programs of the municipality.

We recommended that the LSB submit the Quarterly and Annual SEF Utilization Reports for CY 2024 for CY 2024, and in succeeding years to the DepEd Central Office, through appropriate channels, and furnish copies thereof to the Sangguniang Bayan, and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG within the prescribed timeline, pursuant to Item No. 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1 s 2017, to ensure transparency and accountability in the allocation and utilization of the Special Education Fund to the intended users of information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 and PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2018-04, to ensure that gender issues of the locality would be fully addressed with the various GAD-related activities set out of the Fund so as to achieve the GAD Goals and the outcome;

b. Empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others; and

c. Require the GAD Focal Point System to monitor the implementation of the GAD Agenda according to the budget allotted for the year to avoid under-utilization of the funds intended for each specific programs and activities.

We recommend the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Recompute the depreciation based on the approved estimated useful life of each Infrastructure Assets indicated in DPWH EO No. 176, series of 2015; and

b. Prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting records to correct the depreciation and effect the necessary adjustments in order to rectify the errors and present the asset accounts to their proper carrying amounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the concerned CITS personnel to formulate solutions in order that the computerization project procured will be more productive and unserviceable; and inform the system provider for the system (GAAMS), (TOIMS) and Management of the computerization system project.

b. Submit justification or explanation on why last payment was made to the contractor even with the issues with the system that were not yet addressed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: .

a. Require the Municipal Assessor to submit lacking documentary requirements to support the payment of the purchased real property, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 for further evaluation of the Audit Team; and

b. Request the Sangguniang Bayan for the ratification of the approved budget for the purchase of land, specifying the purpose of purchase, cost, location and other details needed for the authorization of the local chief executive to enter into contracts on behalf of the local government unit.
We recommend the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the noted lacking documents required in the grant of cash advance corresponding to the above CF cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the other members of the GAD Focal Point System, to modify the structure and composition of the GFPS to conform with the prescribed organizational structure under Section 4.1(2) of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive Committee to require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to:

a. Prioritize gender mainstreaming efforts in GAD planning and budgeting by using existing gender analysis tools such as the HGDG in the identification, design, implementation, monitoring and evaluation stages; and

b. Develop a GAD Database or Sex-Disaggregated Data for proper utilization in the planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation of the Municipality, as well as proper charging of the object of expenditures on actual accomplishments.

We recommended that:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant and the Committee to reconcile their records and adopt the procedural guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE balances of the Municipality;

b. Require the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties with acquisition cost of P4,174,768.83 to prevent further deterioration of the asset and decongest the storage area, in accordance with existing rules and regulations and in compliance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445; and

c. Plan/strategize and take remedial actions to reconcile and regularize the discrepancies between the accounting records and RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Re-compute the depreciation based on the approved estimated useful life of each Infrastructure Assets under DPWH DO No. 176, series of 2015 as the basis of estimating its useful life and make the necessary recomputation and preparation of journal entries as recommended; and

b. Prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting records on the depreciation and effect the necessary adjustments in order to rectify the errors and present the asset accounts to their proper carrying amounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Undertake a thorough review and analysis of the details of the payable accounts that remained outstanding for more than two years and submit the result of analysis to the Audit Team; 

b. Prepare the corresponding Journal Entry and Voucher, after seeking authority from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to revert to unappropriated surplus all APs that remained outstanding for two years and more, duly supported by the list of APs reverted; and

c. For all APs that were reverted to unappropriated surplus, determine first the existence, validity and legality of claims before making the payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO and Municipal Planning and Development Office to strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM related PPAs to achieve the goals and objectives of the fund to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to build a disaster resilient community and enhance disaster preparedness and response.

We recommended that:

a. The Inventory Committee headed by the General Services Officer (GSO) to continue the conduct of physical count of PPE in order to determine the existence of the recorded PPE accounts and reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and establish the causes of discrepancies, whether items have already been missing, donated, sold and unserviceable to arrive at the correct balance in the financial statements. As to the infrastructure assets, coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to verify their existence, and prepare and maintain the required Property Cards and update Acknowledgment Receipt of Equipment (ARE) to facilitate the reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records;

b. The Municipal Accountant in coordination with the GSO to clearly Identify the items under Replacement Costing and Labor Payroll, amounting to P81,384,996.97 as to what specific PPE items or accounts they were attributed; and

c. The GSO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant to consider the remedy provided in COA Circular No. 2020-06, dated January 31, 2020 to assist in coming up with the reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and GSO (i) maintain a record of the perpetual inventory method for accounting of supplies and inventories, (ii) maintain subsidiary records for inventory in the form of ledger cards and stock cards, and (iii) ensure regular reconciliation of the accounting and property records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee to submit the lacking documents noted above or provide justification for the further evaluation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Head of the Municipal Planning and Development Office to coordinate with the concerned department heads to enforce the utilization and implementation of those projects that were not yet implemented while pursuing the continuation of ongoing utilization. Pursue the enforcement of utilization and monitoring of the utilization of unexpended balances.

We recommended that the GSO to continue the conduct of the required physical count of PPE. Prepare and maintain the inventory of PPE, Property Cards and update Acknowledgment Receipt of Equipment (ARE) to facilitate the reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records; and the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the GSO to clearly identify the items under Replacement Costing and Labor Payroll, amounting to P81,384,996.97 as to what specific PPE items or accounts they were attributed.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct all concerned department heads and heads to coordinate with each other and exert extra efforts to secure the documents relative to the purchase made from Mr. Reynaldo Cargado. Work together towards the reconciliation and registration of all landholdings to protect the municipality against undue risk of third-party claim.

We recommended that:

a. GSO to (i) prepare the stock cards for the purchased supplies; (ii) conduct periodic inventory of supplies; (iii) prepare the corresponding RPCI and submit the same to the Accounting Office for reconciliation with the reported inventory balances in the books; and

b. Municipal Accountant to (i) record the purchases under the proper Inventory account as Asset instead of as Expenses and (ii) maintain inventory ledger cards for all inventory accounts to support the recorded balance of inventory.

We recommended that the MPDC, in coordination with the GAD Focal Point, to:

a. Spearhead the establishment of the GAD Database, manual or on electronic, consisting of sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information;

b. Ensure the effective implementation of the database that contains systematically gathered and regularly updated information relevant to gender-responsive planning, programming, and policy formulation; and

c. Evaluate thoroughly the data gathered from the database to come up with gender-responsive and gender-sensitive plans and programs which are in accordance with the GAD mandate.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman in coordination with the BAC Technical Working Group to explain the inconsistencies in the procurement process and to immediately submit the aforementioned lacking documents and other relevant supporting documents/ reports to facilitate further review and evaluation by the Audit Team and the COA Information and Technology Audit Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MENRO to expedite the compliance of the requested documents necessary for the approval of the 10-Year Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan (2019-2028) by the NSWMC and to expedite the disposal of residual waste in the central MRF that lead to the proper implementation of the SCRP and the renovation of the damaged steel gate and other requirements for approval.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned department heads to immediately initiate the settlement of the disallowances in accordance with the Revised Rules of Procedure on the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA) and secure documents such as the Notice of Finality of Decision/COA order of Execution to trace back those disallowances reflected in the Receivables - Disallowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to expedite the submission of the request for write-off of these dormant accounts to the Commission on Audit which should be supported with Sangguniang Bayan Resolution requesting write-off of the dormant accounts, certification as to extent of validation made by them on these accounts and reasons why the records could not be located, aging schedule and available records pertaining to these dormant accounts, if there is any.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned action to enforce the settlement of disallowances, and the Municipal Accountant be directed to record the audit disallowances of P4,117,000.00 as Receivables–Disallowances/Charges, providing subsidiary records on individual recipients of the disallowed claims that includes the reference ND and the liable persons and include disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements (NFS) regarding the audit disallowances of P3,654,662.51 that is subject of a recently-submitted request for reconsideration to COA RO IV, Quezon City.

We recommend ded that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the transfer of nine completed projects
with an aggregate contract cost of P222,407,326.64 and carrying value of
P180,516,438.93; and

b. Ensure necessary coordination with the DPWH on the implementation of
government projects, particularly those that are intended to be subsequently
transferred to the LGU, to ascertain timely and complete recording of assets in the
financial statements.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Engineer to furnish the Municipal Accountant copies of Certificate of
Completion of the above projects and moving forward, upon every issuance of the
same;

b. Municipal Accountant to immediately book up as CIP account and Accounts
Payable the unpaid portion of the above completed projects upon receipt of the said
copies of Certificate of Completion. Accordingly, reclassify the completed assets to
appropriate PPE accounts and recognize depreciation when it is available for use; and

c. Municipal Accountant to record PPEs upon actual date of completion and not on
the dates of partial or full payment.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that the succeeding grants of assistance for these purposes
are recorded in their corresponding account appropriately.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to strictly implement monthly reconciliation of reciprocal accounts such
as Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to ensure the accuracy and
reliability of reported balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant in
coordination with the concerned offices to produce the required supporting
documents and to document all procedures employed to establish the location or
whereabouts of the debtors and borrowers involved including the actions taken to
collect the amounts due from them; and adopt the provisions of COA Circular No.
2023-008 for the cleansing of dormant accounts.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant and General Services Office
(GSO) to conduct the one-time cleansing of account following the guidelines and
procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and submit to
the Audit Team the proposed timeline for the immediate completion thereof.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to
ensure that all reported items in the RPCPPE are those PPEs physically existing and
duly accounted with complete descriptions and cost per item.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the General Services Office in coordination with the Accounting Office
and other departments concerned to establish a Disposal Committee responsible
for overseeing the disposal process of unserviceable properties in a timely and
efficient manner;

b. Conduct a comprehensive inventory and valuation of all unserviceable properties
with clear documentation to determine the exact condition of each asset and in
coordination with the Appraisal Committee to establish the appraised values of
the properties; and

c. Fast track the disposal of the unserviceable properties to prevent further
deterioration of the assets and decongest the storage area, in accordance with the
procedures provided by the Revised Manual on Disposal of Government
Properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
formulate a well-defined policy/guideline, include therein documentary requirements,
criteria for the selection of qualified applicants, desired amount and the like, for the
grants and financial assistance to ensure determination of qualified
individuals/entities, equitable allocation of assistance and economical use of
Municipal funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO, in coordination with
the Municipal Budget Office to consider the update of the daily wage rate of JO
workers of the Municipality taking into consideration the daily rate of comparable
position in the government, and if budget restrictions persist, at least consider the
daily minimum wage rate in the private sector.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the hiring of a private lawyer without
securing the prior written concurrence of the COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRM Officer to:

a. Judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF and to resolve
the issues on the implementation of PPAs to achieve the goal/target of the
Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and
management; and

b. Ensure that all available fund for the DRRM has corresponding PPAs that will be
included in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Office of the MPDC and
Municipal Engineer to ensure timely utilization of 20 percent Development Fund in the
succeeding periods by including development projects that are well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation-ready and that detailed engineering has been
properly conducted by the Municipal Engineer for infrastructure projects, to evaluate
their readiness.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee together with the Gender Focal Point System and the Technical Working
Group:

a. Conduct thorough planning and analysis, taking into consideration the feasibility and
propriety of the proposed PPA to ensure that those identified PPAs to be included in
Page 19 of 21

the GAD Plan and Budget are those that can be implemented and completed within
the target period so that the envisioned GAD objectives and results will be fully
achieved; and

b. Require all offices to submit their individual offices’ GPB at an earlier date set by the
GFPS in time for their preparation and submission of the consolidated GPB within
the prescribed deadline. This is to be able to secure the Certificate of Endorsement
of the Annual GAD Plan and Budget from the DILG Provincial Office before
incorporating to the Municipality’s Annual Budget the planned PPAs to properly
ascertain the relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness
of the PPAs indicated in the GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the General Services Office and Disposal Committee to furnish the
Accounting Office with the supporting documents of the IIRUP, as required by
COA-DBM JC No. 2024-1, to facilitate the removal of non-existing/missing PPE
items costing P14,066,799.91 from the books of accounts; and
b. In case there are no available records, direct the Accounting Office and General
Services Office to follow the procedures for the disposition and derecognition of
non-existing/missing PPEs detailed in Sections 7 and 8 of COA Circular No. 2020-
006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the FS in compliance with the requirements of IPSAS, particularly on the
reconciliation of the actual amounts of income and expenses per SCBAA with the
figures presented in the Statement of Financial Performance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant and General Services Office (GSO) to conduct the one-time cleansing of account to eliminate the discrepancies noted, following the guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and submit to the Audit Team the proposed timeline for the immediate completion thereof. Henceforth, ensure the preparation of complete Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) in accordance with the NGAS Manual for LGUs to establish accuracy in the reported values.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. The Acting Municipal Treasurer to review the noted discrepancies, reconcile and immediately update the Audit Team on the results of the reconciliation, record the disbursements by check in the Cashbook based on its issuance and submit the updated Cashbook for TF PCF and TF PFPR; and
b. The Municipal Accountant to review the noted observations above, prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry and submit the same to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Also, submit the bank statements of Cash in Bank – LCCA under TF –PCF and PFPR for February to December 2024. Lastly, ensure that the succeeding bank reconciliation statements will be supported with monthly bank statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. The Municipal Accountant and the acting Municipal Treasurer to conduct regular reconciliation of their balances at least quarterly, to immediately adjust and/or take up in the books any errors detected by both office and ensure the correctness of the balance of the Cash – Local Treasury account in the financial statements;
b. The acting Municipal Treasurer to review the noted discrepancies and immediately update the Audit Team on the results of the reconciliation. Likewise, properly fill-up the Cashbook, particularly, the footing of entries at the end of each month and update the same daily; and
c. The Municipal Accountant to review the noted observations and make the necessary adjusting journal entries to be submitted to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Also, ensure that succeeding collections and deposits are properly recorded in the Cash Receipts Journal per date of transaction. Include in the particulars of the journals the reference OR Nos. to ensure that all issued ORs for the collections made by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office were all accounted for and recorded in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to:
a. Cause the immediate implementation of the remaining unimplemented project and the completion of the on-going projects identified in the audit finding and provide an update as to the status of those projects. If the projects will no longer be implemented and the funds are reprogrammed to another projects, include those along with the reasons in the status for submission to the audit team; and
b. Consider the unexpended balances of the completed projects as of December 31, 2024 of P52,190.36 for reversion to unappropriated surplus for possible allocation to other developmental projects.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
 Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to adequately plan and monitor the 17 programs, projects and activities under the Current, Continuing Appropriation and Trust Liability totaling P10,980,758.26 for effective utilization of the LDRRMF and update the audit team as to the status of their implementation; and
 Municipal Budget Officer to consider the unexpended balances of the completed projects of P2,525.67 for reversion to unappropriated surplus for possible allocation to other disaster mitigation and response projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Accountable Officers to liquidate the cash advances within the prescribed period; and
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances granted do not exceed the maximum cash accountabilities, otherwise, require the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer and other AO who also serve as the Disbursing Officer to consider increasing their fidelity bond to sufficiently cover the total daily accountabilities in accordance with the schedule provided under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
 Municipal Accountant to exhaust efforts to secure copies of documents to verify the existence and validity of the fund transfers in the amount of P63,533,732.79. Conduct confirmation among identified source agencies to confirm the balances due to them and facilitate the return of the remaining unutilized/unused fund; and
 Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to communicate with the departments concerned the fund transfers and ensure the immediate implementation of the planned programs/projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal HRMO to:
a. Strictly comply with the conditions as stipulated in the JO contracts. Ensure completeness of documentation for all payments made and comply with Section 1.2.1, Section 4.1.3 and Section 5.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-001;
b. Establish a more stringent process for reviewing the Daily Time Report (DTR) and accomplishment report, in coordination with the department heads, as basis for the payment to JO personnel to ensure that the days paid, days worked and tasks performed align; and
c. Strictly enforce its policies on work hours and implement corresponding deductions for any observed late or undertime based on the DTRs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the required documents as stated in Annex I, to complete the audit of the transactions. Likewise, ensure that supporting documents are complete and properly verified before payments are made.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GFPS Focal Point Person and GAD TWG to conduct thorough planning and analysis to ensure that those identified PPAs to be included in the GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2025 are those that are considered by the Municipality as gender activities that can immediately address the identified gender issues/mandates. Take into consideration the feasibility and propriety of the proposed PPA before including it in the plan and exert effort in accomplishing all the targeted PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Engineer to obtain documents from the DPWH DEO relative to
these projects, and determine whether the roads are part of the municipal road
network, for recording as Municipal assets;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize under the appropriate PPE accounts the
11 completed projects, including road as verified to be part of the Municipal
road network, with aggregate contract cost of P141,413,898.25 and record as prior
year adjustments the relative accumulated depreciation of P7,617,018.64; and

c. Ensure necessary coordination with the DPWH DEO on the implementation of
government projects, particularly those that are intended to be subsequently
transferred to the Municipality, to enable timely and complete recording of
Municipal assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to include in the estimate of fund appropriation under
Personnel Services the estimated cost of earned leave credits per year starting this
year 2025 as part of the employee benefits;

b. Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to update the leave cards, adjust
balances for the correct computation of mandatory leave, and ensure that DTRs are
validated on a monthly basis to reflect approved leave, official travel, or any other
justified reasons for missing time entries that did not result in deduction from leave
credits; and

c. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries for the leave benefit
expenses incurred in prior years including that for year 2024 and record quarterly
the expenses for earned leave credits as incurred beginning with the first quarter
of 2025.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take
up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to correct the
misstatement in the carrying amount of PPE accounts totaling P3,247,361.99 and
submit the same to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly observe
the policies on the recognition of PPE and its depreciation prescribed in PAG 3 of
IPSAS 17 and Section 125 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume III.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to obligate funds upon perfection of contracts in order to
provide/earmark funds for all commitments or encumbrances incurred as of every
year-end; and

b. Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review all claims of suppliers/contractors,
prohibit processing payments to suppliers prior to full delivery and ensure that
these are recorded as Accounts Payable only upon actual receipt of goods or
completion of services.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and GSO
to ensure that all required forms be properly maintained, filled out and updated
regularly. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant accordingly draw
adjusting entries for the reported inventory based on RSPI from the GSO.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Engineer, Accountant and GSO to establish each road network under the
Municipality’s jurisdiction with segregated costing per component based on
complete description and cost segregation of road components in time for the
cleansing of Property, Plant and Equipment of the Municipality;

b. GSO to maintain road networks property cards and Report on Local Road Network
and the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the prescribed property ledger
cards for Local Road Networks and disclose all the required information for the said
properties in the Notes to Financial Statements;

c. Inventory Committee to conduct and update the inventory count of all the
Municipality’s Road Network Systems and accordingly submit a Report on the
Physical Count of the Road Network System as of every year-end; and

d. Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting Journal Entry Voucher debiting the Prior
Period Adjustment account and crediting the Accumulated Depreciation–Road
Networks account totaling P469,803.20 and stop the practice of providing residual
value for the depreciable component of the road network systems in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRM Officer to re-evaluate
the feasibility of planned PPAs and identify projects which will no longer be
implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare adjusting entries for the erroneous utilization recorded in the Trust-Liability
DRRM account amounting to P339,210.55 and misclassification of the P20,000.00
donations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Issue a certification that cash advances were liquidated and accounted for in the
books prior to grant of any cash advance to an accountable officer, and strictly limit
cash advances to transactions where payment by check is impractical;

b. Enforce liquidation of the remaining P37,684.47, as applicable, or comply with the
proper disposition on long outstanding receivables as prescribed in COA Circular
No. 2016-005;

c. Immediately submit the deficiencies noted in the liquidation of P160,539.00 cash
advances detailed in Annex K; and

d. Closely monitor cash advance liquidations by requiring and acknowledging receipt
of Liquidation Reports with proper numbering, verifying supporting documents for
expenses and corresponding refunds, and promptly issuing written demands for
those overdue.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor require strict compliance by all
accountable officers with the prescribed timeline for liquidation of cash advances,
ensure that all cash advances are liquidated as of year-end, issue written demand for
those outstanding beyond the prescribed period to liquidate, and enforce withholding
of any payments due to the accountable officers, if warranted, pursuant to COA Circular
No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant,
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer to:

a. Refrain from making disbursements from the General Fund unless they are duly
supported by approved appropriations in strict compliance with Item 1, Section 4
of PD No. 1445 and adhere to fund classification rules to ensure that future projects,
programs and activities are charged against and paid using their appropriate
funding source; and

b. Strengthen internal controls and enhance coordination among the Budget,
Accounting, and Treasury offices to effectively monitor the availability of funds and
ensure the timely and proper utilization of public resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Officer to identify and propose priority
projects for the 20 percent Development Fund, ensuring that they are ready for
procurement and detailed engineering has been properly conducted by the
Municipal Engineer for infrastructure projects to evaluate their readiness, suitability
and sufficiency of funds;

b. Bids and Awards Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office,
to plan the procurement, taking into consideration the implementation dates
outlined in the approved investment plan to ensure their timely completion within
the year pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No. 2020-01; and

c. Municipal Budget Officer to present in itemized appropriations the
programs/projects/activities per AIP in the Annual Budget pursuant to Article 454(d)
of the IRR of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
immediately submit the lacking supporting documents noted for the 12 DVs totaling
P611,439.35. Otherwise, the transactions shall be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Social Welfare and
Development Officer of the formulation and adoption of a comprehensive policy/guidelines
governing the granting of donations and financial assistance that would clearly define the
eligibility criteria, allowable purposes, documentary requirements, funding limits, approval
processes, and monitoring mechanisms to ensure that limited resources are utilized
economically and for legitimate public purposes.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
submit the official receipt issued by the barangays and SKs for the assistance given
totaling P162,500.00 and P60,000.00, respectively, as shown in Annex O.

Moving forward, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to recognize income in the Trust Fund books using the appropriate income
accounts upon utilization of funds received from national government agencies or any
other sources, whether private or government, that are considered as non-exchange
transactions and record entries relevant to the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to provide allocation for confidential funds in an amount
not exceeding 30 percent of the Peace and Order allocation;

b. Municipal Treasurer to specify in the disbursement voucher the period of expenses
covered by the cash advance as required in Item 6.1.5 of the Joint Circular; and

c. Municipal Accountant together with the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with
certification and documentary requirements of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 of the
COA, DBM, DILG, Governance Commission of the GOCC, and DND on the granting,
utilization and liquidation of all cash advances for Confidential Funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require the Municipal Engineer to incorporate the occupational safety and health
in every project construction cost; and

b. Direct the BAC to require the concerned contractors of ongoing infrastructure
projects to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP in compliance with DOLE DO No. 13,
s. 1998 and Section 37.2.3(f) of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, or refund of the pay
item for occupational health and safety.

We also recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant
require the contractors to submit CSHP duly approved by DOLE prior to signing of
contracts for future projects to be implemented by the Municipality, and deduct the
corresponding CSHP cost from the payment to the contractor, in case the contractor
could not submit the approved CSHP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:

a. The Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to expedite the reconciliation of RPT
and SET records to establish accurate receivable balances;

b. The Municipal Treasurer to prepare a duly certified list of taxpayers, based on the
Real Property Tax Account Register or Taxpayer’s Index Card, detailing the names
of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year, in compliance with
Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and furnish this list to the Municipal
Accountant; and

c. The Municipal Accountant to adopt the Accrual Method of accounting in full by
utilizing the certified list of taxpayers provided by the Municipal Treasurer as the
basis for establishing and recording the correct RPT and SET receivables at the
beginning of the year, and establish SLs per debtor for the Real Property and
Special Education Tax Receivables based on updated balances from the Municipal
Treasurer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-HRMO to:

a. Formulate uniform policy and procedures in engaging and managing outsourced
workers taking into consideration the relevant issuances of oversight agencies; and
internal controls to ensure that they are performing the required task, reporting to the
official station, and bases of assigning wage rates; and

b. Require offices to document their organizational structure presenting the actual
manpower requirement vis-a-vis the number of existing permanent personnel as
basis for requesting/hiring of JOs of the concerned offices.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and
OIC-HRMO to implement rigid monitoring mechanisms, including regular reminders to
JO workers about submission deadlines, and ensure that payroll documents were fully
supported with documentary requirements in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-
001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to
institutionalize the conduct of gender analysis during the planning phase of all PAPs
with the use of appropriate tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming Evaluation
Framework (GMEF), the HGDG, and sex-disaggregated data to support the
identification of gender issues and require the implementing offices of the relevant
documentation for means of verification of the identified PAPs based on results of
gender analysis.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries for the leave
benefit expenses incurred in prior years and record expenses as incurred for
leave credits earned in ensuing years;
b. Municipal Budget Officer to include in the estimate of fund appropriation under
Personnel Services the cost of actual earned leave credits per year as part of the
employee benefits; and
c. Municipal HRM Officer to update the leave cards, adjust balances for the correct
computation of mandatory leave, and address the issues of the attendance
application system affecting leave credits.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Engineer to obtain documents from the DPWH DEO relative
to these projects, for recording as Municipal assets;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize under the appropriate PPE
accounts the four completed building, with aggregate contract cost of
P35,442,647.62 and record as prior year adjustments the relative accumulated
depreciation of P783,421.96; and
c. Ensure necessary coordination with the DPWH DEO on the implementation of
government projects, particularly those that are intended to be subsequently
transferred to the LGU, to enable timely and complete recording of Municipal
assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the ICO-GSO to maintain RegSPI
for each class of issued semi-expendable property, prepare the RSPI to report all
issued semi-expendable property and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant
at least weekly.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
record purchases under the appropriate semi-expendable inventory account and
recognize expense upon issuance to the end-user based on the submitted RSPI by
the GSO and likewise maintain SPLC for each class of semi-expendable property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Recognize the Discount on Special Education Tax and Tax Revenue - Fines and
Penalties - Property Taxes for every collection of Special Education Tax; and
b. Record the advance collections during the year under the Deferred Real Property
Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
record in the separate Cashbooks for Cash – Local Treasury the advance collections
of RPT and SET.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Heads of the Municipal departments and offices, in coordination with the In-
Charge of Office General Service Officer (ICO-GSO), to ensure that complete
documentation for all delivered purchases as of year-end is submitted to the
Municipal Accountant for proper recording of Accounts Payable;
b. Municipal Budget Officer to obligate funds upon perfection of contracts in order to
provide/earmark funds for all commitments or encumbrances incurred as of every
year-end; and
c. BAC to ensure that a Procurement Monitoring Report is prepared and submitted
on a semestral basis, pursuant to Section 12 of the Government Procurement
Reform Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to review
the nature of each disbursement by the Municipality and identify the specific expense
accounts to which they should be attributed in order to accurately and fairly reflect
the balances of the financial statements and ensure compliance with COA
regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take
up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to adjust the
surplus and net book value of the affected PPE accounts totaling P746,253.18 and
submit the same to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly observe
the policies on the recognition of depreciation for PPE prescribed in PAG 3 of IPSAS
17 and Section 104 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
record the subsequent transactions in accordance with the prescribed presentation
and recording in the IPSAS and Revised Chart of Accounts of LGUs, and review and
reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts presented in
the Financial Statements as of year-end.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
issue an official receipt and record the amount in the appropriate cashbook
immediately upon receipt of a check from intra-agency fund transfers, regardless of
whether the check has been cleared in the bank.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer,
Accountant and ICO-GSO to establish each road network under the Municipality’s
jurisdiction with segregated costing per component based on complete description
and cost segregation of road components in time for the cleansing of Property, Plant
and Equipment of the Municipality.

Thereafter, we recommended that the ICO-GSO maintain road networks property
cards and Report on Local Road Network and the Municipal Accountant prepare and
maintain the prescribed property ledger cards for Local Road Networks and disclose
all the required information for the said properties in the Notes to Financial
Statements.

Lastly, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct and update the
inventory count of all the Municipality’s Road Network Systems and accordingly
submit a Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System as of every year-
end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Engineer to incorporate the occupational safety and health
in every project construction cost; and
b. Direct the BAC to require the concerned contractors of completed and currently
ongoing infrastructure projects to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP in
compliance with DOLE DO No. 13, s. 1998 and Section 37.2.3(f) of the Revised IRR
of RA No. 9184, or refund the pay item for occupational health and safety.

We also recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant
require the contractors to submit CSHP duly approved by DOLE prior to signing of
contracts for future projects to be implemented by the Municipality, and deduct the
corresponding CSHP cost from the payment to the contractor, in case the contractor
could not submit the approved CSHP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Provide certification that previous CAs are liquidated as a requisite in granting
cash advances in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2012-001; and
b. Notify the Audit Team of unliquidated cash advances supported with issued
demand letters pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-03 and inform the Municipal
Mayor to facilitate withholding of payment of any money due to the defaulting
accountable officer as stipulated by COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to account only those cash advances granted to disbursing
officers under the Advances accounts as prescribed in the Chart of Accounts and
directly record as expenses those which are not paid/disbursed in cash; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to stop granting cash advance in paying for individual claims
and settle them through check payable to the claimants.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. ICO-MDRRMO to strictly refrain from charging against the LDRRMF the subject
disbursements and similar charges that are not related to DRRM activities as
provided in NDDRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC JMC No. 2014-01; and
b. BAC Chairman to ensure that all procurement items for the implementation of
programs, projects, and activities are incorporated and are clearly identified in the
APP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management (MDRRM) Officer to provide and propose to the MDRRM
Council utilization plans for the P4,138,483.56 unexpended balance of prior years’
LDRRMF, incorporate the same in the LDRRMFIP and ensure that such plan is
updated with the unexpended balance recorded by the Municipal Accountant in the
Trust Fund books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Officer in-Charge-Municipal Planning and Development Officer (ICO-MPDO) to
identify and propose priority projects for the 20 percent Development Fund,
ensuring that they are ready for procurement and detailed engineering has been
properly conducted by the Municipal Engineer for infrastructure projects to
evaluate their readiness; and
b. Bids and Awards Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering
Office, to plan the procurement, taking into consideration the implementation
dates outlined in the approved investment plan to ensure their timely completion
within the year pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No. 2020-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Establish and maintain SLs by debtor/creditor for the asset and liability accounts,
and ledger cards for PPE accounts; and
b. Establish SLs per debtor for the Real Property and Special Education Tax
Receivables based on updated balances from the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the ICO-HRMO to:
a. Formulate uniform policy and procedures in engaging and managing outsourced
workers taking into consideration the relevant issuances of oversight agencies;
and internal controls to ensure that they are performing the required task,
reporting to the official station, and bases of assigning wage rates; and
b. Require the requesting office to submit justification for the hiring of JOs based on
the actual manpower requirement vis-a-vis the number of existing permanent
personnel of the concerned offices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System to
require each office to identify the expenses to be incurred for every GAD activity and
distinguish the programs and corresponding expense items upon which they are to
be charged, for review of the Municipal Budget Office, to ensure that the GAD Plan
and Budget is directly traceable to the Annual Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee, the
Municipal Accountant and General Services Office (GSO) to conduct the one-time
cleansing of account following the guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and submit to the Audit Team the proposed timeline
for the immediate completion thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Review the noted observations regarding depreciation, effect the necessary
adjustments and submit copies of the adjusting journal entries to the audit team
for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly observe the policies on the recognition
of depreciation for PPE prescribed in PAG 3 and 6 of IPSAS 17 and Section 14 of
COA Circular No. 2015-008; and

b. Record the advance collections during the year and in the succeeding years under
the Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to
expedite the immediate implementation of the project with an appropriation of
P600,000.00; and

b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Budget
Officer to plan, budget and utilize the 20 percent Development for
Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) which are aligned to the provisions of JMC No. 1
s. 2020 specially for the non-allowable expenses to be charged therein. Henceforth,
stop the practice of charging administrative expenditures and personnel benefits to
the 20 percent Development Fund.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:

a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to
implement the programs, projects and activities under Current, Continuing
Appropriation and Trust Liability in the succeeding years; and

b. Municipal Planning and Development Officer in coordination with the MDRRMO to
carefully plan the PPAs under the LDRRMF that will be included in the Annual
Budget. Ensure that the identified PPAs are the priority mitigation, preparedness
and response activities of the Municipality to lessen the frequent changes in the
Annual Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Accountable Officers to facilitate the renewal of fidelity bonds, before or 30 days
prior to the expiration of the present bond coverage while the accountable public
officer is holding such position for which he was bonded in compliance with
Treasury Circular No. 02-2019;

b. Municipal Accountant to deny the request for additional cash advance if the same
will exceed their maximum accountability, otherwise, consider increasing the
fidelity bond to sufficiently cover the total daily accountabilities in accordance
with the schedule provided under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019; and

c. Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances will only be granted to
accountable officers with no previous unliquidated cash advances and with the
necessary certification in the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) that previous cash
advances had been liquidated.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure that the project
costing P5,000,000.00 for the Concreting of Farm to Market Road at Barangay
Guinhawa Phase II is immediately implemented; and

b. Municipal Local Finance Committee to prepare quarterly reports on fund
utilization and status of program/project implementation using the prescribed
format (Annex C) in the succeeding quarters. Post the quarterly reports in at least
three conspicuous public places and on the LGU’s website. Consequently, send
written notices to Secretary of DBM, Secretary of DILG, Chairperson of the NTF-
ELCAC, coursed through the NTF-ELCAC Vice Chairperson and RTF-ELCAC
concerned, Speaker of the House of Representatives, President of the Senate of
the Philippines, and the Chairpersons of the House Committee on Appropriations
and Senate Committee on Finance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting HRMO to:

a. Formulate a uniform policy and updated organizational structure relative to the
hiring/rehiring of JO workers to establish a clear expectation and protect the rights of
both the JO personnel and the Municipal Government. Henceforth, ensure that the
hiring of JOs is in accordance with Sections 76 and 77 of RA No. 7160 and CSC MC
No. 17, series of 2002; and

b. Establish a more stringent process for reviewing the DTR and accomplishment
report, as basis for the payment of wages to JO personnel to ensure that JO
personnel are paid accurately based on actual days worked and reported
accomplishments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the members of the Inventory Committee and the MGSO to conduct a
comprehensive physical count and inspection of all PPE items to fully verify their
existence, and accurately incorporate the results into the RPCPPE, ensuring that
only those PPE duly accounted for during the inventory count are included in the
report;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accounting Office and the MGSO to address any deficiencies
that may arise out of the conduct of complete inventory-taking of all PPE items
owned by the Municipality, and to maintain the required Property Cards/
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment in their custody, for easy reference
during the conduct of inventory-taking; and
c. Adopt and fully implement the one-time cleansing procedures of PPE accounts
incorporated into the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to properly
address the missing/unaccounted/undocumented PPE items that have been in the
books for several years, for a valid reporting and presentation of PPE accounts
balances that are verifiable, accurate and in existence, thus, attain fairness of
presentation of all PPE accounts in the Financial Statements of the Municipality at
every reporting period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to review and
reconcile their records on the total assessed value of taxable real properties to
correctly set up the RPT and SET Receivable in the books of accounts; and
b. Municipal Accountant to make appropriate adjusting entries in the books of Special
Education Funds to record the SET Receivable accounts in CY 2024, including that
of the affected deferred accounts, pursuant to Sections 19 and 20 of the NGAS
Manual for LGUs, Volume I, so as to reflect reliable and accurate account balances
and ensure fairness of presentation in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to draw
the necessary correcting entries and ensure accuracy of all information recorded in the
books of accounts so as to avoid inconsistencies in the data being presented in their
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
conduct a thorough verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of all dormant
accounts or those accounts that have been non-moving for ten years or more, and
adopt the procedures for the proper disposition of dormant accounts pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2023-008 to achieve a fairly presented accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to adjust all valid reconciling items determined during the
preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis for an
accurate account balances in the financial statements; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the concerned payees of the stale
checks and require submission of appropriate documents so as to facilitate
issuance of new checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer/Acting Municipal Engineer to conscientiously enforce all pre-
planning activities prior to the preparation of detailed engineering design of every
infrastructure projects so to address any issues that may affect on the viability of the
projects, before funds are allocated, thus timely completion of these projects can be
achieved within the timeframe set, paving the way for immediate use by the projects’
intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Disposal Committee and the
OIC-Municipal General Services Officer to prioritize the disposal of unserviceable
properties, in accordance with the COA – DBM JC No. 2024-1 to avoid further
deterioration leading to lesser resale value, and thus eliminate the continuing storage
cost for the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Human Resource Officer
to strictly adopt the required monthly net take-home pay of employees of not lower
than P5,000.00, pursuant with the directive embodied in the 2024 GAA, so as to
preserve the employees’ morale and work performance. If the total authorized
deductions shall reduce net take-home pay to less than P5,000.00, deductions should
be made in accordance with the order of preference under Section 56 of 2024 GAA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to:
a. Submit the Annual GPB to the PPDO, from PPDO to DILG Provincial Office and
secure the Certificate of Endorsement from the DILG Provincial Office, where such
certification would truly establish the relevance of the gender issues identified and
gender-responsiveness of the PPAs indicated in the GPB pursuant to Section 5.0 of
the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; and
b. Formulate GAD Plan and Budget based on an updated GAD Database, to strengthen
the planning and budgeting processes for GAD, and ensure that planned PPAs
would address the identified gender issues and that objectives, performance
indicators and targets are well-formulated to facilitate monitoring and evaluation for
the attainment of GAD objectives.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts and instruct the
Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Unit to follow the procedural
guidelines on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

b. Direct the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the GSO, to prepare the
Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing, at the least, the specific
assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule
specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to
the targeted completion of the physical inventory and submit the PIP to the COA
Audit Team at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking
activities;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
recognize the utilization of transfers, assistance and subsidies in the appropriate asset
or expense accounts, with a corollary entry recording the related revenue in the Trust
Fund books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
account for cash advances granted to disbursing officers under the appropriate
Advances accounts, as prescribed in the Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to make
the necessary adjustments to the books of accounts to ensure that RPT and SET
income are recognized in the appropriate period when earned, and advance collections
during the year are recorded as liability under the Deferred RPT and Deferred SET
accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer to avoid
granting cash advances near the end of year wherein days are limited to fully serve
its purpose and to liquidate. Otherwise, if necessary, instead of cash advance,
direct the Municipal Accountant to accrue and recognize the payable amounts due
to the payees upon presentation of complete supporting documents;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to:
    i. Maintain subsidiary ledgers per AO, record all grants and liquidations of cash
    advances therein, and conduct periodic reconciliations to ensure that SL
    balances tally with control accounts in the GL;
    ii. Strictly monitor the submission of liquidation reports to ensure timely
    accounting thereof to avoid accumulation of outstanding cash advances;
    iii. Identify the officers and employees accountable for the beginning balance of
    the Advances for Payroll and Due from Officers and Employees accounts
    amounting to P1,220,545.77 and P1,167,844.17, respectively, and immediately
    cause the liquidation or settlement of any unliquidated cash advances; and
    iv. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries for identified errors in recording
    totaling P431,108.82.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Observe the disclosure requirements in the preparation of the financial statements
in accordance with the IPSASs and COA Circular No. 2016-004 to assist users in
understanding the financial statements and to provide them with information
relevant to decision-making; and

b. Reconcile the discrepancies noted in the financial statements and effect the
necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Maintain complete GLs and SLs for all asset, liability, income and expense accounts
under the SEF and TF, in accordance with the MNGAS for LGUs; and

b. Review the accuracy of balances reflected in the GLs/SLs on a monthly basis, to
ensure that financial reports accurately reflect the true financial position, condition
and results of operations of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and
Acting Municipal Treasurer to undertake the immediate reconciliation of RPT and SET
receivables and make the necessary adjustments to their respective records to avoid
misstatement of RPT/SET Receivables and to serve as basis for the collection of taxes
in ensuing years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Development Council to assess the feasibility of implementing the
unimplemented PPAs with appropriations of P1,799,500.00. Otherwise, consider re-
allocating the funds to prioritize development projects that are well-planned,
procurement-and-implementation-ready, and aligned with the current needs of the
constituents, pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020; and

b. Municipal Engineer to expedite the implementation of ongoing project worth
P500,000.00 funded under the 20 percent DF.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:

a. MDRRMO to incorporate all available LDRRM Funds in the LDRRMFIP for allocation
to disaster preparedness and mitigation PPAs of the Municipality, including the
unexpended QRF and DRRM-MOOE of the previous years which were transferred
to the Special Trust Fund;

b. Concerned officials to coordinate with the donors and request approval to re-
allocate cash donations received for Taal Volcano eruption totaling P446,798.52 for
the implementation of other disaster-related PPAs of the Municipality. Otherwise,
facilitate the return of the unutilized cash donations to the donor or the Bureau of
Treasury, depending on the terms of the agreement with the donor; and

c. Municipal Accountant and MDRRMO to conduct periodic reconciliation of the
reported appropriation, utilization and balance of LDRRMF and make the necessary
adjustments to their respective records.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Assessor and City Accountant to submit lacking documentary requirements to support the payment of the purchased real properties and observe the timely submission of contracts as prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 2012-001 and the IRR of RA No. 10752.
observations.managementAction: The noted lacking documentary requirements were substantially complied and submitted by the Offices of the City Assessor and City Accountant to the Audit Team on April 8, 2024, June 27, 2024, and January 23, and 31, 2025

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the: a. City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) to submit within 60 days from receipt thereof, the City’s plan for the utilization and implementation of the unutilized balance of 20% DF; and b. CPDO and City Budget Officer to judiciously appropriate the savings from completed project to avoid unutilized appropriations to attain optimal utilization of the fund; and to further conduct thorough and careful study of priority projects to ensure implementation throughout a given year.
observations.managementAction: The City’s plan for the utilization and implementation of the unutilized balance of 20% DF as of December 31, 2023 was submitted to the Audit Team on May 16, 2024. Out of the unutilized balance, the CPDO had included the unutilized balances as new source of funds for the new priority projects identified by City officials and the amount of P82,468,947.81 under the 20% DF was included in the Supplemental Budget No. 1 for CY 2024 as a new source of funds for the new priority projects identified by the City officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM) Officer to: a. Judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of the PPAs and to resolve the issues hounding implementation of PPAs in order to achieve the goal/ target of the City regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management; and b. Ensure that all available fund for the LDRRM has a corresponding PPAs that will be included in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP).
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management Reiterated with modification in Observation and Recommendation No. 4, Pages Nos. 61 to 64. Resolution No. 22-523 s. 2024, a resolution approving the plans and projects to be charged against the CY 2024 LDRRMFIP funded of Special Trust Fund totaling P136,064,073.23 was approved on September 9, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Assessor to evaluate the appraised values of properties under the jurisdiction of the City based on prevailing market prices and propose the necessary revisions on the City Code and every three years thereafter, to comply with the provisions of Sections 201 and 219 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management
observations.reason: In compliance with RA No. 12001 or the Real Property Valuation and Assessment Reform Act, which took effect last July 5, 2024, mandating the update of the Schedule of Market Values within two years from the effectivity of the said Act, the initial planning and data gathering is being done and the timeline of activities was already prepared for the proper evaluation and appraisal of 188,544 real properties under the jurisdiction of the City Government of Batangas.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer in coordination with the CPDO to: a. Conscientiously carry out detailed engineering addressing any issues on the revised viability of the project sites to ensure that projects are implementation-ready before they are posted for bidding; and b. Thoroughly review and evaluate the Program of Works to ensure that project designs and revised estimates are approved and properly prepared and suit the needs of end-users and that all phases of the projects are covered to minimize time extensions.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management
observations.reason: In order to fast track completion, Notices of Award and Contract Agreements were issued for pending projects and implementation was diligently monitored by the Office of the City Engineer. The projects were revised and approved by the Regional Director on January 15, 2025 and were excluded in the ensuing year report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20% DF by including approved development projects in the succeeding period that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management
observations.reason: The projects were revised and approved by the Regional Director on January 15, 2025 and were excluded in the ensuing year report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require concerned City Accountant to strictly follow the guidelines and procedures in the write-off of dormant accounts as required under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant is still in the process of preparing the documents for the request for write-off of dormant receivable accounts.
observations.reason: Unavailability of records and documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the LDRRM Council headed by the City Mayor to evaluate and review the LDRRMFIP with emphasis on the proper identification and selection of specific affected areas of priority PPAs to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/ target of the City regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management; and ensure that unexpended balances forwarded to the LDRRMFIP are properly identified and included.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management
observations.reason: Out of the Continuing Appropriations from previous years of the LDRRMF, the amount of P36,152,813.00 was utilized and the remaining balance of P3,663,228.57 pertains to the savings from the implemented PPAs. Likewise, P698,400.00 was utilized during the year from the financial assistance received amounting to P703,327.00 at the beginning of the year 2024, leaving a balance of P4,927.00 as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Planning and Development Coordinator and City Budget Office to judiciously appropriate budget for identified developmental projects to avoid unutilized appropriations to attain optimal utilization of the fund; and further instructed the City Planning and Development Coordinator to make a thorough and careful study of the priority projects to ensure their implementation throughout a given year.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the management
observations.reason: The projects were revised and approved by the Regional Director on January 15, 2025 and were excluded in the ensuing year report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make an adjusting entry for the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums paid last April, 2024 as of the latest cut-off date and submit to the Audit Team the Journal Entry Voucher for appropriate evaluation; and
b. Ensure that at every year-end, adjusting entries will be prepared to recognize the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums paid to reflect reliable balances of the affected accounts.
observations.managementAction: An adjusting entry was prepared last April, 2024 under JEV No. 10024040761 to recognize the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums as of April 30, 2024. As of December 31, 2024, the Municipal Accountant recognize the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums paid.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the accountable officers to provide justification on the late liquidation of cash advances mentioned above. Henceforth, strictly adhere to Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on timely liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to provide justification/explanation on the findings noted and the corresponding actions taken necessary to correct such deficiency and submit to the Audit Team the original plan for further evaluation.
observations.managementAction: After re-evaluation of the TAS, the Management was required to submit the original plan for further evaluation. The documents were forwarded by the Audit Team on July 9, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory and Disposal Committee, in cooperation with all offices concerned, to prioritize the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPEs, including reconciliation of accounting records versus physical count pursuant to the provisions and guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary accounting entries to recognize unrecorded lots after the conduct of comprehensive reconciliation of lands owned by the Municipality. Also, to take the proper identification and acquisition costs of the concerned parcels of land with noted deficiencies.
observations.managementAction: The 131 parcels of land owned by the Municipality with market values of P444,099,350.90 are recorded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory and Disposal Committee, in cooperation with all offices concerned, to prioritize the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPEs, including reconciliation of accounting records versus physical count pursuant to the provisions and guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended and the Management agreed to recognize the receivables of the noted unpaid rentals supported by individual subsidiary ledgers. Prospectively, we recommended to accrue the monthly receivables and income from rentals and establish individual subsidiary ledgers for each stallholder and reconcile unpaid balances with the reports of the Market Administration as of any reporting period. Finally, we recommended and the Sangguniang Bayan agreed to make clarification/ amendments on the rates of market rental fee provided in Section 12 of Revised Market Code of 2020 and Section 5A.01 of Article A of Chapter 5 of Vol. II of Revised Revenue Code of Bauan 2020.
observations.managementAction: The Revenue Code was revisited and to make the necessary amendments/ on the imposition of market stall rental amount.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to Require the Municipal Treasurer and BPLO to verify, assess and validate the reported under collection totaling P708,688.76 and consequently the collection/ payment that the business entities will be required to collect in the ensuing year. The absence of documented action or information to counter the under collection shall mean the issuance of the corresponding Notice of Charge.
observations.managementAction: The concerned business entities were informed, thru written communication, that there will be adjustment in the amount to be collected in the ensuing years or the amount will be deducted on payments due to them in case of suppliers/ contractors.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to regularly and the bank account intact all collections with AGDB daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance COA Memorandum No. 2013-004.
observations.managementAction: The amount of P9,306,000.00 was credited to bank account after next working day because the same was deposited late from clearing cut-off time of the bank. The Acting Municipal Treasurer ensures to deposit regularly and intact all collections with AGDB daily or not later than the next banking day.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Department Heads to refrain from assigning JO personnel to perform collection functions innate to regular accountable officers of the Municipality such as Revenue Collection Clerk/Officer as such entail responsibility and accountability for money and property.
observations.managementAction: Starting June 2024, only regular and bonded employees were allowed to perform collection functions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in hiring of Job Order (JO) employees have established require the Head of the HRMO to come up with concrete policy guidelines relative to the observed hiring/rehiring of JO personnel to establish a clear expectation and protect the rights of both the employees and the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Policies on the hiring of Job Order employees have been improved. Proper documentation is now being established.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Tourism Officer to: a. Stop the use of improvised tickets and use the AF No. 51 or detailed Reports of Collection (RCD) by the collectors, contrary to Sections 130(c) and 305(c) of the Local Government Code and Section 29 of New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGU), Volume I, hence, manifests weakness in internal control specifically as to responsibility and accountability for money and property and exposing government funds to possible risk of loss if continuously pursued to the disadvantage of the Municipality.; b. Refrain from making delegations of collecting functions to unauthorized person; c. Require the collectors to accomplish the daily RCD; and d. Instruct the collectors to remit the collections to the Municipal Treasurer on a daily basis or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00.
