We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to check and verify the accuracy of the generated depreciation expenses in succeeding years and prepare manual lapsing schedule, if necessary, and stop the practice of providing residual value for the depreciable component of the road network systems pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record the subsequent transactions in accordance with the prescribed Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, review and reconcile the Subsidy from Other Funds and Subsidy to Other Funds accounts presented in the Financial Statements as of year-end pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reclassify the remaining balance to appropriate income accounts and record the subsequent 20 percent share of the City from the Fire Code Fees as Permit Fees and shares from the fees collected from cutting coconut trees remitted by the PCA as Share from National Wealth in the General Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009, RA No. 9514 and BLGF MC No. 03-01-2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Administrator in coordination with the City Accountant, to set cut-off date on the submission of supporting documents for Accounts Payable at year-end to the Office of the City Accountant and monitor compliance by all Department Heads of the City; and
b. City Accountant to recognize in the books the taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of Accounts Payable starting CY 2024 for goods received or services rendered, and construction/infrastructure projects fully completed, inspected and accepted.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the:
a. City Personnel Officer and City Budget Officer to prepare Plantilla of Casual Personnel and Casual Appointment using the daily wage rate computed by dividing the corresponding monthly salary rate in the salary schedule by 22 working days pursuant to RA No. 11466 and DBM LBC No. 149;
b. City Budget Officer to provide sufficient appropriation for the salaries and wages and benefits of all casual employees based on the salary schedule provided under RA No. 11466 and submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval; and
c. City Personnel Officer, City Budget Officer, City Accountant, City Treasurer and City Administrator to pay the salaries and wages of all casual employees at uniform percentage rates of the salary schedule similar to the regular employees of the City Government pursuant to RA No. 11466.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
a. City General Services Officer to prepare the PIF by extracting the data from the RPCPPE and submit to Audit Team and GIF of the GSIS which will be used as a basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the City’s insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002;
b. City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; and
c. City General Services Officer to insure with the GIF of the GSIS all of the City’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that the CDRRMC, headed by the City Mayor, to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs, and monitor the full implementation of planned PPAs starting CY 2024.

We recommended the City Development Council, headed by the City Mayor to assess the feasibility of the implementation and responsiveness to the current needs of the constituents of those unimplemented projects totaling P40,951,605.76 and include development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No. 2020-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Personnel Officer, City Treasurer and City Accountant to revisit the process of withholding and remittances of premium contributions and loan amortizations due to PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG and GSIS, and address the causes of delay in the submission of the required documents to the Office of the City Accountant;
b. City Accountant and City Treasurer to remit all PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG and GSIS premium contributions and loans amortizations of all officials and employees of the City within the prescribed period; and
c. City’s Principal AAO and ERF Officer to immediately reconcile and coordinate with the GSIS the remaining balances of Due to GSIS account.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) to provide sufficient MOVs for the HGDG checklist of the attributed programs or projects presented in the GPB in CY 2024 and subsequent years.

We recommended that the City Mayor prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing
of PPE balances as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 taking strict
consideration of the guidelines and procedures on the preliminary activities,
inventory taking, reconciliation, and disposition and derecognition of non-
existing/missing items in order to reconcile the variance between accounting and
property records and come up with accurate and updated PPELCs.

Further, we recommended that the City Mayor require the Disposal Committee to
undertake the disposal of the unserviceable PPEs to prevent further deterioration
and reduction of economic value of the properties brought about by their
continuous exposure to natural elements.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Accountant to: (i) immediately require the submission of documents for all
outstanding payables and validate the claims based on valid and complete
supporting documents and/or proofs of the actual delivery of goods and
services. Otherwise, effect necessary adjustments/reversal in the books to
conform with regulations; (ii) strictly record claims as accounts payable only
and when complete supporting documents are submitted to substantiate their
validity; and (iii) ensure that reversals/adjustments of Accounts Payable
account are made only for errors or omissions, and not for cancellation of
previously recorded liabilities that were found invalid;

b. City Budget Officer to refrain from approving obligation requests unless
otherwise a contract has already been perfected; and

c. General Services Officer to strictly ensure that contracts are submitted for
obligation requests, while delivery receipts, sales invoices and Inspection and
Acceptance Reports (IARs) along with the procurement documents are
submitted in support of recording claims by the City Accountant.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to

a. reclassify the remaining balance of Due to NGAs of P14,417,105.17 to the
appropriate income accounts; and

b. record the subsequent receipt of shares from the BFR, CHED and PCSO as
Permit Fees, School Fees and Share from PCSO, respectively, in the General
Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:

a. Prepare Journal Entry Voucher to properly classify the pathways totaling
P5,304,527.35 into the Other Infrastructure Assets account and its
corresponding accumulated depreciation totaling P3,345,001.33 and removing
the same in the Road Networks account; and

b. Prepare Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the Road Networks account and its
corresponding Accumulated Depreciation  Road Networks account by
P17,067,737.54 and stop the practice of providing residual value for the
depreciable component of the road network systems in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2015-008.

Henceforth, we recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to
comply with the COA Circular No. 2015-008 in accounting and reporting local road
networks.

In view thereof, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant
to adjust and withhold taxes from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date
representing procured goods and services, and apply this practice prospectively
in succeeding accounting periods pursuant to BIR MC No. 10-2018.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:

a. the City Treasurer to continuously identify the corresponding payors and the
nature of these payments for proper recognition of income; and

b. the City Accountant to record the remaining unidentified receipts through a
debit to Cash in Bank and credit to Trust Liability account and recognize as
income when accounted.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the discontinuance of assigning Job
Order staffs as accountable officers particularly as cashier/collector to protect the
interest of the City Government against loss thru theft or pilferages, consider
appointing additional permanent collector positions in the City Public Market.

We also recommended that the City Market Supervisor require the following:

a. Preparation and use of RCDs by all collectors and liquidating officers in the form
prescribed by the LTOM;

b. Proper monitoring of accountable forms issued to each collector thru the
monthly preparation of the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms;

c. Issuance of distinct booklets of accountable forms/ORs to each collector
prohibiting sharing of forms; and

d. Use and maintenance of individual cashbooks by the liquidating
officers/cashiers as prescribed by the LTOM.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to comply strictly with
the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, whereby, no
granting of additional cash advance shall be made unless previous cash advances
have been settled.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CHRMO, in coordination with
concerned Department Heads to:

a. Re-evaluate its existing organizational structure based from organization charts
of each department to easily determine the need number of regular plantilla and
outsourced workers;

b. Formulate uniform policy and procedures in engaging and managing
outsourced workers taking into consideration the relevant issuances of
oversight agencies; and internal controls to ensure that they are performing the
required task, reporting to the official station, and bases of assigning wage
rates; and

c. Provide a Statement of Duties and Responsibilities to JO workers to clearly
identify their responsibilities and perform their duties efficiently.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CHRMO to:

a. Generate and maintain copies in the CHRMO of finalized and approved DTRs of
JOs as basis in the preparation of the payroll; and

b. Require the JOs to submit accomplishment reports every pay period as support to
the paid payroll in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Budget Officer to require Project Procurement Management Plans (PPMPs)
indicating all goods to be procured by each office for their programs or daily
operations for thorough evaluation in terms of amount and coherence with the
proposed budget of the City Government; and

b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) consolidate the evaluated PPMPs into an
indicative APP and recommend appropriate modalities subject to approval of the
City Mayor.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. General Services Officer to consolidate PRs of goods alike and similar in nature
and refer all PRs to the BAC for adoption and conduct of appropriate
procurement modality; and

b. BAC, as one of its functions provided in Section 12.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A.
No. 9184, to recommend adoption and undertake alternative modalities of
procurement, if and when applicable, including shopping and small value
procurement.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer and the concerned
Project Engineers to ensure that the quantities are reasonably estimated to avoid
excessive expenditures in order to conform with COA Circular No. 2012-003.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the City Mayor require the
concerned six City Government Department Heads and the City Vice-Mayor for the
five Sanggunian Panlungsod Members to refund the expenses incurred totaling
P828,088.50 for the trip to Japan.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the refund of the remaining
excess travel expenses of P5,788.00 for the trip to Korea.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. City Planning and Development Office (CPDO) and City engineering Office (CEO)
to identify and propose priority projects under the 20% Development Fund that are
ready for procurement and implementation;

b. BAC, in coordination with the CEO, to undertake procurement as planned taking
into consideration the underlying circumstances of the sites involved aiming for
their timely completion within the year pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG JMC No.
2020-01; and

c. City Budget Officer to obligate funds upon perfection of contracts in order to
provide/earmark funds for all commitments or encumbrances incurred as of every
year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRM Officer to:

a. Re-evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under
Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be
implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-
related PPAs; and

b. Regularly review and update the LDRRMFIP to include the PPAs appropriated
with continuing appropriations and those to be funded from all the prior years’
unexpended balances of the LDRRMF in the Trust Fund;

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build
their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender
mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting,
and GAD-related tools among others; and

b. Require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance
with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

In order to ensure that the GAD Plan and Budget is incorporated in the Annual
Budget, we recommended that GAD Focal Point System require each office to
identify the expenses to be incurred for every GAD activity and distinguish the
programs or expense items upon which they are to be charged subject to review of
the City Budget Office.

We also recommended that the City Budget Officer account for the GAD budget and
its utilization by applying responsibility costing, or devise a separate monitoring for
all allotments attributed to GAD as well as obligations charged therefrom. Such
data shall be linked to or be the source of utilization amounts reported in the GAD
Accomplishment Report.

Therefore, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Budget Officer to
appropriate funds for property insurance of the City Government and the General
Services Officer to prepare the Property Inventory Form and cause the appraisal of the
properties, and submit the form for application of insurance with GSIS to protect the
City Government thru indemnification in cases of damage or losses due to fortuitous
events.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Instruct the GSO and the Accounting Unit to perform the preliminary activities of the one-time cleansing and reconcile their records thereof;
b. Plan/strategize on how the Municipality would apply the one-time cleansing and incorporate it in the PIP to be submitted to the Audit Team at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled inventory-taking activities; and
c. Immediately start the application of one-time cleansing of the PPEs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make an adjusting entry for the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums paid last CY 2023 as of the latest cut-off date and submit to the Audit Team the Journal Entry Voucher for appropriate evaluation; and
b. Ensure that at every year-end, adjusting entries will be prepared to recognize the unexpired portion of the insurance premiums paid to reflect reliable balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the accountable officers to provide justification on the late liquidation of cash advances mentioned above. Henceforth, strictly adhere to Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on timely liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately conduct confirmation among identified source agencies of the unexpended and unremitted balances and facilitate the return thereof. Otherwise, if there is still a need, the Municipality may make a written representation to the source agencies to request the use of money for other projects or purpose; and
b. Municipal Accountant ensure that all subsequent unused balance of funds received shall be returned immediately after the completion of the project pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties; and
b. Review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors and ensure that these taxes are remitted on the prescribed period to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council headed by the Municipal Mayor to evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper identification and selection of specific priority PPAs and include in the plan all PPAs to be charged against the current, continuing and unexpended appropriation of previous years to facilitate monitoring and accelerate implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to provide justification/explanation on the findings noted and the corresponding actions taken necessary to correct such deficiency and submit to the Audit Team for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly comply with Annex A of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Instruct the Inventory Committee and the Accounting Unit to perform the
preliminary activities of the one-time cleansing and reconcile their records thereof;

b. Plan/strategize on how the Municipality would apply the one-time cleansing and
incorporate it in the PIP to be submitted at least 10 calendar days before the
scheduled inventory-taking activities; and

c. Immediately start the application of One-time cleansing of the PPEs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit
regularly and intact all collections with AGDB daily or not later than the next banking
day in compliance COA Memorandum No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to coordinate
with the contractors to refund the cost deficiencies of P411,470.47, otherwise, a Notice
of Disallowance will be issued. Henceforth, it is recommended that the Municipal
Engineer prepare accurate project cost estimates in the future projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and other
concerned Department Heads to refrain from assigning JO personnel to perform
collection functions innate to regular accountable officers of the Municipality such as
Revenue Collection Clerk/Officer as such entail responsibility and accountability for
money and property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Sangguniang
Bayan, to require the Head of the Human Resource Management Office to come up
with concrete policy guidelines relative to the hiring/rehiring of JO personnel to
establish a clear expectation and protect the rights of both the employees and the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Tourism Officer to:

a. Stop the use of improvised tickets and use the AF No. 51 or cash tickets for the
collection of TEF and light house fees;

b. Refrain from making delegations of collecting functions to unauthorized person;

c. Require the collectors to accomplish the daily RCD; and

d. Instruct the collectors to remit the collections to the Municipal Treasurer on a daily
basis or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee to require the GFPS and the Technical Working Group to:

a. Prioritize gender mainstreaming efforts in GAD planning and budgeting by using
existing gender analysis tools such as the HGDG in the identification, design,
implementation, management and monitoring and evaluation stages of the various
PPAs to determine the extent of gender-responsiveness and amount to be
attributed to the GAD budget;

b. Develop a GAD Database or Sex-Disaggregated Data for proper utilization in the
planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation of the Municipality, as
well as proper charging of the object of expenditures on actual accomplishments;
and

c. Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and conduct regular
coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that programs are
undertaken within the target period. Submit justification why said GAD PPAs were
not implemented as of year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the:
a. Property and Accounting Units to immediately commence the reconciliation
of PPE balances and ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly
recorded in their respective records;
b. Inventory Committee to re-evaluate the noted inconsistencies in the adoption of
the One-Time Cleansing and take note of those procedures that can be re-applied
or make corrective actions like the tagging of the counted PPEs with new
property stickers; and
c. Inventory Committee to expedite the remaining procedures in application of
one-time cleansing of the PPEs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payee within 15 days
from receipt thereof the presentation and submission of stale checks as
requirement prior to issuance of the replacement check and to practice the
sending of written notice to the payee at least one month before a check becomes
stale;
b. Municipal Accountant to restore the amount to Cash in Bank and to set up the
related accounts payable accounts for the cancellation of the checks for the fair
presentation of the related accounts in the financial statements; and
c. Municipal Accountant to adjust all valid reconciling items determined during
the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis
for accurate account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Coordinate with the implementing units to assess the needs for those
unexpended fund balances from NGAs and LGUs. Should the funds be proven no
longer needed, remit immediately to the SA, but if still needed, request authority to
use the fund for other related projects of the Municipality. Revert to the GF book the
counterpart funds of the completed projects in the TF;
b. Submit within 10 days from receipt hereof a justification on the noted delays in
the implementation of two financial grants from DA and LGSF and a progress
report on the status of the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program;
and
c. Prepare the appropriate adjusting journal entries for the transfer of the grants and
donations recognized in the GF book and submit to the Audit Team the
corresponding Journal Entry Voucher within 10 days from receipt hereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer that the
fidelity bond of the Collecting Officers be increased to an amount sufficient to cover
their maximum accountability, in accordance with the Revised Schedule of bonds
provided by the Bureau of Treasury under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 to protect the
interest of the Municipality in case of loss resulting from unlawful use and negligence
in the safekeeping of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to
appropriate funds for property insurance of the Municipality subject to its financial
capacity and the Acting GSO to prepare the Property Inventory Form and cause the
appraisal of the properties and submit them for application of insurance with GSIS to
protect the Management thru indemnification in cases of damage or losses due to
fortuitous events.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the:
a. Local Development Council (LDC) to assess the viability of the identified PPAs
in the planning stage to ensure that only those projects which are procurement
and implementation ready will be given budget allocation;
b. Municipal Engineering Office (MEO) to perform in-depth planning and detailed
engineering including thorough site inspections to anticipate and consider
interruptions that may probably arise during implementation of the projects to
ensure the optimal and timely implementation of the projects to be funded by
the 20% DF; and
c. LDC, MEO and Municipal Planning and Development Office to immediately
look into the unimplemented projects under the 20% DF and take appropriate
actions on the causes noted of their non-implementation. Formulate/develop
specific strategies/ solutions to effectively implement development projects.
Consider formulating a timetable within which to complete the unimplemented
projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Evaluate the necessity of appropriating Confidential Funds to the Annual Budget
considering the continual non-utilization of such for an extended period of time; and
b. If found necessary, assign a Project Accountable Officer to focus on the
preparation of the Physical and Financial Plan indicating the proposed amount
allocated for each program to be submitted to the Municipal Budget Officer as
support to the allocations of Confidential Funds pursuant to Section 4.2 of
COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point System
Technical Working Group to:
a. Prepare GAD Agenda as soon as possible and strictly comply with the
provisions on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to ensure that the PPAs
indicated in the annual GPB are aligned with it or with the identified gender
issues to be addressed during the three-year term of the LCE’s leadership; and
b. Submit the GPB and GAD Accomplishment Report within the prescribed period
to facilitate evaluation and validation of the GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Property Unit and Accounting Unit to walk through the Preliminary activities and prepare their respective reports and documents;

b. Property Unit to prepare the inventory working papers and submit them to the Inventory Committee as basis in preparing the PIP;

c. Inventory Committee to prepare a feasible and final PIP, duly approved by the Head of the Agency, and submit a copy COA Audit Team at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory activities; and

d. Head of the Inventory Committee to ensure that the inventory-taking activities are executed in accordance with the approved PIP.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the designated members of the Inventory Committee are diligent and will devote themselves to complete the inventory taking of all the properties rightfully belonging to the Municipality.

Further, we recommend to revisit COA Circular No. 2020-006 and adopt the procedural guidelines outlined thereat to establish reliable PPE balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to cause the settlement of the subject payable accounts, otherwise initiate reversion thereof to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, with the concurrence of the Sangguniang Bayan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:

a. Stop the grant of additional cash advances to the Municipal officials and employees unless the previous cash advance has been fully liquidated in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002;

b. Furnish a copy of the authority given by the Local Chief Executive to the Municipal employees who are performing disbursing functions as basis in the performance of their duties to the COA Audit Team; and

c. Require the Disbursing Officers to prepare a schedule of payment to be made through cash advance so as to prevent the incurrence of overlapping cash advances intended for the same purposes such as payroll of JO personnel, provision of certain financial assistance and the like.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Disposal Committee to submit a valid explanation as to the non-disposal of numerous unserviceable properties costing as much as P47,211,417.27;

b. Instruct the Disposal Committee to prepare a timetable for the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties in the custody of the LGU to avoid further deterioration, loss, and unnecessary consumption of storage space and submit a copy to COA Audit Team; and

c. Subject to the financial capability of the Municipality, prioritize the provision of an adequate and secured storage area for the reported unserviceable properties, pending disposal thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation in prior year that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Human Resources and Management Officer to:

a. Trace back and determine the validity of the long outstanding amounts under the Due to GSIS, Due to PAG-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts which were existing since CY 2001; and

b. Make representation with the inter-agencies, perform reconciliation of records, and adjust accordingly any discrepancies noted for the eventual cleansing of the long outstanding amounts.

We also recommended strict compliance with RA Nos. 8291, 7875 and 9679, the laws, rules regulations on remittance/payment of withheld contributions and/or loan payment to the GSIS, PAG-IBIG and PhilHealth, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Human Resource and Management Officer to verify the veracity of year-end balances of the Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to PAG-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts. If necessary, make representation to these inter-agencies to facilitate the reconciliation of records and adjustment of disparity, if any.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to evaluate the technical viability of development projects at the planning stage to ensure that only development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready will be funded by the 20% DF.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor to evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper identification and selection of specific priority PPAs to accelerate their implementation to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to submit justification/explanation why the brand name is not indicated in the Contract, particularly in the Technical Specifications provided. Henceforth, ensure that all future Contract/Purchase Orders include the brand name, model and complete detailed specification of the items being procured, in compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System, through the GAD Focal Person to implement the identified PPAs with GAD budget allocation and ensure that these fully correspond, address and adhere to the basic purpose and objectives for which the funds were provided.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee, the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to:

a. Plan/strategize on how would the Municipality apply the one-time
cleansing and incorporate it in the PIP to be submitted at least 10 calendar days before
the scheduled inventory-taking activities;

b. Immediately start the application of one-time cleansing of the PPEs; and

c. Prepare the RPCPPE in conformity with the format and contents prescribed by
Annex 49 Chapter II of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume II and completely
fill up and include the vital information on the report pertaining the actual result
of the physical inventory count undertaken.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prepare the Reconciliation between the Actual Amounts as presented in the
SCBAA and the SFPer as additional disclosures in the NFS to clarify the
discrepancies between the two reports, and submit the same to the Audit Team
as well as the Statement of Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities; and

b. Prepare the NFS for the succeeding year using the prescribed Pro-forma under
Annex G of COA Circular No. 2016-004. Ensure that each item on the face of the
statement of financial position, statement of financial performance, statement of
changes in net assets/equity and cash flow statement shall be cross-referenced
to any related information in the notes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payee for the presentation and
submission of stale checks as requirement prior to issuance of the replacement
check and to practice the sending of written notice to the payee at least one month
before a check becomes stale;

b. Municipal Accountant to restore the amount to Cash in Bank and to set up the
related accounts payable accounts for the cancellation of the checks for the fair
presentation of the related accounts in the financial statements; and

c. Municipal Accountant to adjust all valid reconciling items determined during the
preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis for
accurate account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Reconcile employees’ records with GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the
affected employees, if any, on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the
amounts due to these agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance
for some accounts and make the proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of
unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties; and

b. Review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors and its
corresponding remittances, and ensure that these taxes are remitted within the
prescribed period to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal General Services
Officer (GSO) to:

a. submit justification why the information as to brand was not indicated in the POs
and ensure that all future POs include the brand name, model and complete
detailed specification of the items being procured, in compliance with COA
Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996;

b. submit valid explanation why the delivered dump trucks were not in accordance
with the specifications provided for in the POs for further evaluation by the Audit
Team. If the said deficiency will not be addressed sufficiently, the procurement will
be considered an irregular expenditure and may be disallowed in audit; and

c. instruct the concerned barangay officials to make corrective repairs to the
damaged rear wheel of the donated dump truck for it to be usable again for the
benefits of the barangay constituents.

We also recommended strict compliance with COA Circular No. 96-010 and ensure that
all future Contract/Purchase Orders include the brand name, model and complete
detailed specification of the items being procured to ensure accuracy of the items
delivered by suppliers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Engineer to review the unexpended
balances and unobligated appropriations of PPAs carried over as CY 2023
Continuing Appropriations to determine which are eligible as continuing PPAs
and thereafter, consider the reversion of those unobligated appropriations of
completed PPAs which are no longer to be pursued by the Municipality to be
used for other potential developmental projects;

b. Municipal Planning and Development Council to assess the viability of the
identified PPAs in the planning stage to ensure that only those projects which
are procurement and implementation ready will be given budget allocation; and

c. Municipal Engineer and Implementing Departments to ensure the optimal and
timely implementation of the projects to be funded of the 20% Development Fund
to address the development needs of the residents and to reap the desirable
socio-economic benefits at the earliest pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG
JMC No. 2020-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRM Officer to:

a. Submit the Municipality’s plan for the implementation of unutilized balance of
LDRRM Fund amounting to P4,173,392.17;

b. Submit written justifications and explanations regarding the above observation
on unutilized LDRRM Fund;

c. Ensure correct classification of PPAs to be charged under the 5% LDRRM Fund
whether for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses or Capital Outlay for
proper treatment thereof of the unutilized portion/savings; and

d. Judiciously plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Fund and to
accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the
Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and
management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to refrain
from assigning JO personnel to perform collection functions innate to regular
accountable officers of the Municipality such as Revenue Collection Clerk/ Officer as
such entail responsibility and accountability for money and property.

In order to have additional plantilla positions, we recommended that Management make
an extra effort in raising revenue/receipts such as tax collection efficiency and
generating revenue related projects or activities so as to increase the base of
PS limitation for the succeeding fiscal year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee and the Technical Working Group to:

a. Submit the Annual GPB to the PPDO, from PPDO to DILG Provincial Office and
secure the Certificate of Endorsement from the DILG Provincial Office before
incorporating to the Municipality’s AIP and Annual Budget to properly ascertain
the relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness of the
PPAs indicated in the GPB;

b. Submit the GAD AR within the deadline set by the Joint Memorandum Circular to
facilitate timely evaluation and validation of GAD reports, budget, and utilization;

c. Comply with the proper prescribed format of the Annual GPB to present the
significant data needed for the assessment of the gender-sensitivity of the GPB;
and

d. Ensure reconciliation of the contents of the GAD AR and the GPB and with other
reports prepared by the Municipality to ensure the fairness and reliability of the
reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GFPS TWG to:

a. Prepare GAD Agenda as soon as possible and strictly comply with the provisions
on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to ensure that the PPAs indicated in the
annual GPB are aligned with it or with the identified gender issues to be
addressed during the three-year term of the LCE’s leadership; and

b. Submit the GPB and GAD AR within the prescribed period to facilitate evaluation
and validation of the GAD PPAs.

We emphasized and reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the
Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Submit a valid explanation on the consistent non-submission of the monthly BRS for
several bank accounts, particularly those under the LBP despite being pointed out in the
preceding years, and on the noted discrepancy between the Subsidiary Ledger Balance and
Post-Closing Trial Balance of the Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Current Account for the
Development Bank of the Philippines account;
b. Strictly observe the monthly preparation of complete and reliable BRS for all the bank
accounts of the Municipality to validate the balance of the Cash in Bank accounts
presented in the financial statements in compliance with Section 3 of COA Circular No. 96-011; and
c. Restore to the appropriate Cash in Bank and liability accounts the amount of stale
checks and unreleased checks as at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
account for the unreconciled amounts for Income and Capital Outlay in the amount of
P3,089,729.74 and P53,467,543.13, respectively, and resubmit the Reconciliation
between the Actual Amounts as presented in the SCBAA and the SFPer to the COA
Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
HRMO to verify the veracity of Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth
accounts balances. If necessary, make representation to the inter-agencies to facilitate
reconciliation of records and adjustment of any disparity.
Henceforth, strictly comply with the aforesaid GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth
regulations on withholding of contributions and remittance/payment of withheld
amounts and/or loan payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Acting Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to expedite the retrieval of other
pertinent documents for the donated motor vehicles and furnish the Municipal
Accountant with the copy thereof for recording in the books of accounts;
b. Inventory Committee to submit an explanation as to the non-submission of the
standard ICF for the counted PPEs during the conduct of inventory taking, and
furnish a copy thereof to COA as basis of the submitted RPCPPE;
c. Inventory Committee to update the RPCPPE after posting all remaining unrecorded
acquisition/receipt, issue/transfer and disposal of PPE, if any, and submit a copy to
the COA;
d. Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary accounting entries to write-off/ drop
from the books of accounts the balances lumped as either "Unreconciled
Amounts", "For adjustments" or “Beginning Balances” for PPEs, and the like, if any
as of December 31, 2023 pursuant to Items 6.3.1(e) of the same Circular;
e. Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosures of relevant information on
PPEs in the Notes to the Financial Statement such as the total amount and details
of non-existing/missing PPEs included in the PPE balance and the status of request
for authority for derecognition filed with the COA; and
f. Acting MGSO and Municipal Accountant to work together in reconciling their
respective records, undertake collaborative procedures prescribed under Item 6.3
of COA Circular No. 2020-006, and in the course of reconciliation, prepare/
maintain the necessary documents and reports due from their offices such the
complete and updated PCs, PPELCs and PARs.

Henceforth, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Acting Municipal General Services Officer and the Inventory Committee to:
a. Prepare a timetable for the immediate disposal of remaining unserviceable
properties in the custody of the local government unit (LGU) to avoid further
deterioration, wastage, loss, and unnecessary consumption of storage and submit
a copy to the COA Audit Team; and
b. Determine the nature of the item in the amount of P100,000.00 that requires
adjustments and exclude it in the IIRUP, if warranted. Exclude also the sold and
demolished properties since these items no longer existed in the LGU’s premises
to come up with a reliable list and amount of unserviceable properties to be
disposed of.
We also recommended strict compliance with the provisions of RA No, 7160, the Manual
on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments and COA Circular No.
2020-006 relative to the disposal of unserviceable, obsolete and/or no longer needed
PPEs.

We recommended the Management to submit a valid explanation on why the fund
received under the LGSF-FA to LGUs was utilized first prior to securing confirmation
as to the specific intended purpose of the funds.
Given the refund of the LGSF-FA to LGUs to the account of the Trust Fund from the
General Fund, we also recommended that Management cause the implementation of
the Solar-Powered Water System with Filtration Project at Lobo Central School, Lobo,
Batangas in accordance with the terms agreed by both parties.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
Municipal Officials to adhere faithfully to the terms stipulated in the duly approved
Memorandum of Agreement (MOA)/Trust Agreement or any equivalent/pertinent
document as basis of project implementation and fund utilization. Require them to refrain
from utilizing funds received in trust without first securing a copy of any agreement or
related document indicating clearly and specifically the intended purpose for which the
fund was received or given. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of
COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. OIC-MPDC to render justification for not conducting a Feasibility Study for the
Construction of Multi-Purpose/Evacuation Center;
b. OIC-MPDC and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO)
to submit an explanation on the noted observations, see items (b) to (f); and
c. Bids and Awards Committee and OIC-MPDC to ensure adherence on the preparation
of a feasibility or pre-engineering study before the conduct of detailed engineering
of a particular project pursuant to the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to avoid
incurrence of unnecessary costs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. OIC-MPDC and Municipal Engineer to prepare a timetable for the implementation of
the remaining unimplemented projects funded out of the 20% DF and submit to the
COA Audit Team; and
b. OIC-MPDC to evaluate the technical viability of development projects at the planning
stage to ensure that only development projects that are well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation ready will be funded by the 20% DF.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council headed by the Municipal Mayor to evaluate
and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper identification
and selection of specific priority PPAs to accelerate their implementation to achieve
the goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction
and management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to refrain
from assigning Job Order personnel to perform collection functions innate to regular
accountable officers of the Municipality such as Revenue Collection Clerk/Officer as
such entail responsibility and accountability for money and property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Sangguniang
Bayan, to require the Head of the HRMO to:
a. Come up with concrete policy guidelines relative to the hiring/rehiring of JO
personnel to establish a clear expectation and protect the rights of both the
employees and the Municipality; and
b. Set forth, among other stipulations, a standardized work duration for the JO.
Consider employment that has a minimum term of 30 days and a maximum term not
exceeding six months.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the;
a. Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for property insurance of the Municipality
subject to its financial capacity; and
b. Acting Municipal General Services Officer to prepare the Property Inventory Form and
cause the appraisal of the properties and submit them for application of insurance with GSIS
to protect the Management through indemnification in cases of damage or losses due to
fortuitous events.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require to GFPS, through the GAD Focal
Person to submit a valid explanation on the noted inconsistencies between the GPB
and GAD AR.
We also recommended that the GFPS use the DILG-reviewed and endorsed GPB in the
formulation of the GAD AR and attach the documents required in paragraph 5 of
Section 4.C.8 of JMC No. 2013-01 to facilitate proper monitoring, assessment and
evaluation of the GAD PPAs implemented during the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer to cause the production of the lacking
documents necessary to expedite the process and transfer of the land ownership to
Municipality; and
b. BAC, Acting MGSO and Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the documentary
requirements for all types of procurement, particularly, land as prescribed of PD No.
1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Engineer to immediately demand rectification from the contractor the
amount paid equivalent to the excess of Contract Cost over COA Evaluated Cost; and
b. Municipal Engineer, the BAC and the Technical Working Group to meticulously review
all the documents, particularly the ABC computation before proceeding to subsequent
procurement activities, and strictly comply in the computation of the ABC being the
ceiling amount of any bid as prescribed under DPWH DO No. 197 and pursuant to
Section 31.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayorto:
a. create an Inventory Committee for one-time cleansing or PPE accounts, which composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory inthree months orless; and
b. require the General Services Officer, Municipal Accountant and the duly created Inventory Committee to follow the procedural guidelines on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 such as (1) Preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking; (2) Inventory taking; (3) Reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records; (4) Disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs; (5) Procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability; and (6) Accounting entries and disclosure in the NotestoFinancial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the remaining balance to appropriate account in the GF book and record the subsequent funds receive pertaining to the shares from lotto draws and STL, shares from fire safety inspection fees, and shares from the collection of fees for the cutting of coconut trees, to its appropriate income accounts in the GF pursuant to COACircular No. 2015-009.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayorrequire the:
a. Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer to prepare the Local Road Network Ledger Card and Local Road Network Property Card, respectively, using the prescribed format under COA Circular No. 2015-008 for all recorded road networks of theMunicipality; and
b. Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of providing residual value for the depreciable component of theroad network systems pursuant toCOACircular No. 2015-008.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayordirect theMunicipalAccountant to:
a. record the subsequent reversion of staled checks and cancellation of checks to itsappropriate accounts pursuant to COACircular No. 2015-009; and
b. recognize in the books the taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of Accounts Payable for all goods received or services rendered, inspected and acceptedpursuant toBIRRR No. 2-98, asamended by RR No. 12-2001.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayorrequire the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to monitor the effectivity and expiration dates of fidelity bonds of all accountable officers and see to it that renewal thereof shall be made before or 30 days prior to the expiration as required under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019; and
b. Municipal Accountant to grant cash advances to Accountable Officers with valid fidelity bond onlypursuant toSection 305 of RA No. 7160.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayordirect the:
a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of granting simultaneous and overlapping cash advances and require theAccountable Officer to liquidate immediately the first cash advances before granting another cash advances; and
b. Municipal Accountant to propose specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of cash advances, which include, among others, the regular sending of demand letters to Accountable Officers with a reminder that their salaries could be withheld should they still fail to liquidate after demand and non-entitlement tothePerformance-Based Bonus.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare the RAAO per function/program/project, allotment class, current/continuing appropriations and fund and record the obligations based on the source documents, and subsequently prepare the SAAOB based on the prepared RAAO, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2006-002and Section18 of Volume IIof the Manual onNGAS.

We recommended the Municipal Development Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor to assess the feasibility of the implementation and responsiveness to the current needs of the constituents of those unimplemented projects totaling P9,849,464.61 and include development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG Joint MemorandumCircular No. 2020-01.

Werecommended that the Municipal Mayorrequire the:
a. MDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs; and
b. MDRRMC to include in the LDRRMFIP the planned PPAs to be funded by the total unexpended balance ofprioryears’LDRRMF in the TF book.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the GFPS to prepare the GAD Agenda pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDAJMCNo. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to prepare the lapsing schedule with complete information consistent with the balances presented in the financial statements. Also, reconcile it with the recorded amounts in the general and subsidiary ledgers to ensure the correctness of the reported balances of the property, plant and equipment in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to implement the provisions of Sections 4.1 and 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2022-004. Henceforth, use the new capitalization threshold in recording similar assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the remaining balance to appropriate income accounts and record the subsequent 20 percent share of the Municipality from the Fire Code Fees as Permit Fees and shares from the fees collected from cutting coconut trees remitted by the Philippine Coconut Authority as Share from National Wealth in the General Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009, RA 9514 and BLGF MC No. 03-01-2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous recognition of insurance premium payments as outright expense in the books of the Municipality and to fairly present its financial statements and records. Henceforth, strictly follow the aforementioned rules and regulations, and observe caution in the allocation of its insurance premiums.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of Accounts Payable (AP) for goods received or services rendered, inspected and accepted, and for construction/infrastructure projects partially or fully completed and inspected pursuant BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Review the figures posted in the general ledger versus actual payroll deductions to ensure accuracy and make the necessary correcting entries, if necessary;

b. Ensure that posting of transactions is done on the proper ledger to monitor the balances; and

c. Identify the affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to the concerned agencies. Identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments.

Thus, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Instruct the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with Section 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 and with the guidelines per PCW Memorandum Circular 2018-04;

b. Require the MPDO to be assisted by the GFPS and TWG to set up and maintain GAD Database and install update/s, if necessary; and

c. Follow the prescribed format of GAD Accomplishment Report in Annex E of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01. Retain as working paper the data pertaining to check numbers for the actual expenditures incurred for easy verification.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct the complete or full inventory taking of
PPE items and prepare and submit the RPCPPE afterwards;
b. Instruct the Inventory Committee and the Accounting Unit to perform the preliminary
activities of the one-time cleansing and reconcile their records thereof;
c. Plan/strategize on how the Municipality would apply the one-time cleansing and
incorporate it in the PIP to be submitted at least 10 calendar days before the
scheduled inventory-taking activities; and
d. Immediately start the application of One-time cleansing of the PPEs.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Recompute the depreciation based on the approved estimated useful lives of each
Infrastructure Assets indicated in EO No. 16, series of 2023; and
b. Prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting records to correct the
depreciation and effect the necessary adjustments in order to rectify the errors and
present the asset accounts to their proper carrying amounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the concerned personnel to immediately formulate solutions in order that
the computerization project procured will be more productive and effective before
it becomes obsolete and/or unserviceable; and
b. Submit justification or explanation on why last payment was made to the contractor
even with the issues with the system that were not yet addressed.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO and Municipal Planning
and Development Office to:
a. Submit the Municipality’s plan for the implementation of the unutilized balance of
LDRRMF amounting to P36,492,371.53;
b. Submit written justifications and explanations regarding the foregoing
observations;
c. Evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper
identification and selection of specific priority PPAs to accelerate their
implementation and judiciously plan on how to significantly increase the utilization
of the LDRRM Fund to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster
relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management; and
d. Prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan or LDRRM Plan which includes the list of
projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years and
submit a copy to the COA Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant to submit lacking
documentary requirements to support the payment of the purchased real property,
as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 for further evaluation of the Audit
Team; and
b. Request the members of the Sangguniang Bayan for the ratification of the
approved budget for the purchase of land, specifying the purpose of purchase,
cost, location and other details needed for the authorization of the local chief
executive to enter into contracts on behalf of the local government unit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to
require all the Collecting Officers to prepare their respective monthly RAAF in the
prescribed form provided in Annex 42 of the NGAS Manual and submit the same not
later than the 5th day of the ensuing month to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Tourism Officer to:
a. Stop the use of improvised tickets and use the Accountable Form 51 or cash tickets
for the collection of TEF;
b. Require the collectors to accomplish the daily RCD; and
c. Instruct the collectors to remit the collections to the Municipal Treasurer on a
daily basis or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Submit the noted lacking documents required in the grant of cash advance
corresponding to the above CF cash advances; and
b. Henceforth strictly comply with the Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8,
2015 on the submission of the supporting documents with seven days after the
release of the check.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Sangguniang
Bayan, to require the Head of the HRMO to come up with concrete policy guidelines
relative to the hiring/rehiring of JO personnel to establish a clear expectation and
protect the rights of both the employees and the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the HRMO to require the requesting office to submit justification for the
hiring of JOs based on the actual workload of the employees vis-a-vis the number of
organic personnel of the concerned offices; and
b. Provide a written policy on the hiring of JOs and ensure that payment is in
accordance at least with the minimum wage rate issued by the DOLE, subject to
Municipality’s financial capacity, or if funds are available, a comparable rate with
the regular employees and similar items or positions having similar rate for fair and
equitable compensation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and other
concerned Department Heads to refrain from assigning JO personnel to perform
collection functions innate to regular accountable officers of the Municipality such as
Revenue Collection Clerk/Officer as such entail responsibility and accountability for
money and property.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) see to it that
net take-home pay of employees shall not be less than P5,000.00. In the event that total
authorized deductions shall reduce net take home pay to less than P5,000.00,
deductions shall be made in accordance with the order of preference under Section 56
of 2023 GAA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for property insurance of
the Municipality based on its financial capacity; and
b. Instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to prepare the Property Inventory
Form and cause the appraisal of the properties, and submit them for application of
insurance with GSIS to protect the Management thru indemnification in cases of
damage or losses due to fortuitous events.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the other members of
the GAD Focal Point System, to modify the structure and composition of the GFPS to
conform with the prescribed organizational structure under Section 4.1(2) of
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee to require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group
to:
a. Prioritize gender mainstreaming efforts in GAD planning and budgeting by using
existing gender analysis tools such as the HGDG in the identification, design,
implementation, management and monitoring and evaluation stages of the various
PPAs to determine the extent of gender-responsiveness and amount to be
attributed to the GAD budget; and
b. Develop a GAD Database or Sex-Disaggregated Data for proper utilization in the
planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation of the Municipality, as
well as proper charging of the object of expenditures on actual
accomplishments.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible
by creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to
be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;

b. instruct the newly created Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Unit to
perform the preliminary activities through preparation of required reports such as
latest RPCPPE, Inventory Count Form and PPELCs and reconcile their records
before the actual physical inventory; and

c. cause the revision of the submitted PIP to include the specific name and number of
committee members, the cut-off date and other considerations after the conduct of
preliminary activities to be submitted to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar
days before the scheduled inventory taking activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

We reiterated our prior year recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly refrain from processing disbursement
vouchers for a new cash advance of an employee until his/her previous cash
advance has been settled; and

b. require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal HRMO to employ the use of ATM
payroll system for the payment of other allowances and payments to regular
employees and elected officials that will serve as part of the LGU’s internal control
to deter exposure to risks of possible loss of government funds.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from granting cash
advances in the last working day of the year to give ample time for the preparation of
liquidation report of any outstanding cash advances and require all Accountable
Officers to liquidate cash advances issued to them at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
officials/departments to refrain from indicating brand names in the Purchase Requests
and indicate the brand names/model and complete detailed specifications in Purchase
Orders.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer to submit explanation/justification on the following:

a. Difference in quantity indicated between the PR and the PO for Item Nos. 3, 8 and
10 in the above table;

b. Lower SSD capacity of delivered laptop on Item No. 6 versus what was agreed in
the PO, otherwise, demand delivery of laptop with requested specifications in the
PR/PO;

c. For Item No. 11, difference in the model delivered from what was originally
requested; and

d. Tampering of dates in the delivery receipt and the later date of issuance of the
Notice to Proceed as compared with the date of receipt of items in the Acceptance
and Inspection Report.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. direct the Municipal Planning and Development Council to include only
procurement-and-implementation ready projects in the Municipality’s 20 percent
Fund Development Plan in order that the constituents will immediately benefit from
Page 7 of 12
the timely implementation of the projects.

b. direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to ensure 100
percent implementation of planned activities under the 20 percent Development
Fund considering that funds are available for these activities; and

c. review if the projects under the Continuing Funds of the 20 percent DF are still
implementable, otherwise, revert the unexpended amount of P13,289,345.67 to the
unappropriated surplus of the General Fund and reprogram to priority projects of
the Municipality that are likewise procurement-and-implementation ready.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRMO:

a. to strictly refrain from charging against the LDRRMF the subject disbursements and
similar charges and to plan and utilize the fund only for programs and projects
which are supportive of disaster risk reduction and management activities as
provided in Section 3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Item 5 of NDRRMC- DBM-
DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and

b. in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to ensure that the plans and designs
for the rehabilitation of convention center will meet the minimum design
requirements provided under Item 5.1.2 of DILG Memorandum Circular 2018-122 if
the Municipality intends the same to be an evacuation center, especially if the said
disbursement will be charged against the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to regularly review and
update the LDRRMFIP to include the programs, projects and activities, as approved
by the local sanggunian, to be funded from all the prior years’ unexpended balances
of the LDRRMF in the Trust Fund;

b. Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the funds under the TF-LDRRMF for any
unutilized amount that will pass the five-year retention period and immediately
transfer the same to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund which shall be
made available for other social services after subsequent enactment by the local
sanggunian in compliance with Section 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and

c. Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate transfer to the Special Trust Fund of
the unexpended balances of the MF-MOOE from CY 2021 amounting to
P2,336,632.23.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Human Resource
Management Officer and Municipal Accountant to compute and deduct the applicable
taxes from the payroll of JOs whose annual receipts exceeds the P250,000.00 limit or
whose daily rate exceeds P946.97 and remit the same to the BIR within the timeline
prescribed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build their
competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender
mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and
GAD-related tools among others; and

b. require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with
the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC)
No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee together with the Gender Focal Point System and the Technical Working
Group to:

a. exert active efforts in accomplishing all the targeted and planned client-focused
and organization-focused PPAs; and

b. coordinate with the Municipal Human Resources Management Officer to require all
members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads to
undergo capacity building programs/refresher on GAD which shall include but not
be limited to: gender sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and
budgeting, gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender
Mainstreaming Evaluation Framework (GMEF), Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba
(GeRL Ka Ba) Self-Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development
Guidelines (HGDG) and other succeeding GAD tools.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all offices to submit their
individual offices’ GPB at an earlier date set by the GFPS in time for their preparation
and submission of the consolidated GPB within the prescribed deadline. This is to be
able to secure the Certificate of Endorsement of the Annual GAD Plan and Budget from
the DILG Provincial Office before incorporating to the Municipality’s Annual Budget the
planned PPAs to properly ascertain the relevance of the gender issues identified and
gender-responsiveness of the PPAs indicated in the GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible
by creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to
be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less; and,

b. Prepare and submit a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) that includes the specific name
of all committee members, the cut-off date and the schedule specifying the exact
dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted
completion of the physical inventory which shall be within three months or less and
duly approved by the Municipal Mayor to be submitted to the Audit Team at least
ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled inventory taking activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the balance to appropriate income account and record the subsequent 20
percent share of the Municipality from the Fire Code Fees as Permit Fees and the share
of the LGU from PCSO as Share from PCSO in the General Fund pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2015-009, RA 9514 and BLGF MC No. 03-01-2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to make
the necessary adjusting entries to correct balances of the affected accounts and
henceforth, strictly observe the policies on the recognition of depreciation for PPE
prescribed in PAG 3 of IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Strictly record claims as Accounts Payable only and when complete supporting
documents are submitted to substantiate their validity; and,

b. Immediately require the submission of documents for all outstanding payables as
of January 31, 2024 and validate the claims based on valid and complete supporting
documents and/or proof of the actual delivery of goods and services. Otherwise,
effect necessary adjustments/reversal in the books to conform with regulations.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services
Officer to strictly ensure that documents evidencing delivery of goods and services,
such as Delivery Receipts, Statement of Account/Contractor’s Bill, as applicable, and
Inspection and Acceptance Reports along with the procurement documents are
submitted in support of claims for payment forwarded to the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure enough funds are appropriated for
property insurance of the Municipality;

b. Direct the GSO to submit the duly accomplished PIF to the Office of the Supervising
Auditor or Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year
to be used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the
Municipality’s insurable properties; and

c. Direct the GSO to insure with the GIF of GSIS all the Municipality’s insurable assets
and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage to protect the Municipality
thru indemnification in cases of damage or loss due to fortuitous events.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to require concerned
contractors of ongoing infrastructure projects to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP in
compliance with DOLE DO No. 13, s. 1998 and Section 37.2.3(f) of the Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184.

We also recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to
require the contractors to submit CSHP duly approved by DOLE for future projects to
be implemented by the Municipality, and in case the contractor could not submit the
approved CSHP, deduct the corresponding CSHP cost from the payment to the
contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Development
Council to assess the feasibility of the implementation and responsiveness to the
current needs of the constituents of those unimplemented projects and prioritize
development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-
ready pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to:

a. Implement the programs and projects funded under the LDRRMF within the
intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of its benefits to the constituents.
Conduct thorough planning and analysis to ensure that those identified PPAs to be
included in the LDRRM Plan are those that can be implemented and completed
within the target period; and,

b. Review and update the LDRRMFIP annually to include the activities to be funded
from the unexpended QRF and Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund –
Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRM-MOOE) of the previous years
which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive
Committee together with the Gender Focal Point System and the Technical Working
Group to:

a. Exert active efforts in accomplishing all the targeted and planned client-focused
and organization-focused PPAs;

b. Coordinate with the Municipal Human Resources Management Officer to require all
members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads to
undergo capacity building programs on GAD which shall include but not be limited
to: gender sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and budgeting,
gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming
Evaluation Framework (GMEF), Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba (GeRL Ka Ba) Self-
Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) and
other succeeding GAD tools; and

c. Coordinate and reconcile with the Municipal Budget Office to accurately present the
total appropriation and utilization of the GAD Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to identify the
affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts
due to these agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some
accounts and make the proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary
financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties; and

b. Review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors and ensure
that these taxes are remitted on the prescribed period to avoid penalties and
surcharges.
