Based on the foregoing, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible by
creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be
able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;

b. Instruct the Inventory Committee to prepare a PIP to include the specific name of
committee members, the cut-off date and the schedule specifying the dates and
locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of
the physical inventory which shall be within three months or less and duly approved
by the Municipal Mayor to be submitted to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar
days before the scheduled inventory taking activities;

c. Require the newly created Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Office to
perform the preliminary activities by preparation of appropriate reports such as latest
RPCPPE, Inventory Count Form and PPELCs and reconcile their records before the
actual physical inventory; and

d. Provide transportation equipment and other necessary tools for the implementation of
the one-time cleansing.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to effect the necessary correcting entries to reflect the transfer of the
completed projects to their respective asset or PPE account depending on the purpose
and intention of the LGU on the use of such. Henceforth, ensure that CIP accounts by
year-end only pertain to those ongoing projects with accumulated cost or incurred
progress billings during construction.

Therefore, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant, in cooperation with the Municipal Budget Officer, to reconcile the
figures in SCBAA and in the Statement of Financial Performance, have it disclosed in the
Notes to Financial Statements as required in IPSAS 24 and ensure its prompt submission to
the Commission on Audit, otherwise sanction per Section 122 of PD 1445 should be
imposed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
implement the provisions of Sections 4.1 and 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2022-004.
Henceforth, use the new capitalization threshold in recording similar assets.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
to:

a. Review the figures posted in the subsidiary ledger versus actual payroll

deductions to ensure accuracy and make the necessary correcting entries, if
necessary;

b. Ensure that posting of transactions is done on the proper subsidiary ledger to
monitor the balances; and

c. Identify the affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit
the amounts due to the concerned agencies. Identify the cause/s of the over
remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the remaining balance to appropriate income account and record the
subsequent 20% share of the Municipality from the Fire Code Fees as Permit Fees in the
General Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 and RA 9514.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

Hence, we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require:

a. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to stop the granting of cash
advances to those accountable officers who have not yet fully liquidated their
previous advances;

b. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from granting cash
advances to elected officials for purposes other those intended for travelling
expenses and for confidential expenses in case of the Head of Agency.

c. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to limit the grant of cash
advances within the maximum amount covered by their bond.

d. The Municipal Accountant to demand the liquidation of the unliquidated cash
advances from the concerned officials and employees. Failure of the accountable
officer to liquidate the cash advances shall constitute a valid cause for the
withholding of the latter’s salary.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the concerned accountable officers
and/or offices to submit the lacking supporting documents to cash advances and
liquidation and provide justification on the noted lapses including the release of funds for
Pamaskong Handog.

Further, we recommended to stop the granting of communication allowances to DepEd
personnel considering the absence of legal basis for such otherwise, succeeding similar
transactions will be disallowed.

For the granted financial assistance to Barangays, require the Punong Barangays to
submit official receipt acknowledging the financial assistance and Liquidation Report
and supporting documents evidencing the disbursement of funds related to the Barangay
activities for which the funds were intended. Lastly, strictly adhere to the documentary
requirements set for all types of cash advances and its liquidation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the GSO and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to properly plan
and consolidate the procurement of goods/services of the Municipality and adopt
the proper mode of procurement instead of cash advances.

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to limit the grant of
cash advance only to those cases mentioned in COA Circular 97-002, for regular
cash advances to cashiers, disbursing officers, paymasters and/or property/supply
officers for salaries, commutable allowances, honoraria and petty operating
expenses and for special cash advance for travel expenditures and current
operating expenditures undertaken in the field where it is impractical to pay by
check..

We recommended that management stop the practice of spending government funds for
religious or private purposes otherwise, it will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
submit the cash advance voucher and its supporting documents for confidential
expenses within the period prescribed to enable the Audit Team to render timely audit
of the said transaction.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO, in coordination with
concerned Department Heads to consider the update of daily wage rate of Job Order
workers of the Municipality taking into consideration the daily minimum wage rate in the
private sector, and if funds are available, daily rate of comparable position in the
government sector.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Development Council to conduct proper assessment and evaluation of the priority
projects of each barangay and the whole Municipality to avoid idle funds and stalled
implementation of development projects.

Thus, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the GAD Focal Point System
to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with Section 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 and with the guidelines per PCW
Memorandum Circular 2018-04.

to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;

b. Instruct the Inventory Committee to prepare a PIP to include the specific name of

before the scheduled inventory taking activities;

c. Require the newly created Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Office to

d. Provide transportation equipment and other necessary tools for the
implementation of the one-time cleansing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the remaining balance to appropriate income accounts and record the
subsequent 20% share of the Municipality from the Fire Code Fees as Permit Fees,
shares from the fees collected from cutting coconut trees remitted by the Philippine
Coconut Authority as Share from National Wealth and shares from STL as Share from
PCSO in the General Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009, RA 9514 and BLGF
MC No. 03-01-2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
recognize in the books the taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of
Accounts Payable (AP) for goods received or services rendered, inspected and
accepted, and for construction/infrastructure projects partially or fully completed and
inspected pursuant BIR Memorandum Circular No. 10-2018.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to
prepare the Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for
Continuing Appropriations which would be used as basis to prepare SAAOB and
subsequently SCBAA to ensure accurate balances. Further, we recommend that the
Municipal Budget Officer verify the discrepancies noted among the reports, reconcile
and submit the corrected reports to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant,
DRRMO and Budget Officer to review thoroughly the balances reported and account
any discrepancy particularly on the beginning balances of Continuing Appropriation
which are not tie up with reported ending balances last year. Likewise, considering
the noted discrepancies in audit, verify the availability of actual cash back up in
coordination with the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to present to the MDRRMC
the savings from completed projects in previous years to be considered in their
planning of other disaster preparedness and mitigation PPAs; and

b. MDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs with continuing
appropriations and identify projects which will no longer be implemented for
possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs.

Thus, we recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Instruct the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance
with Section 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No.
2013-01 and with the guidelines per PCW Memorandum Circular 2018-04; and

b. Follow the prescribed format of GAD Accomplishment Report in Annex E of PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal General Services
Officer, Municipal Accountant and the members of the Inventory Committee to follow
the procedural guidelines on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 such as (1) Preliminary activities prior to
the conduct of inventory taking; (2) Inventory taking; (3) Reconciliation of inventory
count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records; (4) Disposition procedures
for non-existing/missing PPEs; (5) Procedures in the derecognition of non-
existing/missing PPEs without available record of accountability; and (6) Accounting
entries and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
review the noted observations above and effect the necessary adjustments after
relevant documents are secured. Submit copies of the adjusting journal entries made
to the audit team, if warranted. If adjustments will not be made, submit an explanation
letter supported with necessary schedules.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
a. The Local Scholarship Committee, Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer to provide an explanation letter for the noted issues on the: 1.) Scholarship
and educational assistance; 2.) Non-implementation of project with an appropriation
of P400,000.00 and 3.) Inconsistencies on the reported figures of the submitted
reports and documents.
b. Local Scholarship Committee ensure that the guidelines provided in the Executive
Order were met and strictly followed in the grant of scholarship and educational
assistance for CY 2024. If amendments are to be made, ensure that approval of the
Sangguniang Bayan will be secured. Likewise, ensure that scholars who
graduated were updated regularly to check if the goal of the program materialized.
c. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to cause the immediate
implementation of the unimplemented project. Likewise, provide an update to the
audit team as to the status of the projects. If the projects will no longer be
implemented and the funds reprogrammed to another projects, include those
along with the reasons in the status for submission to the audit team.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:
a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to
implement and complete the programs, projects and activities under Current
Appropriation, which were noted in this audit findings. Provide an explanation
letter and update the audit team as to its status.
b. Municipal Accountant to provide Subsidiary Ledgers per year of transfer of the
unexpended balance of DRRM Funds that were transferred to the Trust Liability
Account to fully identify which of the balances are over five years and due for
transfer to the General Fund for programming under social services; and
c. MDRRMO, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget
Office to conduct periodic reconciliation of their reports pertaining to the plans,
budgets and utilization of the 5 percent LDRRMF, starting from the observations
noted in this audit finding.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the GFPS Focal Point Person, and GAD TWG to:
a. Conduct thorough planning and analysis to ensure that those identified PPAs to be
included in the GAD Plan and Budget are those that can be implemented and
completed within the target period. Take into consideration the feasibility and
propriety of the proposed PPA before including it in the plan to avoid reversion of
funds; and
b. Ensure that the actual expenditures were aligned with the GAD PPA in order
address the identified gender issue/gender mandate. Provide supporting
documents justifying the determined cost of the noted PPAs that is charged to
GAD and explanation letter for those actual expenditures not aligned with the
planned activity in the GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to the
following:
a. Provide explanation letter for the occurrence of granting cash advances to each
disbursing officers with previous unliquidated cash advances and disbursement
vouchers which lacked the required certification from their office; and
b. Ensure that cash advances will only be granted to accountable officers with no
previous unliquidated cash advances and attach the necessary certification in the
Disbursement Voucher.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
maintain separate cash book per disbursing officer for proper monitoring of their
accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Human Resource
Management Officer and Municipal Accountant reconcile employees’ records with the
GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted
balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the
cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts, if there is any, and make the
proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the
form of imposed penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator to
countersign all the checks drawn by the Municipal Treasurer excluding those checks
charged against the SB budget pursuant to the above cited regulations.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the Inventory Committee and the Accounting Unit to perform the
preliminary activities of the one-time cleansing and reconcile their records;
and
b. Plan/strategize on how the Municipality would apply the one-time cleansing and
incorporate it in the Physical Inventory Plan to be submitted at least 10 calendar
days before the scheduled inventory-taking activities and immediately start the
application of one-time cleansing of the PPEs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Reclassify the loan payable due beyond 12 months from reporting date to
non-current liabilities; and

b. Coordinate with the bank for the reconciliation of records and prepare the
necessary adjusting entries and/or disclosures on the Notes to Financial
Statements, if needed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the accountable officers to provide
justification on the late liquidation of cash advances mentioned above. Henceforth,
strictly adhere to Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on
timely liquidation of cash advances.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor as Local Chief
Executive, instruct the Chairperson of GFPS-TWG:

a. To include programs and activities in the GPB with consideration of the
essential elements such as active GFPS, sex disaggregated data and gender
analysis;
b. To submit the GPB within the deadline to enable timely review and
endorsement of the PPDO and DILG; and
c. To strictly follow the guidelines in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01
dated July 18, 2013 which several guidelines was amended by JMC No. 2016-
01 dated January 12, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as soon as possible by
creating an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be able
to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;
b. Cause the revision of the submitted PIP to include the specific name of all committee
members, the cut-off date and the schedule specifying the exact dates and locations of
the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical
inventory which shall be within three months or less and duly approved by the Municipal
Mayor to be submitted to the Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the
scheduled inventory taking activities; and,
c. Instruct the Inventory Committee, GSO and the Accounting Unit to perform the
preliminary activities by preparation of appropriate reports such as latest RPCPPE and
PPELCs and reconcile their records before the actual physical inventory.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Recognize the utilization of transfers, assistance and subsidies in the appropriate
asset or expense accounts, with a corollary entry recording the related revenue in the
Trust Fund books of accounts; and,
b. Prepare and submit the Statement of Financial Performance of the Trust Fund for
succeeding years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Observe the disclosure requirements in the preparation of the financial statements
in accordance with the IPSASs and COA Circular No. 2016-004 to assist users in
understanding the financial statements and to provide them with information relevant
to decision-making; and,
b. Reconcile the discrepancies noted in the financial statements and effect the
necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Require both the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to investigate the
origin and nature of the noted discrepancies and make the necessary adjustments to
their respective records. Reconciliation of the cashbook and subsidiary ledger (SL) /general
ledger (GL) balances shall be conducted at least quarterly in order to ensure completeness
of the recorded financial transactions and accuracy of the cash account balances as of a
certain period;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for all cash accounts
and consistently verify and review the balances reflected in the SL/GL;
c. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payees of the checks
which have become stale, notify them of the existence of checks and require submission
of the required documentation to facilitate issuance of new checks; and,
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to cancel
the payment recorded for the stale checks amounting to P96,372.56, and restore the
said amount to the cash and appropriate liability accounts. Remove the stale checks from
the list of outstanding checks in the bank reconciliation statements and furnish the
Municipal Treasurer with a copy of the JEV as her basis for debiting the amount of stale
checks in the Cashbook - Cash in Bank.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to make the
necessary adjusting entries to correct balances of the affected accounts and henceforth,
strictly observe the policies on the recognition of depreciation for PPE prescribed in PAG 3
of IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
recognize in the books the taxes withheld simultaneously with the setting up of
Accounts Payable (AP) for goods received or services rendered, inspected and
accepted, and for construction/infrastructure projects partially or fully completed and
inspected.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Strictly record claims as Accounts Payable only and when complete supporting
documents are submitted to substantiate their validity; and,
b. Immediately require the submission of documents for all outstanding payables as of
January 31, 2024 and validate the claims based on valid and complete supporting
documents and/or proof of the actual delivery of goods and services. Otherwise, effect
necessary adjustments/reversal in the books to conform with regulations.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services
Officer to strictly ensure that documents evidencing delivery of goods and services, such as
Delivery Receipts, Statement of Account/Contractor’s Bill, as applicable, and Inspection
and Acceptance Reports along with the procurement documents are submitted in support
of claims for payment forwarded to the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Secure/collate all available documents relative to the said accounts and determine the
existence and validity of the receivables; and,
b. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries for identified errors in recording.
Otherwise, adopt the guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No. 2023-008 on the
proper disposition of dormant accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to require concerned
contractors of ongoing infrastructure projects to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP in
compliance with DOLE DO No. 13, s. 1998 and Section 37.2.3(f) of the Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184.

We also recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to
require the contractors to submit CSHP duly approved by DOLE for future projects to be
implemented by the Municipality, and in case the contractor could not submit the approved
CSHP, deduct the corresponding CSHP cost from the payment to the contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to identify the affected
employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these
agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the
proper adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form
of imposed penalties; and,
b. Review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors and ensure that
these taxes are remitted on the prescribed period to avoid penalties and surcharges.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:

a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to implement
the programs and projects funded under the LDRRMF, within the intended time to avoid
delay in the delivery of its benefits to the constituents. Conduct thorough planning and
analysis to ensure that those identified PPAs to be included in the LDRRMFIP are those that
can be implemented and completed within the target period;
b. Municipal Accountant review the amount transferred to the Trust Liability - DRRM
account pertaining to the unexpended balances of prior year appropriations. Likewise,
prepare an adjusting entry through the issuance of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), if
necessary and submit such copies to the audit team;
c. MDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to conduct periodic reconciliation of the
reported appropriation, utilization and balance of the LDRRM Funds and effect necessary
changes to their records, if any;
d. Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers per year of transfer of the
unexpended balance of DRRM Funds that were transferred to the Special Trust Fund; and,
e. MDRRMO to review and update the LDRRMFIP annually to include the activities to be
funded from the unexpended QRF and Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund –
Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRM-MOOE) of the previous years which
were transferred to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. The City Accountant to take up necessary accounting entries to reclassify the road lots under the Road Networks account and transfer the same from the Land PropertyCards to Road Networks Cards; and
b. The City General Services Office (GSO) to transfer the road lots from the Land PropertyCards to the Road Networks PropertyCards; and
c. The City Accountant to take up necessary accounting entries to recognize the unrecorded lots in the Land and Road Networks accounts.
Henceforth, we recommended the strict compliance with IPSAS 1, IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-008.
observations.managementAction:
The necessary adjusting entries with Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reclassify the road networks account in the books to reclassify the road networks account. Consequently, the ledgers cards of the affected accounts were also updated.
The road lots records were transferred from the Property Cards to the road networks property cards by the City GSO.
The necessary accounting entries to recognize the real properties of the Land and Road Networks accounts.
Henceforth, strict compliance with the mentioned accounting standards and COA circular is already being observed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
1) instruct the Inventory Committee to expedite the one-time cleansing activities and reported the results of the one-time cleansing activities and
2) adopt the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of physical inventory taking and submit the appropriate reconciliation/derecognition documents such as IIRUPs and supporting JEVs.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee for the One-Time Cleansing activities completed the one-time cleansing activities and identified the discrepancies between the records of both offices. The one-time cleansing activities were conducted and the committee identified items found at station and excluded items that are not part of PPE.
The City completed the one-time cleansing activities of PPE. The corresponding reports on road networks were also prepared and submitted. Supported by JEVs dated October 31, 2023: • JEV-2023-10-001380 • JEV-2023-10-001397 • JEV-2023-10-001398 • JEV-2023-10-001401 • JEV-2023-10-001402 • JEV-2023-10-001403 • JEV-2023-10-041465 • JEV-2023-10-041468 • JEV-2023-10-041468 • JEV-2023-10-041529 • JEV-2023-10-041532 • JEV-2023-10-041535 • JEV-2023-10-041554 • JEV-2023-10-041558 • JEV-2023-10-041559 • JEV-2023-10-041561 • JEV-2023-10-041569 • JEV-2023-10-041582 • JEV-2023-10-041669 • JEV-2023-10-041839 • JEV-2023-10-041835 • JEV-2023-10-041851 • JEV-2023-10-041681 • JEV-2023-10-041682 • JEV-2023-10-041683 • JEV-2023-10-041680 • JEV-2023-10-041958 • JEV-2023-10-041964 • JEV-2023-10-041856 • JEV-2023-10-041865 • JEV-2023-10-041883

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record and provide depreciation for the two completed school buildings and provide the corresponding depreciation within 15 calendar days from receipt thereof. Henceforth, comply strictly with IPSAS 17.
observations.managementAction: Properties were recognized in the books supported by JEV-2023-02-000133 and JEV-2023-00134 dated February 9 and 14, 2023. Compliance with IPSAS 17 is already observed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Planning and Development Coordinator and City Budget Office to:
1) submit within 60 days from receipt thereof, the City’s plan for the utilization and implementation of the unutilized balance of 20% DF; and 2) utilize the savings from appropriation of completed project. Also, ensure proper planning so that development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
observations.managementAction: The CPDO will collaborate and consult with the Local Finance Committee and other concerned offices, in preparing the City’s plan for the utilization and implementation of 20% DF and undertake to submit the City’s plan for the utilization and implementation of the 20% DF within 60 days from receipt of the AOM. The CPDO has enhanced its planning and coordination with the City Budget Officer and will utilize savings from appropriation of completed projects. The CPDO has forwarded the matter of EPA of eligible priority development projects to the BAC and the CEO and has been consistently following up the preparation or completion of Program of Works (POW) from the CEO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. The City Accountant to take immediate derecognition actions to adjust the PPE accounts beginning balances/ captioned as auctioned/unaccounted/demolished PPE as part of the one-time cleansing activities; and
b. The Inventory Committee and the City General Services Office to identify/locate properties found at station and excluded items that are not part of PPE, and to conduct physical count and reconcile records.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee for the One-Time Cleansing activities completed the one-time cleansing activities and identified the discrepancies between the records of both offices. The one-time cleansing activity resulted in the reconciliation of property records.
The City completed the one-time cleansing of PPE. The guidelines for the one-time cleansing activity were adopted by the City Government thru the Inventory Committee. The corresponding JEVs were created and properties identified as missing were subjected to demand letters.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to reconcile the balance of RPT and SET Receivables as per books of accounts and List of Delinquent Taxpayer Results as of December 31, 2021, and effect the necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: Quarterly reconciliation is being done by the City Treasurer and City Accountant. Separate ledgers supported by tax declarations are being maintained. Results of reconciliation are being effected as necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor instruct concerned city officials of strict compliance with the guidelines and procedures in the write-off of dormant receivable accounts as required under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: A detailed evaluation and application of the COA Circular No. 2016-005 was undertaken. The City has set up an allowance for the dormant receivables and prepared JEVs for the write-off process. JEVs prepared include: JEV-2024-04-012096, JEV-2024-04-012168, JEV-2024-06-018469, JEV-2024-06-018474, JEV-2024-06-018478, and JEV-2024-06-018481.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City General Services Officer and City Assessor to conduct site inspection of each parcel and prepare reports on the actual usage of each parcel of land and existence of unlawful occupants, if any, and provide the said report to the City Accountant.
observations.managementAction: The one-time cleansing of PPE that included the verification of real properties of the City was completed. The necessary reports on road projects, and reports on the physical count of local road network system were prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LDRRM Council headed by the City Mayor evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper identification and selection of specific priority PPAs to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the City regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management.
observations.managementAction: The City LDRRMO will monitor and coordinate with implementing departments to expedite the implementation of the identified projects. The available funds in the LDRRM Trust Fund shall be allocated for the purpose of quick response and incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Planning and Development Coordinator and City Budget Office to judiciously monitor, and properly plan identified priority projects to ensure optimal utilization of the fund and avoid unutilized appropriations; and to conduct a thorough study of the priority projects to ensure their implementation throughout a given year.
observations.managementAction: The CPDO and City Budget Officer were directed to review feasibility of projects from CY 2021 and previous years. The CPDO enhanced its monitoring and planning with coordination among concerned offices; priority projects were planned and listed down. The CPDO, in consultation with the Local Finance Committee, will utilize the savings from completed projects.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the City Mayor: a. obliges the City Accountant to immediately derecognize auctioned/sold and demolished properties from the books; b. the Inventory Committee of the GSO to identify/locate the Flood Control System properties and exclude from the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment items that are not part of PPE; and c. City Accountant and General Services Officer to reconcile the road networks record per book and the result of the physical inventory records at the end of each year.
observations.managementAction: The City fully implemented the one-time cleansing activities. The City Accountant has derecognized the captioned beginning balances/ captioned as auctioned/unaccounted/ demolished PPE as these items were identified and these accountable officers for the unaccounted properties were served with demand letters. The Inventory Committee identified properties found at station and excluded items that are not part of PPE. Completion of the one-time cleansing activity resulted in reconciliation and adjustment JEVs (e.g., JEV-2023-10-040757 dated October 2, 2023).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require all Inventory custodians to maintain complete and updated stock cards and require the City Accountant and the City General Services Officer or custodians to have regular reconciliation of their records to immediately discover and reconcile discrepancies, if any.
observations.managementAction: Inventory custodians are now required to maintain complete and updated stock cards. Regular reconciliation of inventory custodians, the GSO and the Accounting Office is being done to reconcile the different inventory records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the CSWDO, CHO and OCVAS to conduct physical inventory of all undistributed and unconsumed inventories on hand and to prepare and submit the reports to the City Accounting Office for proper recording of Inventory balances.
observations.managementAction: Physical inventory of supplies was prepared for the unconsumed and undistributed inventories on hand and the reports were submitted to the City Accountant for proper recording and reconciliation of inventory balances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:
a. The GSO to coordinate with the City Engineer in the conduct of yearly physical inspection and submission of records and appropriate reports on road network projects, and report on local road network system; b. The City Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and c. Both the City Accountant and GSO to reconcile the road networks record per book and the result of the physical inventory records at the end of each year to ensure existence and accuracy of road networks presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The City completed the one-time cleansing of PPE. The City prepared the reports on road projects and supported reports on local road networks. The City maintains subsidiary ledger cards for local road networks. Completion of the one-time cleansing activities resulted in reconciliation of property records. Supported by JEV-2023-10-040757 dated October 2, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the City Mayor to instruct the OIC-City Accountant to: a) coordinate with the City GSO to evaluate the causes of the material discrepancy and make reconciliation within 90 days from receipt hereof to ascertain the fairness and reliability of the PPE balances in the financial statements; b) coordinate with the City Engineering Office (CEO) for the necessary documents to record/reclassify the aforementioned projects to their proper accounts and record the related Depreciation Expense, Accumulated Depreciation and Accumulated Surplus and submit to the Audit Team the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) within 30 days from receipt thereof; and c) expedite the processing of claims of the completed infrastructure projects by contract and submit the same to the CAO to prevent the delayed recognition of the said infrastructure assets in the books.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-City Accountant started the reconciliation between the GSO records and accounting records and make reconciliation within 90 days from receipt hereof to ascertain the fairness and reliability of the PPE balances in the financial statements; The completed projects were recorded and reclassified to its proper accounts as shown in the submitted JEVs.
observations.reason: While the reconciliation between their records started, the same is still on-going and is substantially unimplemented as the concerned personnel could not yet focus thereto due to their regular daily responsibilities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a) LFC to analyze the trend in the collection of tax revenues for the last five years and determine the amount of income which has reasonable collectivity and use the data as inputs or benchmark in the preparation of Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year; b) City Treasurer to continue the daily monitoring of cash availability and preparation of Status of Cash Report for submission to the Office of the City Mayor and concerned department heads; c) LFC to prepare the City’s plan for the reduction of the cash deficit to a zero level as soon as possible and submit the same to the Audit Team within 30 days from receipt hereof; d) City Budget Officer, OIC-City Accountant and City Treasurer to constantly and regularly exercise diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the City’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year to totally eliminate the cash deficiency of the City; and e) LFC and other concerned department heads to strictly follow the Budget Operation Manual for LGUs 2016 Edition under Local Budget Circular No. 112 of the Department of Budget and Management.
observations.managementAction: The LFC projected only income with reasonable assurance of collectivity, hence actual income exceeded the target. The City Treasurer continue the daily monitoring of cash availability and maintained a monitoring of cash availability. The LFC did not submit a documented plan on eliminating the cash deficit but manifested verbally their approach on the collection of taxes, as well as on the reduction of expenses, leading to cash surplus in 2023. City Budget Officer, OIC-City Accountant and City Treasurer followed the Budget Operations Manual for LGUs.
observations.reason: While the LFC did not submit a documented plan, they manifested verbally their aggressive approach on tax collection and expense reduction; Budget Operations Manual was followed, thus cash deficit was eliminated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the: a) City Treasurer to furnish the OIC-City Accountant a duly certified list of unpaid taxes supported with details containing the name of the taxpayer and the amount due to establish reliable RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of each year; b) City Treasurer and OIC-City Accountant to analyze and reconcile their records to determine the deficiencies noted and make necessary adjustments within 60 days from receipt hereof; and c) City Treasurer and City Administrator to coordinate and jointly exert extra efforts in the collection of delinquent accounts and adopt necessary measures to enable the maximum collection of unpaid taxes.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer furnished the OIC-City Accountant a duly certified list of unpaid taxes. The City Treasurer and OIC-City Accountant started the reconciliation of records.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of records is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the LDC to submit a ratified CDP and LDIP that will specifically serve as the basis for the identification of development projects to be prioritized during CY 2022 and applicable years thereafter.
observations.managementAction: The City submitted a ratified CDP and LDIP to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enforce timely project implementation by requiring the City Engineer in coordination with City Planning and Development Officer to: a) conscientiously carry out detailed engineering addressing any issues on the viability of the project sites to ensure that projects are ready before they are posted for bidding; b) thoroughly review and evaluate the Program of Works to ensure project designs and estimates are properly prepared and suit the needs of end-users; c) ensure full coordination with other government agencies, among others, DPWH, DepEd and DENR including the community and private entities, and d) strictly monitor and supervise the status/progress of projects being implemented.
observations.managementAction: Generally, the City Engineer ensured that projects which were probable to be implemented were thoroughly reviewed and evaluated. Programs of Works for most projects were thoroughly reviewed and evaluated. Projects which were probable to be implemented were monitored and supervised.
observations.reason: There are still projects that were completed beyond the target completion date for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer in coordination with the City Planning and Development Officer to: a) require the contractors to devise catch-up program within 15 days from receipt hereof in order to fast track the completion of the projects; b) take appropriate actions to resolve ROW issue within 20 days from receipt hereof; c) evaluate the viability of the infrastructure project which has yet to start for possible reprogramming of the funds; d) take appropriate actions for the delay in the completion of the project by imposing liquidated damages of at least one-tenth of one percent of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and e) intensify and perform regular monitoring and supervision to accomplish the mandated targets committed for the year.
observations.managementAction: The CEO provided a catch-up plan to the Audit Team. The CEO submitted Resolution No. 23-179 authorizing the transfer of the portion of the project construction. Regular monitoring and supervision were conducted by the CEO on the implementation of projects.
observations.reason: Liquidated damages were not imposed for delayed projects. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 9 with modification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of Inventory accounts and prepare the corresponding adjusting JEV, if necessary, and submit to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting entries were made under unnumbered JEV dated May 30, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Treasurer to revisit and mobilize tax campaign strategies to increase revenue capabilities in order to cover current liabilities and continuing appropriations.
observations.managementAction: Various revenue campaign strategies were implemented by the City which helped eliminate the cash deficit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Inventory Committee to conduct inventory taking and immediately reconcile results with the RPCPPE and accounting records and trace differences; b) Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledger Cards for each group of PPEs indicating every details of the properties pursuant to Section 269, Volume I, of Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and reconcile acquisition cost of all PPEs presented in the lapsing schedule and financial statements as of year-end; and c) GSO to prepare and maintain Property Cards for all PPEs of the municipality with complete description and details for proper monitoring.
observations.managementAction: Property cards were being maintained. The municipal accountant and GSO are preparing subsidiary ledger cards and reconciling records.
observations.reason: Some details were not provided in the ledger cards such as property number and reference document.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to ensure coordination towards the full implementation of the perpetual inventory method in recording and reporting of all procured supplies and materials issued to end users and ensure distribution and consumption are supported by distribution lists and/or proof of acknowledgement.
observations.managementAction: Due to limited room and storage, some items were immediately issued to end-users and recorded as outright expense. The City is implementing improvements to perpetual inventory procedures and maintaining distribution lists where possible.
observations.reason: Due to limited storage and logistics, some procured goods were immediately issued to end-users and recorded as expense. Management committed to implement perpetual inventory.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Sangguniang Panglungsod Members scrutinize and examine properly the sources of appropriation of Annual Budget before approval and ensure that LDRRM funds will be appropriated and planned pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Sangguniang Panglungsod has been consistent in ensuring that LDRRM funds will be appropriated and planned pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a.City Engineer to furnish the City Accountant of the duly signed/approved Certificate of Completion and Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) and all other documents of all completed projects as at year-end.
b. City Accountant to recognize those properties that are to be procured and implemented in the future under the trust fund using the appropriate PPE accounts in the Trust Fund books and transfer such properties to the General Fund books upon completion pursuant to Section 104 of MNGAS for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer furnished the Office of the City Accountant the copies of the duly signed/ approved Certificate of Completion and SWA and all other documents of all completed projects as at year-end. In addition, the City Accountant recognized in the City’s book of accounts the assets acquired using the trust fund in CY 2023 per Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) Nos. 2023-03-003339, 2023-03-003340, and 2023-03-003341, all dated March 8, 2023. Properties procured and implemented in the future under the trust fund in CY 2023 were recognized in the Trust Fund book and transferred to the General Fund book upon completion per JEV Nos. 2023-04-000394, 2023-04-000395, and 2023-04-003025, all dated April 3, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record its financial transactions in conformity with IPSAS 1 and in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 and reclassify accounts as necessary.
observations.managementAction: The revenue and expenses in the Trust Fund book was recognized during the year and the Statement of Financial Performance in the Trust Fund book was prepared and submitted to the Audit Team by the Office of the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the: a. City Health Officer to render monthly report on the receipts, issuances, and balances of all kinds of vaccines, medicines and ancillary supplies directly received from other government and private agencies; and b. City Accountant to recognize in the books of accounts the cost of ending inventory as of December 31, 2022 of the COVID-19 vaccines and all other vaccines, and ancillary supplies received from the DOH and record the future receipts and issuances of the same.
observations.managementAction: The City Health Office submitted the report to the City Accounting Office on the receipts, issuances and remaining balances of vaccines received by the City from DOH. The balances of the vaccines received by the City as of March 15, 2023 were recorded by the City Accountant per JEV of the COVID-19 No. 2023-03-002315 dated March 15, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. City Treasurer to prepare the list of unreleased checks as of year-end and submit to the City Accountant within the first week of the succeeding year; and b. City Accountant to restore to the Cash in Bank and appropriate liability account the corresponding amount of unreleased check as of year-end in compliance with IPSAS 2 and COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The list of unreleased checks as of December 31, 2023 was submitted by the City Treasurer to the Office of the City Accountant on January 3, 2024. The Office of the City Accountant restore to the Cash in Bank and Accounts Payable the corresponding amount of the unreleased checks as of December 31, 2023 totaling P83,274,318.01 per JEV Nos. 2023-12-015439, 2023-12-015105, 2023-12-015106, 2023-12-015419, 2023-12-015420, 2023-12-000450, 2023-12-002099, all dated December 29, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Budget Officer and City Accountant to use the appropriate account for the appropriation and recording in the books of accounts the other compensation and personnel benefit contributions of casual employees in succeeding years in accordance with the prescribed Chart of Accounts for LGUs pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The City officials replied that the corrective measures will be promptly implemented and ensure compliance with the relevant legal provisions and accounting standards. In addition, enhanced internal controls and procedures will be established to prevent similar instances of misstatement and ensure adherence to applicable regulations. Moreover, the Office of the City Accountant informed that some of the Accounts Payable were already processed in CY 2024, and the amount corresponding withholding taxes totaling P3,973,693.84 were already remitted to the BIR as of April 10, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. City Personnel Officer to prepare the Personnel Schedule of Casual Employees and Plantilla of Casual Appointment using the daily wage rate computed by dividing the corresponding monthly salary rate in the salary schedule by 22 working days pursuant to RA No. 11466 and DBM LBC No. 143; b. City Budget Officer to provide sufficient appropriation for the salaries and wages and benefits of all casual employees based on the salary schedule provided under RA No. 11466 and submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval; and c. City Personnel Officer and City Accountant to pay the salaries and wages of all casual employees at uniform percentage rates of the salary schedule similar to the regular employees of the City Government pursuant to RA No. 11466.
observations.managementAction: The City officials replied that the City Government of Lipa employed 142 casual employees as of March 7, 2024, with 11 of them from the Lipa City Hospital System II. The City granted permanent positions to 22 casual workers and undertook not to hire casual employees, focusing on regularizing eligible casual employees. The current casual employees are ensured a fair level of compensation, with a minimum of 65 percent of the stipulated salary in CY 2024, from 50 percent of previous years’. The City undertakes transitioning towards a workforce comprised entirely of regular employees over the next five years. The City Mayor requested the Sangguniang Panlungsod to approve Supplemental Budget No. 2024-05 in the amount of ₱3,278,934.39 to be taken from savings on personnel services and to be appropriated for the implementation of the first tranche of modified salary schedule for local government personnel pursuant to EO No. 64, Series of 2024.
observations.reason: Despite steps taken (regularization of 22 casual employees and commitment to increase daily wage rates), full implementation of SSL for all casual employees has not yet been achieved; appropriations and funding for compensation were not fully executed in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. City Engineer to conscientiously carry out the detailed engineering of the subsequent projects to be implemented by the City, which include among others the conduct of survey and site investigation, and preparation of site or right of way acquisition plans and resettlement action plans, pursuant to 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, to ensure that the same conditions/observation will not happen again; and b. City Engineer and City Planning and Development Coordinator monitor all infrastructure projects and ensure its timely completion as planned.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Engineer conscientiously carried out the detailed engineering of all projects of the City and ensures that implemented projects were completed as planned. The Office monitored the implementation of all infrastructure projects and ensures its timely completion as planned as evident by the submitted Quarterly Government Programs, Projects and Activities (GPPAs) to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Assessor to prepare the Schedule of Market Values of real properties in the City of Lipa based on the prevailing market prices; propose the necessary revisions on the 2005 Code of Classification, Appraisal and Assessment of Real Property in the City of Lipa and submit to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for appropriate action.
observations.managementAction: The Sangguniang Panlungsod approved on August 9, 2023 the Tax Ordinance No. 3 series of 2023, Ordinance providing for the Revised Schedule of Fair Market Values for Lands, Buildings and Other Improvements for the years 2024-2026. This was approved by the City Mayor on September 22, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee Secretariat to submit a copy of contract and its supporting documents to the Commission on Audit within five working days upon its perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.
observations.managementAction: The copy of contracts and its supporting documents were submitted to the Commission on Audit within five working days upon its perfection. For procurements of goods and services through public bidding, the BAC Secretariat has complied with the COA’s audit recommendation and ensured that contracts and supporting documents are submitted within five (5) working days after contract perfection. For infrastructure projects, submission of required documents for contract review was partially complied with during the first half of the current year; the BAC Chairperson has issued orders to follow the COA’s recommendation and achieve full implementation before yearend. The BAC reviewed and adjusted procedures to ensure documents are reproduced, compiled and transmitted to COA within the prescribed period and expect full compliance for infrastructure procurements scheduled September 2024 and onwards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO), in coordination with the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (CDRRMC), to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP containing the list of programs, projects, activities (PPAs) and sources of funding, whether current year appropriation and special trust funds from unexpended Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) of prior years in the Trust Fund book; and b. CDRRMC to evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs, especially those held under Continuing Appropriations, and identify projects which will no longer be implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-related PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMC initially recommends that the purchase of Communication Equipment, Motor Vehicle and Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment totaling P12,538,388.00 funded out of Continuing Appropriations in CYs 2016-2022 be implemented, while the remaining balances will be recommended for reversion and reprogramming. In addition, the City Accountant already provided the list of savings from completed projects to the City LDRRMO on March 19, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned reports and require the BAC Secretariat to post on the City’s website all related procurement documents.
observations.managementAction: The City Government of Lipa had no official website for the BAC, hence the audit recommendation requiring the BAC to post on the Lipa City’s website all related procurement documents is not doable and cannot be undertaken. The head of the monitoring of the TUPAD program issued certification affirming that the beneficiaries have duly rendered their services. The City also stated that DOLE Region IV-A implicitly consented with the master list of beneficiaries and the inclusion of beneficiaries was based on urgent humanitarian considerations due to COVID-19.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CDRRMO provide a copy of the AIP or supplemental program duly approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and City Mayor for the PPAs for which the Special Trust Fund – LDRRMF amounting to P54,831,402.00 was utilized, to avoid suspension in audit.
observations.managementAction: LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 prepared by the LDRRMO and approved by the City Mayor was submitted to the Audit Team. The City officials informed that the LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 includes various PPAs for social services that were used for emergency response and prevention sourced from the Trust Fund amounting to P54,831,402.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned personnel to render the required reports and provide a plan and measures to safeguard the remaining donated used clothes against loss and wastage.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMO informed that their concerned personnel submitted the required reports to NDRRMC through the Office of Civil Defense (OCD) – Regional Office; annual reports for LDRRMF in CYs 2021 to 2023 were submitted to the OCD – Regional Office and provided to the Audit Team. The Management justified that the donated clothes were properly turned over to beneficiaries and immediately distributed; those used clothes have no economic value.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer review the accuracy of the List of RPT collectibles provided at the beginning of the year and harmonize the same with the Monthly Report of Assessment as of year-end or each assessment roll generated by the City Assessor’s Office.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer provided the list of real property tax collectibles for the year 2023 totaling P677,775,276.72 which was tallied with the two percent of the adjusted assessed value for RPT of P33,888,763,836.00 as of December 31, 2022. The City Treasurer reported that collections in CY 2023 from delinquency accounts were P78,492,176.97. Aging of RPT Delinquent accounts showed 55 percent of delinquents were from recent years CYs 2020-2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record its financial transactions in conformity with IPSAS 1 and in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009, and reclassify the Other Payables account to appropriate liability account as recommended in AAR CY 2019.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant prepared Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) Nos. 2022-01-000295, 2022-01-000297, 2023-01-00321, and 2023-12-002183 reclassifying the affected accounts for the amounts received from NGAs, GOCCs and retention fee from the contractors in the proper/appropriate liability accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to submit a copy of contract and its supporting documents to the Commission on Audit within five working days upon its perfection pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, and require full compliance for infrastructure procurements scheduled September 2024 and onwards.
observations.managementAction: The copy of contracts and its supporting documents were submitted to the Commission on Audit within five working days upon its perfection. For infrastructure projects, the submission of required documents for contract review was partially complied with during the first half of the current year. The BAC Chairperson has issued orders to follow the COA’s recommendation and achieve full implementation before yearend. Adjustments to workflows were made and the BAC expects full compliance for infrastructure contracts scheduled September 2024 and onwards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City General Services Officer to prepare the PIF by extracting the data from the RPCPPE and submit to Audit Team and GIF of the GSIS which will be used as a basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the City’s insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; b. City Budget Officer to include in the City’s annual budget the amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; and c. City General Services Officer to insure with the GIF of the GSIS all of the City’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.
observations.managementAction: The Management replied that the feasibility of insuring the City Government properties needs to be carefully evaluated in conjunction with preparation of inventories and risk assessments. The two-storey LASCA Building and the four-storey Lipa Public Market Parking Building have been assessed by the GSIS with fire insurance billing/premium of P4,780,302.00 and P8,523,867.60, respectively, but were not insured in CY 2023 due to budget constraint and will be included in the budget in CY 2025. The one-time cleansing of PPEs will include inventory of buildings with insurable interest to fully implement insurance of the City’s buildings.
observations.reason: Budget constraints prevented full insurance of all insurable properties; only selected properties were insured and PIF inclusion was partial; further action planned (PIF, budget request, and GSIS inspection).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer: a. publicly advertise for due sale or auction the 453 real properties or a usable portion thereof which are already under Warrant of Levy but remain unpaid; and b. exercise the sale through auction and purchase of real property for want of bidder for other properties after the issuance of Warranty of Levy became futile, as may be necessary to enforce collection of delinquent real property taxes.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer informed that there are other steps that need to be undertaken before public advertisement for sale/auction of real properties (e.g., considerations of civil remedies and implications). The City collected P78,492,176.97 in CY 2023 from delinquency accounts due to fieldwork/ on-site distribution of notices of delinquencies with coordination with barangay officials. Aging of RPT delinquents as of December 31, 2023 showed 55 percent were from recent years (CYs 2020-2023).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the Market Superintendent and City Treasurer to immediately collect delinquencies as well as apply any applicable penalties and automatically cancel lease contracts and proceed to receipt of lease contracts, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Out of the P1,165,094.23 total of market receivables as of December 31, 2018, the City collected P724,769.23 as of December 31, 2023. The remaining balance of P440,325.00 was uncollected due to incapacity to pay of previous stallholders while others could not be located by the City. Lease contracts for the uncollected amounts were canceled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services Officer (GSO) and the Inventory Committee to continue disposing unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines set forth in Supply and Property Management under COA Circular No. 92-836 dated October 20, 1992 and to strictly comply on the provisions of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular No. 2020-006 relative to the disposal of unserviceable, obsolete and/or no longer needed PPEs. Also, we recommended for the GSO and the committee to come up with a timeline for the immediate disposal of assets and unserviceable properties so as to avoid further deterioration and/or wastage and gradually disposes unserviceable properties and submit to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The GSO is continuously disposing the identified unserviceable properties. The GSO has come up with list of unserviceable properties and schedule of disposal and disposes them as soon as the documents are completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. Submit justification or explanation on why retention moneys were not deducted from contractors’ claims/billings; and b. Deduct 10 percent retention money from each progress payment for infrastructure projects up to 50 percent completion of works, if the project is within schedule, otherwise it should be continuously imposed, in compliance with Sections 6.1 and 6.2 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: A letter of explanation was submitted to the Audit Team on March 10, 2023. 10 percent retention money is being deducted on all infrastructure projects starting CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource Management Officer: a. To strengthen the system of internal control in the procurement/hiring of JO personnel by (i) creating written policy and guidelines in the procurement/hiring of JO employees and submit the same to the Audit Team; (ii) requiring the applicants to submit valid proofs of identity and suitability for the job being applied for; and (iii) providing a Statement of Duties and Responsibilities to JO employees to clearly identify their responsibilities and perform their duties efficiently; and b. To require the JO employees to submit monthly accomplishment reports every cut-off to be attached to the payroll.
observations.managementAction: To strengthen the system of internal control in the procurement of personnel under JO Resolution No. 23-05-2694 which approves the guidelines on the hiring of job order workers under contract of service was passed on May 22, 2023. JO employees submitted accomplishment reports every cut-off and are attached to the payroll.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory and Disposal Committee to, in cooperation with all offices concerned, to prioritize the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPEs, including reconciliation of accounting records versus physical count pursuant to the provisions and guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006. We also recommended that the Committee continue disposing unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines set forth in Supply and Property Management under COA Circular No. 92-836 dated October 20, 1992.
observations.managementAction: Unserviceable properties are being disposed gradually.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Office (MENRO) to enjoin the barangay officials in the practice of the 4 Rs (Reduce, Re-use, Recycle and Recovery) and segregation of waste at source in the municipality through strict enforcement of the “no segregation no collection” policy and put the barangay MRFs into full functionality in their respective barangay. Also, the Municipality should implement Ordinance No. 11-08-0034s banning the use of plastic packages, and the MENRO should implement the Information, Education and Communication Campaign regarding environmental compliance and promotion of public awareness and information dissemination drives through seminars, dialogues and use of social and mass media.
observations.managementAction: The MENRO continues its campaign of activation of MRFs on all barangays in the Municipality which also includes submission of report of utilization of MRF thru which the quantities of Waste Received and Processed in the MRF where the quantities of Waste Received and Processed in the MRF are being monitored. The Municipality strictly implemented Ordinance No. 11-08-0034s banning the use of plastic packages. Further, the MENRO implements IEC Campaign regarding environmental compliance.

The GSO staff started to prepare Property Cards for the land owned by the municipality. The GSO coordinated with the Municipal Assessor. Conducted physical count of lots last year. The Municipal Accountant is in the process of compiling the needed program of works, complete details of all Road Network are yet to be furnished to the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant is in the process of compiling the needed program of works and preparing needed documents with justification.
observations.reason: It took time before the Municipal Accountant was provided with the complete information of each lot purchased. Preparation of the Property Ledger Card for each lot purchased is ongoing. Need to have a permanent GSO but due to budgetary constraints, the Municipality cannot appropriate funds for the said position.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records, in order to come up with accurate and reliable reports on the levy of RPT and SET. Submit the status of reconciliation to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer will try to request for monthly delinquent report to be generated in the system to facilitate monthly reconciliation with the Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer will try to request for monthly delinquent report to be generated in the system to facilitate monthly reconciliation with the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Engineer and OIC-Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to submit the justification/explanation indicating the clear purpose of the construction of the two-storey building; 
b. Instruct the OIC-MPDO to submit plans on how the municipality will occupy the building to stop it from being idle; and 
c. Meticulously and judiciously plan all procurement of the municipality and include projects that are considered crucial to the efficient discharge of governmental functions, in compliance with PD No. 1445, Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-003.
observations.managementAction: The COMELEC office occupied the first floor of the building while the 2nd floor will be the LGU's conference room. The Management strictly followed the Audit Team’s recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to employ the use of automated teller machine (ATM) payroll system for the payment of salaries to Job Order employees within three months from receipt thereof to deter possible risks of loss of government funds.
observations.managementAction: JO employees already used ATM payroll system since September 1-15, 2023 for the payment of their salaries.

The GSO staff started to prepare the required RPCPPE as a result of conduct of physical count. The Municipal Engineer is in the process of compiling the needed program of works, complete details of all Road Network are yet to be furnished to the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant is still on the process of preparing the prescribed Property Ledger Card for Local Road Network. The Inventory Committee coordinated with the Municipal Engineer. The Municipal Treasurer is yet to prepare the Road Map and maintain a complete Report on the Local Road Networks. The Municipality plans to update property cards and reconciliation is ongoing.
observations.reason: Reconciliation process is still on-going; the Municipal Accountant and staff are undermanned; some documents/references are hard to locate; budgetary constraints to hire permanent GSO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to inform the concerned suppliers for the warranty deposit and require them to prepare a letter request regarding the return/refund of the amount due them of which their claim shall be supported with the photocopy of the disbursement voucher showing such amount was deducted. In case, the concerned suppliers were not anymore existing or no response was received from them, effect the necessary adjustment in the LGU’s financial statement by debiting the account Guaranty Deposit Payable and crediting the account Prior Year’s Adjustment.
observations.managementAction: We sent letters to suppliers with outstanding guaranty deposit. Some suppliers changed their business name and no available Official Receipt. In CY 2023, management received responses from several suppliers and claims were processed. Where suppliers no longer existing or no response, adjustments will be effected as recommended.
observations.reason: Some suppliers changed their business name and no available official receipt. Also, some old files/vouchers to support their deposits are missing.

observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was able to prepare the required RPCPPE as a result of conducting physical count. The Municipal Engineer is compiling program of works. The Municipal Accountant is preparing the prescribed Property Ledger Card for Local Road Network. The Municipality acknowledged the need to hire a separate GSO but due to budgetary constraints cannot appropriate funds for the said position immediately.
observations.reason: Undermanned staff; difficulties in locating old records and documents; budgetary constraints to hire permanent GSO; reconciliation of records is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to determine the nature and amount of acquisition of these lots, as basis of valuation;
b. Municipal Accountant to recognize in the Municipality’s books the unrecorded parcels of land using the market value indicated in the earliest applicable Tax Declarations under the name of the Municipality; and
c. Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Assessor include in the CY 2024 appropriations the titling of land owned by the Municipal Government.
observations.managementAction: The titling of land owned by the Municipality is already included in the CY 2024 budget with a total amount of P1,413,800.00. The Municipal Assessor and staff are in the process of identifying and reconciling the acquired lots.
observations.reason: Process of identification/reconciliation is ongoing; some documentary references are difficult to locate.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the necessary adjustments to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed penalties.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is still in the process of determining the affected employees on the unremitted old balance of GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG due to lack of old reference. Most of the unremitted amount pertains to employees that are no longer in service. The Municipality also admitted difficulty in locating old files because some were damaged or spoiled.
observations.reason: Difficult to identify the affected employees on the unremitted old balances due to lack of old reference; some old files damaged or spoiled.
