When no meetings are held, they report to the Office of the Provincial Governor to assist in carrying out other programs of the government. Ensure that these Local Economic Enterprise (LEE)/Other Economic Enterprise (OEE) are self-sufficient thus, expected to earn profits or at least recover costs.
observations.managementAction: The employees submitted accomplishment reports which were mainly to assist in the meetings between the Provincial Governor and various stakeholders related to the operation of the BPLC. No hiring of additional personnel was made during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial Accountant and the Provincial Treasurer to reconcile their respective records and establish the correct amount of collections and income from BPLC operations in previous years, prepare the necessary adjusting entries, if warranted; and maintain only one bank account for BPLC operation to ensure correctness of the closing information and to avoid erroneous entries.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation was already made by the Accounting Office and the journal entries were made under JEV Nos. 2021-09-037313 and 2022-01-00041 dated September 30, 2021 and January 3, 2022, respectively. Closing of depository account was not yet implemented pending approval by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.
observations.reason: Closing of depository account was not yet implemented pending approval by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor: a. direct the Provincial School Board (PSB) to allocate sufficient funds for the adoption of the blended/distance learning modalities, and ensure optimum utilization thereof; and b. coordinate with the DepEd, and with the Local School Board of the different municipalities to determine the actual needs of the public schools which were not provided by them especially those belonging to lower class municipalities to augment the MOOE of their respective schools and provide sufficient fund for the disbursements thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Provincial Government has already distributed a total no. of 1,895 laptops to different schools in the Province. Proper coordination with the Local School Board of the different municipalities are implemented thru email/ new media platforms and other ways to address all the needs and ensure optimum utilization based on appropriate guidelines by proper authorities.
observations.reason: Full utilization of the budget cannot be attained due to the ongoing pandemic which restrained face to face classes and eventually resulted in reduction of operating expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor: a. direct the Provincial Planning and Development Coordinator (PPDC) and Provincial Engineering Office (PEO) to ensure 100 percent implementation of planned activities under the 20 percent Development Fund considering that funds are available for these activities; and b. instruct the PPDC to conduct regular coordination with the implementing offices, and only include procurement-and-implementation-ready projects under the Development Fund PPAs to ensure that they are undertaken with the target period. We also recommended that the Province direct the PEO, LDC and other offices to review and realign PPAs as necessary.
observations.managementAction: The PEO explained that in spite of various setbacks in the implementation of PPAs under 20 per cent DF, they are doing their best to attain 100 per cent implementation/completion of the said projects. They also stated that the Audit Team’s Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) is always noted as it will be their guiding principle in the implementation of all PPAs. Meanwhile, the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) submitted a letter to the PEO, copy furnished the Audit Team, requesting the latter to submit only PPAs for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) that are classified according to types of projects and prioritized/selected with no underlying issues, and considered as readily implementable in order to achieve optimal utilization of the fund.
observations.reason: Projects were realigned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the PDRRMO to prepare a comprehensive and reliable LDRRMFIP pursuant with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and submit the same; and that the Provincial Governor require the PDRRMC to provide sufficient coordinated efforts among stakeholders and submit justification for charges against the LDRRMF improperly charged.
observations.managementAction: Projects to be implemented under the LDRRMF were provided in detail in the PDRRMC/SP Resolutions issued for the utilization of the fund. Justification submitted for the charging of the inappropriate amount and the Province committed to avoid unnecessary LDRRMF charges. PDRRMC, PDRRMO and PEO are continuously coordinating; projects were realigned accordingly.
observations.reason: Planned programs were re-aligned and coordinated among concerned stakeholders to allow strategic planning and implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. Provincial Social Welfare and Development Office (PSWDO) to submit consolidated reports using the filed Acknowledgement Receipts for all in-kind donations received and Issuance Forms for the distribution of goods made to various beneficiaries during the relief activities specifically in the designated evacuation centers; b. Provincial Treasurer to account the receipts and utilization of cash donations to be extracted from the Trust Fund cashbook, open a separate bank account for the unexpended balance of the fund and reconcile the same with accounting records; c. PDRRMO to warrant preparation of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balance for submission to the concerned offices to enable the General Services Office to prepare Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM for each kind of relief goods using the prescribed format; and d. concerned offices to adhere strictly with the prescribed procedures and guidelines relative to the donations in-kind and in cash as enunciated by COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The PSWDO submitted the list of donations in-kind and the Issuance Forms for the distribution of the goods made to diverse units of beneficiaries during the relief activities. A separate Cash in Bank account was already maintained by the Province effective September 2, 2021. The Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balance was prepared by the Provincial Social Welfare and Development Office and not by the PDRRMO. Management assured that in future undertakings, the PDRRMO in coordination with the PSWDO will prepare the required report using the prescribed format.
observations.reason: The PSWDO prepared and submitted the required lists; a separate bank account was opened; PDRRMO will coordinate with PSWDO for future compliance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to: a. submit explanation for the amount of P18,286,970.90 which was presented as Actual Cost or Expenditure for the 'Provision of Financial Assistance for Community Development Projects' with budget amount of P5,411,006.25 whereas no actual results/accomplishments were indicated in the GAD Accomplishment Report; b. submit justifications for the 13 unimplemented PPAs in Annex B of CY 2020 AAR wherein no reasons for non-implementation were provided; and c. innovate ways for the implementation of programs under the new normal and make use of the virtual/social platforms to be able to optimally achieve the goal of a GAD-responsive governance and environment even at times of quarantine brought by the pandemic.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted explanation/justification for the amount cited. Trainings/seminars were implemented online/virtually.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the: a. Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) to ensure sufficiency of the employee’s net pay after deducting the loan amortizations prior to approving the loan applications of employees; b. Provincial Accountant to deduct the correct amount of premiums and loan amortizations due, to avoid incurrence of penalties and remit immediately to GSIS; and c. AAO and the Provincial Accountant to coordinate with the GSIS for the settlement of the outstanding balance of personal share (PS) and government share (GS) totaling P5,396,984.09.
observations.managementAction: The AAO is properly checking the net pay of employees before approving the loan applications. The Accounting Office has been coordinating with GSIS for proper deduction, remittance and posting. The unpaid loan amortizations pertain to loans which have been granted or have matured even before the borrower’s employment with the Province. For current loans, proper coordination is implemented by the AAO and Accounting Office with the GSIS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial Accountant to submit a request for write-off to the Commission on Audit for the dormant accounts totaling P3,782,293.15 and continue reconciliation with the implementing offices to account the difference of P33,106.00 and effect corrections in the appropriate records and support the request for write-off with required supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Copy of demand letters were submitted by the Office of the Provincial Agriculturist and the Provincial Cooperative, Livelihood and Entrepreneurial Development Office (PCLEDO) to the Provincial Accountant. Adjustments were made for the total collections of P264,450.00 which were erroneously credited by the Accounting Office to Trust Liabilities instead of Loans Receivable. The Provincial Accountant is still awaiting documents from the ProVet Office before submitting them to COA for the request for write off.
observations.reason: The Provincial Accountant is still waiting for the required documents from the ProVet Office before submitting the request for write-off to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor provide explanation or justification why the contract was titled as Development Contract instead of Contract of Sale.
observations.managementAction: The Provincial Government submitted explanation on why the contract was titled as Development Contract.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require:
a. the City Accountant to take immediate and appropriate actions to adjust the PPE unaccounted/auctioned/demolished and unidentified beginning balances totalling P529,645,061.10 these items have always been among the factors why discrepancies of PPE records and RPCPPE. Likewise, oblige her to act immediately on the necessary steps to have the auctioned/sold and demolished properties since these properties are no longer in the possession of the City Government, thus should not have been reported as among its assets for it would mislead users of the City’s financial statements; and
b. the City Engineer to provide other supporting documents such as but not limited to approved demolition permits pursuant to PD No. 1096 and its IRR, copy furnished the Audit Team for evaluation of the documents, if there are any. Update the Audit Team of the status of compliance with the required documentation, and if these are not available, provide justification with corresponding certification as to the unavailability of documents pertinent thereto; and
c. the Inventory Committee of the GSO to identify/locate the Flood Control System properties and to exclude from the RPCPPE the property items or costs incurred by the City Government for the repairs and maintenance of Christmas lamp posts/lights.
observations.managementAction: On February 24 and 26 December 1, 2021, a total of P130,212,489.77 was dropped from the books per JEV Nos. 2021-02-012898, 006514, 006550, 006570, 009779, 009839, 009855, 011224, 011229, 012234, 011237, 013409, 013422, 013431 and JEV No. 2021-12-001102. Of this amount, P21,854,034.28 is under Special Education Fund (SEF). The City Accountant, thru her letter dated April 20, 2022 received by the Audit Team on April 22, 2022, noted that they are continuously exerting efforts to reconcile the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts with the GSO. A new Accountant was hired to focus on the monitoring of the PPE accounts and the total amount of P130,212,249.77 was adjusted in year 2021 from the variance as of December 31, 2020. She added that once the office receives the valid supporting documents for the write-off of all the auctioned, demolished and unserviceable PPEs, necessary adjusting journal entries will be effected. The creation of the Inventory Committee will be a great help to account the undetermined and unaccounted PPE beginning balances. All items will be adjusted this year. Further, during the exit conference on April 21, 2022, the City Officials agreed on the recommendation on the one-time cleansing of PPE within the year 2022.
observations.reason: Large number of items to be validated before dropping, and the unavailability of staff and time allotted for the task, plus the effects on work schedule brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. require the City Accountant, City Treasurer and the implementing cashiers departments, CSWDO, OCVAS and CPDO to continuously exert efforts to locate the supporting documents and to document all procedures employed to establish the location or whereabouts of the debtors and borrowers involved including the actions taken to collect the amounts due from them; and
b. instruct concerned City Officials to explore other appropriate actions, even filing of appropriate case, if necessary, against erring borrowers to collect the large amount of the City’s receivables which could be used to finance other priority projects/programs/activities of the City; increase of 71.43% in the collection of Loan Receivables-Others as compared to 2020 collection.
In addition, reconcile records of the Loans Receivable - Others account so that the records being maintained by both offices reflect the same account balance.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Social Welfare and Development (CSWDO) and Office of the City Veterinary and Agricultural Services (OCVAS) sought the assistance of the barangay chairpersons on the delivery of demand letters to debtors to enforce collection. The Audit Team also recommended other several remedial measures including the request for approval to the concerned City officials to rid from the books the invalid receivables after exhausting all possible actions and efforts to collect but up to year-end, the concerned City officials and employees has yet to submit the set of documents to write-off the dormant receivables. The Office of the City Accountant is coordinating with CSWDO (for capital assistance), OCVAS (for individual/coop loans, and for cattle fattening project) for the reconciliation of records. The CSWDO has sought the assistance of the barangay chairpersons in the delivery of demand letters to debtors (beneficiaries of Tulong Puhunan/Capital Assistance Project). Such effort has brought an increase of 71.43% in the collection of Loan Receivables-Others as compared to 2020 collection. The City Accountant will continuously coordinate with the CSWDO, OCVAS and CPDO for the reconciliation of records. Verification by the Audit Team revealed that debtors disclosed that they have already paid their loans. However, reconciliation of records cannot be done due to unavailability of documents on both offices.
observations.reason: Same as in previous page.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. require the OIC-City Market Administrator under the supervision of City Treasurer to strictly implement the prohibition on occupying more than one stall per stallholder and sub-leasing as enunciated in the Revenue Code of the City.
b. recommend to the Sangguniang Panlungsod to revisit the Batangas City Revenue Code of 2009 for any necessary amendment of the provisions related to Market.
c. require the Market Administrator and City Treasurer to submit the List/Schedule of Tenants/Lessees with delinquent accounts and with one month unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2020 to the City Accountant for recording as Accounts Receivable; and
d. require the Market Administrator to submit pertinent data for the Contract of Lease with the inclusion in the Contract of Lease the details of the stall’s floor area and the monthly rental fee.
observations.managementAction: The OIC City Treasurer commented, thru a letter dated April 18, 2022, that proper coordination with the Office of the City Market Administrator will be done and will propose necessary amendment to the Revenue Code relative to market operations. On the other hand, on her letter to the Audit Team dated April 19, 2022, the City Market Administrator said that their office will act on this matter since they always remind the stallholders of their responsibility and accountability given the right as lessee in the public market. Further the Office of the City Market will review the list of all stallholders of the three public markets and will call the attention of those tenants with more than one stall to strictly follow the market code, thereby imposing them the rightful sanction. The City Market Administrator is open for the strict implementation, abide and adhere to what is proper for the City Market Office as one of the income generating institution of the government. The OIC-City Market Administrator and City Treasurer submitted the List/Schedule of Tenants/Lessees with delinquent accounts and one month unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2020 to the City Accountant for recording in the books and necessary JEV was prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a. the City Accountant to identify/evaluate the discrepancies and to draw the necessary journal entry thru a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reflect reconciled balances of the records; reclassify “expensed-outright” items and/or “lump-summed” inventory to represent actual inventories on hand.
b. require all Inventory Custodians to maintain complete and updated supplies and materials stock cards to account the receipt and disposition of inventory and to facilitate reconciliation of the Accounting and City General Services Office (CGSO) records;
c. instruct the City Accountant and the City General Services Officer or Inventory Custodians to have regular reconciliation of their records to immediately discover and reconcile discrepancies, if any; and
d. require the City Accountant to include disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements regarding the amounts of inventories recognized as an expense during the period.
observations.managementAction: On January 23, May 31, June 1 and August 1, 2021, a total of P4,767,558.33 adjusting per JEV Nos. 2021-01-002484, 2021-05-025007, 2021-06-025271 and 2021-08-029250 were made to reclassify “expensed-outright” items and/or “lump-summed” inventory to represent actual inventories on hand. The City Accountant and the City General Services Officer continue to have regular reconciliation with concerned offices. The City Accountant disclosed the amounts of inventories recognized as expenses in the Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2021. The reconciliation of records between concerned offices is still ongoing.
observations.reason: Ongoing reconciliation of records between concerned offices; conduct of inventory was not yet completed due to COVID-19 pandemic restrictions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. require the City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO), City Health Office (CHO) and Office of the City Veterinarian and Agricultural Services (OCVAS) to conduct physical inventory of all undistributed and unconsumed inventories on hand and to prepare and submit the reports to the City Accounting Office for proper recording of Inventory balances; and
b. require the City Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting journal entry thru the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the Inventory balances for the actual inventories on hand at year-end.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant prepared and recorded JEV-2021-08-029411 and its issuance (April to June 2021) per JEV-2021-08-029419, both JEVs dated August 2, 2021. Subsequently, OCVAS and CHO submitted also their Report on Physical Count of Inventories as of December 31, 2021. The report was recorded per JEV-2021-08-029411. The City Accountant will check, reconcile and make adjusting JEV, as necessary.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require immediate bonding of all the Inventory Custodians, in compliance with the provisions of Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.
observations.managementAction: The GSO and Accounting Office prepared the list of accountable officers and employees including the Inventory Custodians and a total of five (5) Inventory Custodians were immediately bonded effective April 22, 2021. The Accountable Form Custodian of City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) is bonded effective February 24, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. require the Agency Authorized Officers (AAOs) to review and continuously review and evaluate the noted non-payments and underpayments of mandated GSIS premium contributions and in-default loan payments of several active employees of the City Government of Batangas. Thereafter, coordinate with the GSIS Batangas Branch for reconciliation of the records of both offices; and
b. instruct the City Accountant to continuously coordinate with the AAOs to ensure that the mandated and correct GSIS premium contributions and/or loan amortizations are deducted regularly and correctly/accurately to prevent unnecessary interests and penalties, possible disqualification of entitlement to other loans and benefits and possible delays on the release of any benefits due to the concerned employees.
observations.managementAction: The AAOs and the Office of the City Accountant had a meeting with the GSIS Batangas to reconcile records. The City Accountant transferred the necessary corrections and updated the records of Batangas City in which separate records for employees currently connected with the City Government and those who have already retired and deceased were maintained.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. direct the BAC Chairman to require the BAC Secretariat and IT Department to facilitate the posting of the awarded contract and other documentation/information for the procurement of goods concerning the related COVID-19 response in the Agency website and GPPB Online Portal;
b. require the BAC Secretariat to monitor posting requirements that were given to the IT Department for posting; and
c. instruct the BAC Secretariat to document their postings of awarded contracts and other relevant information that were posted in three conspicuous places.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Chairman replied that the BAC Secretariat required the BAC Secretariat to facilitate the required documents and has already assigned a member of the BAC Secretariat that will monitor the posting and publication of all awarded contracts. The Approved APP was posted on GPPB website, agency website and conspicuous places. The PPMP is published in the agency website and in conspicuous places.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to cause immediate appropriate actions on the result of the technical evaluation made by the COA-TAS and submit to the Audit Team the corresponding actions taken necessary to correct such deficiencies. Failure to correct said deficiencies would lead to appropriate audit action by the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The CEO submitted justifications related to said projects. Justifications have been submitted to COA to answer the technical evaluation made by the Technical Audit Specialist. The CEO continuously reviewed all costing of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LDRRM Council headed by the City Mayor to:
a. evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the proper identification and selection of specific priority PPAs to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the City regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management; and
b. require the CDRRMO to include in the LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be charged against the unexpended appropriation of previous years, continuing appropriations and those financial assistance received from the National Government for affected areas of calamities.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRM Officer replied that they are currently on the process of updating the LDRRMFIP for the year 2022 in order to achieve the goal of providing disaster risk reduction and management services for constituents. They will be coordinating with the Budget and Accounting Offices and will include the PPAs to be charged to the Continuing Fund and Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to ensure that proper costing on every components or details of each infrastructure project be adopted to ensure reasonableness on the cost of every project thereby establishing integrity of all the projects being completed by the City Government.
observations.managementAction: Justifications have been submitted to COA to answer the technical evaluation made by the Technical Audit Specialist. The CEO continuously reviewed all costing of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, and the OIC-City Treasurer to exert utmost effort to trace the details/whereabouts and locate any record / information relative to the outstanding non-current receivables of P56,551,672.07 to substantiate its continued classification under the Receivables accounts; and for them to analyze and make appropriate actions necessary, even filing criminal case against erring borrowers so that the collection of the City at the said amount could be recovered. Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to assign personnel specifically to handle receivable accounts of the City and to design and establish a manual or computerized system that would provide detailed processes for approval, recording and reporting of Receivables accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant recognized the Audit Team’s observation that most of these receivable accounts were in their books accounts, but they have difficulty locating the documents supporting these accounts as they were recorded years ago. She promised though that they would still exert their effort to locate some, if not all, and if all else fail, they will act accordingly to clear the books of undocumented accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor cause provision of a suitable and well-ventilated storage room where medicines and medical supplies may be stored in order to attain the required temperature and other conditions for storage, thereby preserving the quality and potency of drugs and medicines for the best interest of the general public.
observations.managementAction: The City Health Office claimed there is really an intention to improve their office, including the stockroom, but the fastest way to do it is beyond their means.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterate our recommendations that the City Mayor require:

a. the City Accountant to cause the appropriate actions on PPE accounts
captioned as unaccounted/auctioned/demolished and unidentified
beginning balances totaling P473,524,987.85 as these items have always
been among the factors why discrepancies of PPE records and reports
persisted. Likewise, oblige her to act immediately on the necessary steps
to have all auctioned/sold and demolished properties be taken off from the
books as said properties are no longer in the possession of the City
Government, thus should not have been reported as among its assets for
it would misled users of the City’s financial statements; and

b. the City Engineer, to provide other supporting documents such as but not
limited to approved demolition permits pursuant to Presidential Decree
(PD) No. 1096 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR), copy
furnished the Audit Team for evaluation of the documents. Update the
Audit Team of the status of the required documentation, and if these are
not available, provide justification with corresponding certification as to
the unavailability of documents pertinent thereto.

c. the Inventory Committee of the GSO to restore in its RPCPPE all properties
with value of P10,000 to P15,000.00 per item totalling P140,120,004.24 as
there has been no accounting guidelines yet revising the capitalization of
properties for local government agencies into P15,000.00 and above per
item.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, City
Treasurer and the CSWDO to document all procedures employed to establish
the location or whereabouts of the debtors involved including the actions
taken to collect the amounts due them, and make appropriate actions
necessary, even filing criminal case against erring borrowers so that the large
amount of City funds would be recovered. If all actions are fully documented,
then the City Accountant may seek approval from the concerned City officials
for the appropriate remedial measures to rid the books of invalid receivable
accounts so as to attain fair presentation of the said accounts in the City’s
financial statements.

Likewise, require the Accounting Office and the concerned offices to reconcile
their records to reflect similar balances of Loans Receivables - Others account
so that the records being maintained by both offices is reflective of the true
and correct balances of the Receivable accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to
immediately transfer and record back in the City’s books of accounts all
infrastructure assets recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure totaling
P381,226,280.38, charged these assets with depreciation and fully disclose
these information in the Notes to Financial Statements for proper valuation,
and fair presentation in the City’s financial statements.

Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor require the following
concerned city officials of strict compliance with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2015-008, wherein:
a. the City Engineer shall provide the City Accountant and the General
Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of all
road projects completed by the City Government including those still in the
Registry of Public Infrastructure, which are to be transferred and recorded
back to the City’s books of accounts;
b. the General Services Officer to coordinate with the City Engineer in the
conduct of yearly physical inventory of local road network projects, and
cause submission of the Report on Local Road Network (RLRN) and Report
on the Physical Count of Local Road Network System (RPCLRN) in the
format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008, and maintain Local Road
Inventory and Road Map;
c. the City Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and
road components for every road network; and
d. both the City Accountant and General Services Officer to reconcile the
record per book and the result of physical inventory at the end of each year
so as to ensure existence and accuracy of road networks presented in the
financial statements of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CENRO and the TDRO to
comply strictly with all the provisions contained in the E-trike Deployment Plan
as approved by the Asian Development Bank (ADB) to ensure that the purpose
for which the deployment of e-trike to its intended recipients would be in
accordance with the attainment of a healthy and eco-friendly transport sector
in Batangas City thru the reduction of Greenhouse Gas emission.

Re-visit the criteria and the selection processes set by the CENRO and TDRO,
which were used as basis in the granting of e-trike to its recipients and
determine whether these are in accordance with the E-trike Deployment Plan
for the City Government. Thereafter, have these guidelines approve either thru
an executive order of the City Mayor or an ordinance/resolution by the
Sanggunian so that legitimacy of the process would be established.

Secure authorization from the Department of Energy on the new E-Trike
Deployment Plan dated December 31, 2019, specifically on the revision of cost
to be paid by the recipients/beneficiaries of the said project.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC to prepare the
Procurement Monitoring Report in the format required of GPPB Circular No.
03-2017 and submit to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within
fourteen (14) calendar days after the end of each semester pursuant to the
RIRR of RA No. 9184.

Likewise, submit the APP-CSE in the revised format provided under GPPB
Circular No. 07-2015 on or before November 15 of every year to the DBM-PS in
accordance with DBM Circular Letter No. 2013-14.

We reiterate our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the City’s GAD
Focal Point System (GFPS) to ensure that the gender issues reported in the
GPB and the GAD Accomplishment Report are supported by sex-
disaggregated data or relative gender information. Said information is
necessary to justify the issues cited as gender related issues, where correct
application of intervention thru various GAD related activities is needed to
address unequal opportunities for men and women.

Likewise, the corresponding GAD objectives in every gender issues should
address the very core of the gender issues where it must be specific so as to
ensure that said gender issues would be addressed appropriately through
correct interventions or undertaking of various activities.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and
Development Officer to submit explanation/justification on why purchases of
said machineries and equipment had been undertaken despite the
disallowance made by the Local Finance Committee of the Provincial
Government as embodied in the enacted Sangguniang Panlalawigan
Resolution No. 163 dated February 18, 2019.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to cause
immediate appropriate actions on the result of technical evaluation noted by the
COA-TAS on the remaining eight projects, and submit explanations/justifications
to the Audit Team for appropriate actions necessary on the matter.

Likewise, require the City Engineer to ensure that proper costing on every
components or details of each infrastructure projects proposed by the City be
adopted to ensure reasonableness on the cost of every projects thereby
establishing integrity of all the projects being completed by the City Government.

observations.managementAction:
The GSO Partially submitted result of reconciliation of accounting and GSO records.
The City Accountant reasoned out that they are coordinating with the City Treasury and the City Social Welfare Development Office for the resolution of inadequacy of supporting the subject receivables accounts, and if it would yield negative results then appropriate action would be undertaken by her Office to obtain fairness in the presentation of receivables accounts in the financial statements of the City.
The City Engineer submitted the report on demolished property on San Pedro ES.
Not See Audit Implemented Finding No. 1 in Part II of this Report.
The Inventory Committee of the GSO submitted the list of reconciling items found during the reconciliation of accounting and GSO records.
observations.reason:
Not Lack of manpower
Due to eruption of Taal Volcano in January and the current pandemic the City Engineer and her staff are busy in the relief efforts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor stop approving the charges of financial assistance or subsidy allowances for students or subsidy allowances for high school students under its Scholarship Program out of the appropriation of SEF, as such expense is not one of the allowable expenses provided for under Section 272 of RA No. 7160, and its IRR, as embodied under JC No. 1 s 2017 of the DBM, DepEd, & DILG, where such prohibition had already been declared by the Supreme Court through its Decision, G.R. No. 141386.
Likewise, require the Local School Board to ensure that expenditures charged against the SEF include only those programs or projects provided for under JC No. 1 s. 2017 so as to protect the welfare of school children, who are the sole beneficiaries of the Fund.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor directed the concerned City officials to stop charging the SEF for the subsidy allowance as recommended and the charges of subsidy allowances to students for CY 2019 has been immediately stopped.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the required members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), including the Technical Working Group (TWG), the OIC-City Accountant and all other concerned City officials to submit justifications with relevant supporting documents on why the grant of Fringe Benefits to all City officials and employees in the total amount of P24,495,000.00 was made and to enable the Audit Team determine the appropriate audit action relative to the subject ND. Otherwise, the grant of the so called "Fringe Benefits" to all City officials and employees in the stated amount may be considered as irregular disbursements/expenditures.
observations.managementAction: A letter dated March 25, 2019 was sent to the Audit Team detailing their explanations on the grant of Fringe Benefits. The City submitted their response to the subject ND.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), including the Technical Working Group (TWG), the OIC-City Accountant and all other concerned City officials to submit justifications with relevant supporting documents on why the 87 infrastructure projects of the City passed thru the eligibility and evaluation of bids and the post-qualification despite incomplete submission of documents in the opening of bids as contained in the first envelope, where non-compliance should have merited a rating of “failed”, hence, ineligible to participate in the bidding for the projects. Likewise, require the BAC and TWG to adopt the updated set of Bidding Documents under the 5th Edition of the Philippine Bidding Documents which has been prepared by the Government of the Philippines in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184, as amended, and ensure strict compliance of its provisions in the succeeding conduct of public bidding for completeness, veracity and reliability of information/documents and bidding results.
observations.managementAction: The BAC has adopted the new set of Bidding Documents based on the documents supporting the succeeding bidding of infra projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City’s GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to ensure that the gender issues reported in the GPB and the GAD Accomplishment Report are supported by sex-disaggregated data or relative gender information. Said information is necessary to justify the issues cited as gender related issues, where correct application of intervention thru various GAD related activities is needed to address unequal opportunities for men and women. Likewise, the corresponding GAD objectives in every gender issues should address the very core of the gender issues where it must be specific so as to ensure that said gender issues would be addressed appropriately through correct interventions or undertaking of various activities.
observations.managementAction:
The City Planning and development Coordinator claimed that "the City GFPS specifically its TWG are really amenable to the OA findings as reflected in the AOM No. 20202-007(19) that there should be more improvement and strategies to be done to fully comply with the recommendations of the Commission on Audit."
Likewise, he emphasized that "the City GAD Focal Point System, through its TWG is committed to correct and improve the formulation of the City’s Gender and Development Budget and Plan (GBP) for the succeeding years and will continuously remind all concerned agencies to ensure that the gender issues reported in the GBP and GAD Annual Accomplishment Report are supported by sex-disaggregated data and other gender data focusing on gender related issues."

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the respective parents of the students receiving financial assistance under the City’s Scholarship Program to submit sworn statements of their gross annual income in accordance with the requirement under Section 2 of EO No. 4, s. 2017 so as to validate whether the students are indeed eligible to receive such assistance from the City Government. Likewise, review the criteria for which a student may qualify for the City’s scholarship program and consider adopting criteria appropriate to current situation.
observations.managementAction: The concerned City officials promised to address this concern and to look into the matter as time permits given other pressing City concerns (eruption of Taal Volcano and COVID19 pandemic).
observations.reason: With the eruption of Taal Volcano in January and the current pandemic the Secretariat Audit Team has limited time for the verification of the voluminous documents related to the Scholarship Program of the Government.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor stop making requisitions of accountable forms directly to the National Printing Office by the Market Administrator as such function is delegated to the Office of the City Treasurer in consonance with the provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 92-382.
observations.managementAction: The Market Administrator has transferred the custody of accountable forms to the Office of the City Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC, the OIC-City Accountant, and all other concerned City officials to submit justifications with supporting documents on why the said procurement of infrastructure projects was processed and recommended for award despite violation of RA No. 9184 and RA No. 7160 as discussed. Likewise, direct the BAC and TWG to ensure strict compliance with the RIRR of RA No. 9184 whereby Tax Clearance is included in the Technical requirement for the first envelope to be submitted by the bidders, pursuant to GPPB Resolution No. 21-2013 dated July 31, 2013 so as to guarantee that all prospective bidders in every government projects are compliant with all existing tax laws, rules and regulations. Also require that the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) supported the said projects be incorporated in the Annual Procurement Program (APP) for Calendar Year 2015; and other deficiencies be addressed.
observations.managementAction: In a letter dated May 26, 2020 and in reaction to AOM No. 2020-004-100(18) dated March 9, 2020, the City Engineer asked for more time to answer the said memo and requested time extension until June 26, 2020 to provide necessary justification on the said project. The City Engineer requested for more time to answer as her Office is busy attending to the needs of the City Government in addressing the current pandemic.
observations.reason: Tax Clearance is included in the Technical legal requirement for the first envelope to be submitted by the bidders. The City Engineer requested extension due to transfer of office and COVID19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor direct the:

h. Inventory Committee and City Accounting Office to exhaust all efforts in the
determination of the particulars, whereabouts, and any documents or
information pertaining to the unreconciled/unaccounted properties of
P142,118,246.25, and those properties captioned only as beginning balances
in lump-sum amount only under each PPE accounts totaling P170,723,893.97.
Make certain that these discrepancies, which totalled to P312,845,140.22
would be lessened, as years pass by so that eventual elimination of these
unidentified properties could be achieved in the coming years, preferably
sooner than later so as to reflect fair balances of PPE accounts. If necessary,
require the submission from persons in charge of the PPE items of all the
procedures adopted by them in the reconciliation process in order to
document the process and move forward to the next possible course of action;

i. City Accounting Office to assign one or two personnel in her Office who will
focus only on the evaluation and analysis of all properties already auctioned or
disposed of, which totalled to P123,249,499.83 as found by the Inventory
Committee of the GSO and effect adjustments, if found accurate and correct, in
the books of accounts so as to eliminate the presence of these kind of
properties;

j. City Engineering Office to submit the required documentation in support to the
demolished properties of P11,348,775.14 to the IC-GSO and the City
Accounting Office for them to effect appropriate action thereof;

k. City Engineer to submit to the Audit Team all necessary documents and
information pertaining to the demolished properties, pursuant to PD No. 1096
and its IRR, and RA No. 7160 so that corresponding analysis of these
documents could be made to determine the City’s compliance with the proper
procedures on property disposal, and for transparency and proper
accountability of such undertaking; and

l. Inventory Committee and City Accounting Office to submit to COA the
updates/development of the reconciliation of their records every semester for
verification and monitoring purposes.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor require the OIC-City
Accountant, and the OIC-City Treasurer to exert utmost effort to trace the
details/whereabouts and locate any record / information / documents relative to
the outstanding non-current receivables of P56,551,672.07 to substantiate its
continued classification under the Receivables accounts; and for them to analyze
and make appropriate actions necessary, even filing criminal case against erring
borrowers so that the collection of the said amount could be recovered.

Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to
assign personnel specifically to handle receivable accounts of the City and to
design and establish a manual or computerized system that would provide
detailed processes for approval, recording and reporting of Receivables accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor stop approving the payment of financial
assistance or subsidy allowances for high school students under its Scholarship
Program out of the appropriation of SEF, as such expense is not one of the
allowable expenses provided under Section 272 of RA No. 7160, and its IRR, as
embodied under JC No. 01 s. 2017 of the DBM, DepEd, & DILG, where such
prohibition had already been declared by the Supreme Court through its Decision,
G.R. No. 141386.

Likewise, require the Local School Board to ensure that expenditures charged
against the SEF include only those programs or projects provided under JC No. 1
s. 2017 so as to protect the welfare of school children, who are the sole
beneficiaries of the Fund.

Considering that the City has been appropriating huge amount for its Scholarship
Program out of the General Fund, it is best that all expenses related to it be
charged thereat, and let the SEF be utilized only for the purpose for which it was
created.

Henceforth, with the SC’s en banc Decision, G.R. No. 141386, on the prohibition of
charging the cost of scholarship program against SEF, all officials who allowed
and cause the utilization of P4,022,000.00 be required to refund the full amount so that the
real objectives of setting up SEF would be attained in order not to deprive the intended
beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Committee tasked to oversee
the implementation of the City’s Scholarship Program to strictly enforce the
required qualifications for a student to become a recipient of the Program as
specifically stated under Sections 1 and 2 of Executive Order (EO) No. 4, s. 2017,
so that the reliability of the Program would not be compromised with the non-
disclosure of the true combined gross income of parents of students, and of
several scholars who are also recipients of other scholarship program.

Likewise, require the respective parents of the students receiving financial
assistance under the City’s Scholarship Program to submit sworn statements of
their gross annual income in accordance with the requirement under Section 2 of
EO No. 4, s. 2017 so as to validate whether the students are indeed eligible to
receive such assistance from the City Government.

Lastly, a review of the criteria for which a student may qualify for the City’s
scholarship program may consider to adopt to the current situation, where the
real intention of giving assistance to students in need of ways to ease the burden
of their parents in sending them to school may be addressed appropriately.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials to submit
sufficient legal basis by which monetary reward called Fringe Benefits may be
given to all City officials and employees so as to ascertain the appropriate audit
action that the Audit Team may take based on the circumstances presented.

Otherwise, the grant of the so called “Fringe Benefits” to all city officials and
employees in the total amount of P24,495,000.00 may be considered as irregular
disbursements/expenditures as not in accordance with the provisions of RA No.
6758, and COA Circular Nos. 2013-003 and 2012-003.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the members of the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC), including the Technical Working Group (TWG), the OIC-City
Accountant and all other concerned City officials to submit explanations with
relevant supporting documents on why the 87 infrastructure projects of the City
passed thru the eligibility and evaluation of bids and the post-qualification despite
incomplete submission of documents in the opening of bids as contained in the
first envelope, where non-compliance should have merited a rating of “failed”,
hence, ineligible to participate in the bidding for the projects as prescribed RA No.
9184 and its RIRR, as amended.

Likewise, require the BAC and TWG to adopt the updated set of Bidding
Documents under the 5th Edition of the Philippine Bidding Documents which has
been prepared by the Government of the Philippines (GoP) in accordance with the
provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184, as amended, and ensure strict
compliance of its provisions in the succeeding conduct of public bidding for
completeness, veracity and reliability of information/documents and bidding
results.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City’s GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) to ensure that the gender issues reported in the GPB and the GAD
Accomplishment Report are supported with sex-disaggregated data or relative
gender information. Said information is necessary to justify the issues cited as
gender related issues, where correct application of intervention thru various GAD
related activities is needed to address unequal opportunities for men and women.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Market Administrator to
refrain from making requisitions of accountable forms directly from the National
Printing Office as such function is delegated to the Office of the City Treasurer in
consonance with the provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that the City Mayor cause provision of a suitable and well-
ventilated room where medicines and medical supplies may be stored in order to
attain the required temperature and other conditions for storage, thereby
preserving the quality and potency of drugs and medicines for the best interest of
the general public.

We recommended that the City Mayor demand from the OIC-City Accountant and the GSO the strict and immediate actions for the settlement of the discrepancies noted by the Audit Team by undertaking the following remedial measures:

																																																																																							
																																								 (See table for full list of remedial measures)

The CAO has yet to ascertain the amount of unreconciled properties since most PPE accounts in the books of accounts have unreconciled amounts subject for reconciliation.
