observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, the City Treasurer and the City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) exert utmost effort to trace the details/whereabouts and locate any record/information/documents relative to the outstanding receivables of P75,914,694.90; and Analyze said receivables and make appropriate actions necessary, even filing criminal case against erring borrowers so that the collections of the large amount of City funds would be recovered to finance other programs and projects of the City.
Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to assign personnel specifically to handle receivable accounts of the City and to design and establish a manual or computerized system that would provide detailed processes for approval, recording and reporting of Receivables accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Due to limited number of personnel in the City Accounting Office, such tasks would be delegated to a new personnel since the former City Accountant, who was assigned to handle the receivable accounts has Loans retired in June 2018.
Receivables to be participated also by the City Mayor, City Treasurer and the CSWDO to ensure a reconciled and intact database of borrowers, loan amount, collection and updated loan balances.
observations.reason: See Observation No. 2 Implemented under Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant to adopt one accounting treatment for the borrowing costs to avoid or eliminate misstatements of the appropriate PPE accounts and disclose the same in the Notes to Financial Statements for the transparency of information.
observations.managementAction: One accounting treatment has been adopted for the borrowing costs immediately after the receipt of the Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) on borrowing cost.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and the OIC-City Accountant to make final assessment of the subject checks found stale after more than six months and make appropriate action to inform the respective payees of the cancellation of the checks given no encashment after more than six months. Likewise, require the OIC-City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjustment so that the amount corresponding to the stale checks would revert back to the balances of Cash in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts, thereby presenting an accurate account balances in the financial statements of the City at any given period.
Lastly, for the best interest of the City, we recommended that the City Mayor or his representative make proper coordination with the concerned depository bank so that the bank statement would be released immediately after the close of each month for the timely preparation of bank reconciliation statements in order that any variances of records would be immediately adjusted in the City’s books of accounts, paving for an accurate presentation of cash balances.
observations.managementAction:
All stale checks were adjusted accordingly in the books of accounts in January 2018 and every month thereafter. Also, released checks which became stale are also reverted back to cash and recorded in Cash Book since the City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) prepares reconciliation of cash balances with the City Accountant’s Office (CAO) on a monthly basis.
The OIC-City Treasurer coordinated with the officers of the Land Bank of the Philippines, Batangas City Branch for timely release of bank statements, resulting timely submission of bank reconciliation statements at year-end 2018.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), the Design and Build Committees for Calumpang Bridge No. 3, the OIC-City Accountant, and all other concerned City officials to submit justifications with the supporting documents on why the said procurement of four infrastructure projects was processed and recommended for award of contract to concerned contractors despite violation of RA No. 9184 and RA No. 7160 as discussed.
Likewise, direct the BAC and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to ensure strict compliance with the revised provision of Section 23.1 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184, whereby Tax Clearance is included in the technical legal requirement for the first envelope to be submitted by the bidders, pursuant to GPPB Resolution No. 21-2013 dated July 31, 2013 so as to guarantee that all prospective bidders in every government projects are compliant with all existing tax laws, rules and regulations. Strict compliance with the procedures and documentary requirements for common government transactions as prescribed in the afore-cited laws, rules and regulations should be adopted so as to ensure that all contracts entered into by the City is valid and legal.
Direct the City Mayor to comply strictly with the proper procedures in the preparation, submission and approval of PPMP and APP so as to ensure that procurement of programs or projects are in accordance with the provisions of Section 17 of RA No. 9184.
Furnish the Audit Team of all the required but lacking supporting documents and the necessary explanation for the deficiencies noted to substantiate the payments made. Strict compliance with the procedures and documentary requirements for common government transactions as prescribed in the afore-cited laws, rules and regulations should be adopted so as to ensure that all contracts entered into by the City is valid and legal.
Comply strictly with the procedures in the preparation, submission and approval of PPMP and APP. Comply with payments and deductions for unsatisfactory rating given, and process, approve and pay all claims only with complete documentation to protect the interest of the City.
observations.managementAction:
Not See Observation No. 6
implemented
observations.reason: See Observation No. 6 Implemented under Part II of this report.

observations.managementAction:
The City Legal Officer submitted that (1) Biddings for the 87 infrastructure projects were conducted pursuant to the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184; (2) all the documents more specifically the Platinum Membership of the contractors for the 87 infrastructure projects worth a total of Php636,851,678.61 were all submitted and complied with respective contractors pursuant to the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 which took effect on October 28, 2016. These documents were submitted to your office. It is submitted that since the BAC followed the RIRR 2016 to the letter, the integrity and the procurement process for the 87 projects had not been compromised.
All of the infrastructure projects had been completed and fully paid for. It is regrettable that it was only after we receive your AOM that we took notice of the changes to the rules. Had we known about the changes, we would have followed them.
observations.reason: Other required but lacking supporting documents are yet to be complied with such as proof that Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and Approved Contract were posted in PhilGEPS, agency website and conspicuous places due to difficulty of retrieving files as the one who filed said documents is on maternity leave.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instructed the OIC-City Treasurer and the City Legal Officer to exert all available legal remedies provided under Sections 256-258 of RA No. 7160 so as to reduce, if not totally eliminate, the rise of delinquent real property taxes, which is detrimental to the interest of the City, as such amount, if collected, would be an abundant source of funds to finance its priority programs. Likewise, we recommended that the OIC- City Treasurer and OIC-City Accountant be directed by the City Mayor to conduct the needed reconciliation of records in order to establish paralleled balances of RPT and SET receivables and effect the necessary adjustment(s) in the books of accounts for fair presentation of balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
The CTO submitted to the CAO all the supporting documents necessary for the reconciliation of their records. Adjustments are yet to be taken up by the Accounting Office.
The Management has paid attention to the legal remedies provided by the existing laws, rules and regulation on the delinquent taxpayers, and studying its applicability on the Collection of the uncollected properties on hand.
observations.reason: See Observation No. 7. Decision of the Local Board of Assessment Appeals (LBAA) pending on some cases; reconciliation efforts ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor, being also the Chairman of the City Development Council, ensure that all projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund be priority projects, and that these projects were proposed for implementation, to ensure that the full intention of development would be attained by the intended beneficiaries of the said projects. Similarly, four out of the 16 projects funded under the Continuing Appropriations from 2017 operation with costs of P91,220,225.68 were not undertaken during the year 2018. In addition, proposal for financial expenses, if there is any, should be given appropriation outside of the 20 percent DF, as such nature of expenses are not covered by the allowable funded out of the Fund. As to the aforecited financial expenditures charged out of the 20 percent DF for CY 2017, consider inclusion of such expenditures in the succeeding annual budget of the City.
Likewise, cause the immediate appropriation of the unexpended balances of completed projects of prior years totaling P3,692,423.00 and whatever amount that may be left from outstanding balances of ongoing and completed projects of CY 2017 into other developmental projects that will contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes, and partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures in order that attainment of the real intent and purpose of the 20 percent Development Fund may be realized to the fullest.
observations.managementAction: The recommendations were considered by the City Government, particularly the implementation of all priority projects. In addition, funds for pending projects were classified to Continuing Appropriation and some unexpended balances were utilized to fund priority projects identified in the Supplemental Budget No. 1 for CY 2018 which were listed in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of 2018.
observations.reason: See discussion on 20 Percent Development Fund under Part II of this report. Four of the 69 projects funded under the current years appropriation of 20 percent DF of P275,483,705.89 are pending implementation; four out of the 16 projects funded under the Continuing Appropriations from 2017 were also not undertaken during the year 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require all the concerned City officials to adhere strictly to the guidelines set under JMC No. 2013-1 for the utilization of LDRRMF, and to stop the practice of charging to LDRRMF the expenses that are not related to and not supportive of disaster risk management activities. Administrative expenses should be charged to the respective appropriation of the Offices concerned considering that the LDRRMF is only for specific purpose and not related to the usual operation of the City.
observations.managementAction: The City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (CDRRMO) claimed that they will adhere to the guidelines of JMC No. 2013-1 for the utilization of DRRM Fund. The office likewise responded that there is intention to improve their office and stockroom and requested for repair which is included in the Annual Investment Plan.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the City Mayor direct the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDO) and the Accounting office to:
(a) Conduct a gender analysis to have appropriate basis of its gender issues.
(b) Set a well-defined performance indicators or targets for concrete evaluation and monitoring of the GPB, Budget (GPB) result;
(c) Account for the detailed information of GAD (d) Prepare the GPB and GAD AR in accordance with the guidelines; and (e) Provide justification on the improbable non-compliance. (See table for full recommended actions)
observations.managementAction:
The City Planning and Development Coordinator (being the designated City GAD Focal Point System) will coordinate with all the concerned offices with GAD programs and projects and remind them that all gender issues in the GAD Plan and Budget that will be formulated next year and the corresponding GAD Accomplishment Report be supported with sex disaggregated data and other related gender information. A memorandum will be issued by the City Mayor to that effect to ensure compliance to the recommendation of the COA.
Recommendations (a) to (e) were duly noted and considered by the City GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and its TWG. Explanations together with the copy of Purchase Orders supporting the acquisition of goods were submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: See Observation No. 7 implemented under Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor demand from the OIC-City
Accountant and the GSO the strict and immediate actions for the settlement of
the discrepancies noted by the Audit Team by undertaking the following
remedial measures:
 Direct the GSO to provide clear description, including the property tag
numbers of the disposed and condemned PPE accounts so that the OIC-
City Accountant can expedite the dropping from the books of accounts all
properties already disposed of;
 Require the City Engineer’s Office to forward the supporting documents
for the demolished properties to the Accounting Office to effect the
necessary entries in the books;
 Require the OIC-City Accountant and the GSO to produce the
corresponding documents relative to the properties found in existence but
not recorded in the City’s books of accounts. If found in order and
ownership thereof belong to the City, then appropriate action should be
undertaken for eventual recording in the books to properly safeguard the
subject properties; and
 Instruct the OIC-City Accountant to fully establish the amount of
unreconciled properties where there were properties in the books of
accounts, but could not be located during the physical count. If warranted,
seek the assistance of proper offices so that the properties that can no
longer be located or identified could be dropped from the books of
accounts, after the appropriate legal proceedings so as to rid the books of
unidentified properties, and for fair presentation of PPE accounts in the
financial statements of the City of Batangas.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant, City
Treasurer and the CSWDO to exert utmost effort to trace the
details/whereabouts and locate any record/information/documents relative to the
unsubstantiated and long outstanding receivables of P75,914,694.90; analyze
said receivables and make appropriate actions necessary, even filing criminal
case against erring borrowers so that collections of the large amount of City
funds would be recovered to finance other programs and projects of the City.
Likewise, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City
Accountant to assign personnel specifically to handle receivable accounts of the
City and to design and establish a manual or computerized system that would
provide detailed processes for approval, recording and reporting Loans
Receivables to be participated also by the City Mayor, City Treasurer and the
CSWDO to ensure a reconciled and intact database of borrowers, loan amount,
collection and updated loan balances.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-City Accountant to
adopt one accounting treatment for the borrowing costs to avoid or eliminate
misstatement of appropriate PPE accounts and disclose the same in the Notes to
Financial Statements for transparency of information.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and
the OIC-City Accountant to make final assessment of the subject checks found
no encashment after more than six months and make appropriate action to
inform the respective payees of the cancellation of the checks issued to them.
Likewise, require the OIC-City Accountant to prepare the necessary
adjustments so that the amount corresponding to the stale checks would revert
back to the balances of Cash in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts, thereby
presenting an accurate account balances in the financial statements of the City at
any given period.
Lastly, for the best interest of the City, we recommended that the City
Mayor or his representative make proper coordination with the concerned
depository bank so that the bank statements would be released immediately
after the close of each month for the timely preparation of bank statements in
order that any variances of records would be immediately adjusted in the City’s
books of accounts, paving for an accurate presentation of cash balances.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the members of the Bids
and Awards Committee (BAC), the Design and Build Committee for
Calumpang Bridge No. 3, the OIC-City Accountants, and all other concerned
City officials to submit justifications with supporting documents on why the said
procurement of four infrastructure projects was processed and recommended
for award of contract to concerned contractors despite violation of RA No. 9184,
and RA 7160 as discussed above.
Likewise, direct the BAC and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to
ensure strict compliance with the revised provision of Section 23.1 of the RIRR
of RA No. 9184, whereby Tax Clearance is included in the technical legal
requirement for the first envelope to be submitted by the bidders, pursuant to
GPPB Resolution No. 21-2013 dated July 31, 2013 so as to guarantee that all
prospective bidders in every government projects are compliant with all the
existing tax laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned City
officials to:
f) submit the detailed breakdown of the ABC showing in sufficient detail its
derivation; the measurement in kilometers of the total distance covered by
one complete route for all the required routes to be traveled; and estimated
volume in cubic meters of garbage to be hauled from area of operation,
including the basis for such estimates used in the breakdown of the contract
amount so as to determine the reasonableness of the contract price of
P57,716,362.82;
g) furnish the Audit Team of all the required but lacking supporting documents
and the necessary explanation for the deficiencies noted to substantiate the
payments made. Strict compliance with the procedures and documentary
requirements for common government transactions as prescribed in the afore-
cited laws, rules and regulations should be adopted so as to ensure that all
contracts entered into by the City is valid and legal;
h) comply strictly with the proper procedures in the preparation, submission
and approval of PPMP and APP so as to ensure that procurement of
programs or projects are in accordance with Section 7 of RA No. 9184;
i) submit explanation/justification on why penalties imposed against the
Metrowaste for the unsatisfactory rating given by CENRO for the
maintenance of sanitary landfill for July and October 2017 were not
deducted from the payment to the said service provider. Otherwise,
collection of the amount of P269,082.93 from Metrowaste should be
undertaken; and
e) process, approve and pay claims only with complete documentation to protect
the interest of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City Treasurer
and the City Legal Officer to exert all available legal remedies provided under
Section 256-258 of RA No. 7160 so as to reduce, if not totally eliminate, the rise
of delinquent real property taxes, which is detrimental to the interest of the City,
as such amount, if collected, would be an abundant source of funds to finance its
priority programs and projects.
Likewise, we recommended that the OIC-City Treasurer and the OIC-
City Accountant be directed by the City Mayor to conduct the needed
reconciliation of records in order to establish paralleled balances of RPT and
SET receivables and effect the necessary adjustment(s) in the books of accounts
for fair presentation of balances in the financial statements.
The attention of Management is hereby invited to the Implementing
Rules and Regulations (IRR) of the Local Government Code of 1991, under Rule
XXX – Local Government Taxation, Part X – Civil Remedies of Collection of
Revenues particularly on Sections 260 to 268 for the subsequent actions after the
service of warrant or levy on delinquent real property.

We recommended that the City Mayor, being also the Chairman of the
City Development Council, ensure that all projects funded out of the 20%
Development Fund be implemented during the year that these projects were
proposed for implementation, to ensure that the full intention of development
would be attained by the intended beneficiaries of the said projects.
In addition, proposal for financial expenses, if there is any, should be
given appropriation outside the 20% DF, as such nature of expenses are not
covered by the allowable programs/projects to be funded out of the Fund. As to
the aforecited financial expenditures charged out of the 20% DF for CY 2017,
consider inclusion such expenditures in the succeeding annual budget of the
City.
Likewise, cause the immediate appropriation of the unexpended balances
of completed projects of prior years totaling P3,692,423.00 and whatever amount
that may be left from the outstanding balances of ongoing and completed
projects of CY 2017 into other developmental projects that will contribute to the
attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental
management outcomes, and partake the nature of investment or capital
expenditures in order that attainment of the real intent and purpose of the 20%
Development Fund may be realized to the fullest.

We recommended that the City Mayor require all the concerned City
officials to adhere strictly to the guidelines set under JMC No. 2013-1 for the
utilization of LDRRMF, and to stop the practice of charging to LDRRMF the
expenses that are not related to and not supportive of disaster risk management
activities. Administrative expenses should be charged to the respective
appropriation of the Offices concerned considering that the LDRRMF was only
for specific purpose not related to the usual operation of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and
Development Coordinator and the Accounting Office to provide reconciliation of
the P5,074,976.45 variance.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and
Development Coordinator to:
f) conduct a gender analysis to have appropriate bases of its gender
issues;
g) set a well-defined performance indicators or targets for concrete
evaluation and monitoring of the GPB;
h) account for the detailed information of GAD’s actual results;
i) prepare the GPB and GAD AR in accordance with the guidelines; and
j) provide justification on the noncompliance of the prescribed
regulations for the procurement of goods, particularly on the lacking
purchase order.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the members of the BAC,
and all other concerned City officials to submit justifications on why the contract
agreements for the 13 barangay roads projects have been undertaken despite the
fact that the SB No. 1, s 2016, had not yet been approved, thus no funds actually
available for the said project as required under Section 321 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services
Officer and the OIC-City Accountant to implement the following
recommendations to support the assertion made, ensure the reliability of the
PPE accounts balances, and to fairly present PPE account in the financial
statements:

a) Observe strictly the rules and regulations in the acquisition of
real properties, and ensure that all lands are titled and
registered in the name of the City:

b) Conduct a complete physical inventory of all the City's
properties;

c) Incorporate major repair/ rehabilitation/ improvement/
renovations — permanent or capital in nature made to a
particular infrastructure or equipment in the respective PC,
instead of separate item in the RPCPPE; and

d) Maintain updated Property/Stock Cards and SL as control
records and regularly undertake reconciliation of the GSO and
Accounting Unit records.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OIC-City
Accountant to trace the existence of the bank account numbers of the three
unknown bank accounts with outstanding balances, as well as the account
not confirmed by the LBP. Also, obtain confirmation from the concerned
depository banks to establish validity. If the confirmation result is negative,
make the appropriate adjustment to present a correct and reliable Cash in
Bank account balance.

Further, direct the OIC-City Accountant to locate the necessary
documents regarding the counterpart fund of the unrecorded fund transfer,
which remained outstanding for almost ten years, and draw the appropriate
adjusting journal entry in the books of the City for the fair presentation of
the Cash in Bank account balance.

In view of the above, we recommended that the City Mayor:

direct the City Market Administrator to impose the sanctions
on stallholders with more than two months unpaid rentals as
provided under Section 110 of the Batangas City Market Code
and as stipulated in the Contract of Lease:

transfer the collection activities of the market operation under
the direct supervision and control of the Office of the City
Treasurer to prevent defrauding the government through
falsification of records,

bill and collect stall rental payments from stallholders found
with posted Official Receipt (OR) number in their individual
SL, but not covered with corresponding payments; and

submit explanation/justification on the erroneous recording of
collection in the individual SL of stallholders.

a. instruct the BAC, the responsible City ENRO, and GSD
personnel to:

1. check the actual volume of garbage hauled and disposed each
trip;

2. check the actual vehicles used during the daily trips versus the
listed vehicles provided in the contract;

3 validate the accuracy and reasonableness of the monthly
billings and payments made to the Contractor; and

4. verify the availability of transport facilities committed/pledged
in the contract.

b. require from the City ENRO and GSD Officers a written explanation
as to why the use of the City’s transport facilities and manpower were
resorted to instead of directing the service provider to make available
its resources to meet unexpected demand of its client; and

¢. direct the service provider to submit explanation on why it shall not
be held liable for its failure to utilize the transport vehicles as agreed
and stipulated in the contract.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. require from the City ENRO a written explanation on the
reason for the discrepancies observed between the submitted
service invoices of the Contractor and the monitoring reports
prepared by the City ENRO;

b. instruct the City ENRO to incorporate in the contract the
defined list of routes/service areas and corresponding schedule
of collection and mopping operation;

[ direct the Contractor and the City ENRO to incorporate in
their service invoices and monitoring reports, respectively, the
volume of hauled and disposed garbage in order to determine
the accuracy and reasonableness of the claims paid and gauge
the efficiency of the services rendered;

d. require the City ENRO Officer and OIC-City Accountant to
validate the correctness of all documents supporting claims for
payment and compliance to the provisions of contract prior to
payment of such claims; and

e comply with the deficiencies noted in the previous year’s audit
report and those reiterated this year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:

a. City Social Welfare and Development Officer to:

1. submit the lacking relevant documentary requirements relative
to the Supplemental Feeding Program to prevent issuance of
NS/ND; and

2. submit justification/explanation on:

o the discrepancies on the total number of beneficiaries in the
approved program proposal, masterlist of beneficiaries and
nutritional status reports;

o the deficiencies on the purchase, inspection and acceptance
and distribution of eggplants and carrots; and

o why the procurement process prescribed under RA No.
9184 was not strictly followed.

b. OIC-City Accountant to ensure completeness of the necessary
documents supporting the liquidation reports for the SFP.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the responsible CPDO
and other Department Heads concern to:

a prepare comprehensively the ATP and incorporate only those PPAs
which are feasible and capable of being implemented. Strictly adhere
to the provisions under Annex A of RIRR of RA No. 9184 on the
conduct of feasibility study and detailed engineering prior to the
implementation of any project:

b. evaluate the necessity of programs and projects funded under prior
years’ budget, otherwise, revert the fund and make use of such to
other PPAs which are more needed as determined and recommended
by the LCE and approved by the Sanguniang Panlungsod;

c. ensure that the timeline set for each PPA is realistic and followed
accordingly; and
d. fast track the implementation of unimplemented and under
implemented projects and programs to the attainment of desirable
socio-economic development and environmental management
outcomes.

We recommended that the City Mayor make representation to the
Sangguniang Panlungsod for the installation of IAS directly under the Office
of the City Mayor, in compliance with AO Nos. 278, series of 1992 and 70.
series of 2003 in order to evaluate the adequacy of the existing operational
system of the City and strengthen the same as needed.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee coordinate with the City Accountant and perform reconciliation of the PPE accounts in order to establish the precise causes of discrepancies, whether items had already been missing, donated, sold or unserviceable in order to arrive at the correct balance of PPE accounts representing existing serviceable and unserviceable properties.
observations.managementAction: Property,  Plant  and Equipment amounting to Partially This was reiterated per  physical inventory conducted,  while the amount of P67.442,590.33 has been subjected to Public Auction from CYs 2011 to 2016 and was yet to be dropped from the books of accounts.
observations.reason: This was reiterated under Part I, Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the ICO Treasurer to

a.) terminate immediately the investment in special savings/time deposits and return the amount to appropriate current account. Consider the possibility of using the said idle funds for the implementation of the much needed developmental programs and projects in the approved Annual Investment Plan (AIP) so as not to delay projects instead of favouring interest earned in time deposit; and henceforth

b.) secure prior authority from Sangguniang Panlungsod and approval of the Chief Executive in case there are idle funds in the General Fund for investment in time deposit
observations.managementAction: Termination of investments in time deposits was made immediately.
observations.reason:
Fully
Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Assistant City Accountant- OIC to:
a) coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile the discrepancies of the Due from and Due to LGUs accounts balances; and
b) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct account balance of the Inter-Agency payables at year-end
observations.managementAction: The Assistant Accountant- OIC informed the Audit Team that they have coordinated with the Provincial Accountant regarding the discrepancies of the Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs accounts’ balances and agreed to settle the issues,

Justification letter from City ENRO was already submitted to COA.
The monitoring personnel consider the possibility of hiring additional personnel to perform the said monitoring duties.
Request for printing of serially numbered trip tickets was already made. Serially numbered tickets has been printed and delivered and will be used effective CY 2017.
observations.reason: There is an insufficient number of monitoring personnel, hence, consider the possibility of hiring additional personnel to perform the said monitoring duties. This was reiterated in Part I, Observation No. 7. There is a delay due to the unavailability of printed forms, since it has to undergo the usual procurement procedures.

observations.managementAction:
Formulation and review of guidelines and criteria are still in process.
Program of activities of each department with job order employees had been required.
Job description of job order employees has been prepared and duly signed before signing the contract.
The ID system is still in process. New layout and design is for approval. Specific job functions were not yet implemented in the contract
Accomplishment reports were attached to the payroll and duly approved before processing individual payments.
observations.reason: Not completed the required documents for submission including payments. The ID system is still in process.

observations.managementAction: The CPDO, which is preparing the Annual Investment Plan (AIP), started the adoption of detailed AIP for CY 2017. This was reiterated in Part II, Observation No. 10. The CPDO is exerting best efforts to adopt the recommendations and realize common objectives.
observations.reason: Non-availability of cash along with the restriction on period for contract awarding during last quarter of the year resulted in under utilization of the 20% Development Fund. Nevertheless, Management, thru the CPDO, assured that they are exerting best efforts to adopt the recommendations and realize common objectives.

observations.managementAction: Management, thru its GAD Focal Point System and CPDO, which serve as the TWG secretariat, has already adopted the standard format and procedures in coming up with its annual GAD Plan and Budget and Annual Accomplishment Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) City Treasurer's Office to require the business taxpayers to submit sworn statement of his gross sales/receipts for the preceding calendar year and other pertinent records/information for the determination of gross receipts;
b) City Accountant to submit to the City Treasurer a List of Payments made to Contractors/Suppliers who transacted with the City Government to serve as one of the basis in the assessment of business taxes in the current year; and
c) City Treasurer verify/assess the gross receipts of the seven sampled Contractors/Suppliers and validate the reported under collection totaling P£780,388.08, and enforce the collection of underpayment including penalties and surcharges, if applicable, pursuant to the rates provided in the City’s Revenue Code. Absence of documented action or information to counter the under collection shall mean the issuance of the corresponding Notice of Charge.
observations.managementAction: Out of the seven Contractors, only one has not completed the required documents for submission including payments. The said Contractor, Jasmig Construction for the retirement of Kumintang Ibaba business address was already received the Business Tax Order of Payment dated October 17, 2016.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a) the CSWD Head to: review the MOA/Trust Agreement prior to the implementation of the SFP and comply with the guiding principles provided for its implementation; immediately implement the CY 2015 SFP for the remaining balance to address and attain the set objectives of the program (iho unreasonable delay); ensure proper implementation of the program and compliance in the submission of other documentary requirements.
b) indicate the items’ complete specifications, include proper delivery term and appropriate delivery date in the preparation of Purchase Order (PO). Make sure that items listed in the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) match with their corresponding Purchase Requests (PR) and POs;
c) prepare and submit monthly Physical and Financial Report and Quarterly Report on Nutritional Status of Children Beneficiaries;
d) submit justification for the discrepancy between the total number of beneficiaries fed as listed in the master list against the proposed target. Also, explain why delivery was made on the latter part of December 2015, wherein Christmas vacation of children already started.
e) the City Accountant to: ensure that documentary requirements for the liquidation of the fund for the SFP are complete, and submitted on time to the Audit Team within the prescribed period to effectively monitor and evaluate the programs’ outcomes; and
f) regularly submit the required liquidation reports, be it partial or full, for the implementation of 2015 SFP.
observations.managementAction: The CSWD Head explained that delay in the implementation of the program has caused slowdown in the processing of liquidation of cash advance which is a prerequisite prior to the grant of another cash advance. For other concerns mentioned, the concerned personnel advised the Audit Team that they will look into these matters and submitted the lacking documents/reports. Photos of day care center children during the feeding program were submitted as well as the nutritional status of beneficiaries.
observations.reason: Purchase Order (PO) is not prepared in the case of purchases for the SFP since the General Services Office (GSO) advised the CSWD to prepare Purchase Request (PR) only. This was reiterated in Part II, Observation No. 9.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) require the City Officials concerned to strictly comply with Section 21 of RA No.10121 and Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; b) require the City DRRMO to make the most of the LDRRMF to prepare for the observance activities, and the City Budget Officer to properly identify the correct charging of accounts; and c) require the City DRRMO to regularly submit to COA Auditor the Report on the Sources and Utilization of Funds prepared by the Accountant, for proper and timely evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer commented that meals and snacks incurred on account of prepositioning the responders prior to a major event. Also, he explained that other expenses incurred for various supplies and materials were used mainly for disaster risk management operations and trainings conducted. Meals and snacks, on the other hand, were served for the meetings carried out by the City Council with CDRRMO, Batangas DRRMC and Barangay DRRMC for the review, assessment and updating/revising of Contingency and Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plans. The DRRMO will regularly submit to COA Auditor the Report on the Sources and Utilization of Funds prepared by the Accountant, for proper and timely evaluation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Assistant City Accountant-OIC make proper accounting reconciliation of the RPT/SET Receivables with tax delinquencies at the end of the year to present the reliable balances of these accounts in the Financial Statements. We also required that the City Treasurer promptly inform the Accounting Department of any adjustments to be made on the tax due and collectible from taxpayers for timely recording/adjustments in the books. Furthermore, we reiterated that the Assistant City Accountant-OIC maintain subsidiary ledger for each taxpayer/barangay which should be reconciled with the general ledger balances and the tax delinquencies submitted by the City Treasurer to ensure the reliability of the balances of Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer's Office (CTO) suggested that the City Assessor’s Office be linked directly to the Accounting Office to make proper accounting reconciliation of Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax Receivables.
observations.reason: There is no computerization linkage between the two Offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Assistant City Accountant-OIC verify and analyze the long outstanding payables and revert to the unappropriated surplus the outstanding accounts payable aged over two years to reflect the correct Accounts Payable and Government Equity accounts of the City as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has drawn the following Journal Entry Voucher to revert the long outstanding Accounts Payable: a 2015-03-006346 for P476.82 in the General Fund-Market; b. 2015-03-006347 for P… in the General Fund - Colegio ng Lungsod ng Batangas; c 2015-03-006348 for P3,062.50 in the General Fund. The long outstanding accounts payable of P10,000.00 under the Trust Fund needs further verification and has not been reverted yet.
observations.reason: The long outstanding accounts payable of P10,000.00 under the Trust Fund are subsidiary ledger items and has not been reverted yet.

observations.recommendation: Enforce strict compliance with the terms and conditions on the grant of the loan and close monitoring of the programs/projects, including collections to ensure realization of its objectives and high collection efficiency. We reiterated that the Accounting Division exert its best effort to collect the long outstanding receivables and that the Legal Office enforce sanctions on the delinquent borrowers, and file appropriate charges against those local officials responsible for the grant to ineligible borrowers.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that collecting efforts are being exerted. The City Accountant was designated by the City Mayor to assist the City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) in enforcing collection. The CSWDO informed the Audit Team that they have been receiving payment in view of the demand letters sent. The CSWDO has also requested for write-off of the balances after confirmation of death of deceased beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod review City Ordinance No. 11, Series of 2011 and make the necessary revision/repeal to realize more income for the City Government through recovery of delinquent taxes. We also recommended that Management cause the transfer of title of the forfeited properties, which are non-redeemable as of October 31, 2014, by registration of Declaration of Forfeiture with the Register of Deeds in order to fully vest the ownership to the City Government as provided by law in order to present the reliable balances of the Real Property Tax (RPT) receivables and the Land assets in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Management concurred with the recommendation and promised to evaluate and study the matter. With the implementation of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS), auctioned properties were recognized as Inventory for sale. Hence, they need to transfer the title of ownership to the Batangas City Government.
observations.reason: Copy of the City Ordinance. The Sangguniang Panlungsod Secretary explained the basis of extension of redemption period and promised to evaluate and study the matter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Government submit to the Auditor the copy of the Authority to Purchase from the DILG and justification for the purchase of such vehicles which are not included in the approved APP/AIP.
observations.managementAction: Specific measures were undertaken. The General Services Office promised to strictly comply with the provisions of Administrative Order No. 15 regarding the procurement of motor vehicles. Notices of disallowances were issued which are currently under appeal.
observations.reason: Copy of the Authority to Purchase from DILG is not possible to secure because the procurement transactions have already been made — notices of disallowance were issued which are under appeal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Officer ensure that only those that are emergency in nature and those ordinary or regular supplies and materials not available in the Procurement Services will be purchased through shopping.
observations.managementAction: Except for those that are emergency in nature and purchases from Procurement Service of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), the City’s mode of procurement was made thru public bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly comply with the conditions of RA No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations in resorting to alternative modes of procurement. In the absence of any exceptional condition that would warrant resorting to alternative method of procurement, the required mode of procurement is still public bidding.
observations.managementAction: Management was enlightened regarding the proper mode of post-disaster operation/unforeseen emergency cases and promised to comply with the Audit Team's recommendation.
observations.reason: Procurement of food commodities during immediate contingency required immediate purchase was made thru shopping.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned City officials refund the Transportation Allowance claimed. Ensure that no official who has been assigned or uses government motor vehicle be granted TA. Likewise, adhere strictly to the rules of the Department of Management on the grant of Representation and Transportation Allowances.
observations.managementAction: Effective CY 2016, all government employees/officials receiving Transportation Allowance (TA) are required to attach certificate that no RP vehicle is issued under their name. Issued ND No. 16-024-100(14) dated May 11, 2016 to one of the concerned City officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Officer ensure the timely submission of Purchase Orders and contracts to the Auditor concerned for the review and evaluation. All Purchase Orders and contracts submitted to the Auditor should be properly supported with the required documents to establish their validity and propriety. Likewise, submit Notice of Acceptance within 24 hours after delivery to enable prompt inspection, as well as pointing out of defects, should there be any.
observations.managementAction: Perfected Purchase Orders and Contracts are submitted to the Auditor’s Office within five days. Deliveries with Certificate of Acceptance within 24 hours after delivery to enable prompt inspection, as well as pointing out of defects, should there be any.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Department include in the conduct of physical count at year-end the inventory of unserviceable properties or those that are no longer used and returned by the end-users, and report them in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) for the City Accounting Office to facilitate reclassification to Other Assets account. We also recommended that the City Accounting Office comply with Section 4(p) of the MNGAS by reclassifying properties to Other Assets account as they become unserviceable based from the IRRUPs submitted by GSD and not upon disposal so that only those serviceable and in use by the City Government would be depreciated and properly included in the PPE account balance.
observations.managementAction:
Implementation: Perfected Purchase Orders and Contracts are submitted to the Auditor’s Office within five days. Deliveries with Certificate of Acceptance within 24 hours after delivery to enable prompt inspection, as well as pointing out of defects, should there be any.
(This cell in the source appears to have been completed and indicates compliance.)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer adopt measures and strategies on how to improve collection efficiency. Coordinated and extra efforts in the implementation of Real Property Tax Program should be applied in the City and Barangay levels to maximize collection of taxes. If in case all the measures were undertaken but still the collection efficiency is very low, the remedies and sanctions as provided in RA No. 7160 can be availed of promptly to preclude delayed and non-payment of taxes.
observations.managementAction: It was found out that some properties with delinquent Real Property Taxes totaling P419,249,045.56 were subject to auction in December 2015, while other properties were forfeited in favor of the City Government. However, ownership of such is not yet transferred, hence, they are still reflected as delinquents. The City Treasurer's Office coordinated and wrote to the City Assessor regarding some properties that were included in the list of delinquencies but such properties are only on record and do not actually exist.
observations.reason: It was found out that some properties are only on record, but not actually existing upon distribution of notices of delinquencies in different barangays, making Real Property Tax delinquency continuously increasing.
